An Explanation stipulates that an officer not below the rank of Superintendent of Police shall decide whether to register the case under this section or the Unlawful Activities (Prevention) Act, 1967.
BNS_S115:1d0e5fc2011095ae1b73d0ef4cd93f4: Section 115(1) defines "voluntarily to cause hurt" as performing any act with the intention of thereby causing hurt to any person, or with the knowledge that he is likely thereby to cause hurt to any person, and thereby causing hurt to any person. Under sub-section (2), except in the case provided for by sub-section (1) of section 122, whoever voluntarily causes hurt shall be punished with imprisonment of either description for a term which may extend to one year, or with fine which may extend to ten thousand rupees, or with both.
BNS_S334:a531669b2d7eaf673a8cf46b1556ed7: Section 334(1) mandates that whoever dishonestly or with intent to commit mischief breaks open or unfastens any closed receptacle which contains or which he believes to contain property shall be punished with imprisonment of either description for a term which may extend to two years, or with fine, or with both. Section 334(2) provides that whoever, being entrusted with any closed receptacle which contains or which he believes to contain property, without having authority to open the same, dishonestly or with intent to commit mischief breaks open or unfastens that receptacle, shall be punished with imprisonment of either description for a term which may extend to three years, or with fine, or with both.
BNS_S336:88dd3a43afe101da4fc0c8775f6e0fb: Section 336 defines forgery as making any false document, false electronic record, or part thereof, with intent to cause damage or injury to the public or any person, support any claim or title, cause any person to part with property, enter into any express or implied contract, or commit fraud. General forgery is punishable with imprisonment of either description for a term which may extend to two years, or with fine, or with both. Forgery intending that the document or electronic record forged shall be used for the purpose of cheating is punishable with imprisonment of either description for a term which may extend to seven years, and shall also be liable to fine. Forgery intending to harm the reputation of any party, or knowing it is likely to be used for that purpose, is punishable with imprisonment of either description for a term which may extend to three years, and shall also be liable to fine.
BNS_S117:ea67b26460eafdde00d9be496ad8428: Section 117 defines "voluntarily to cause grievous hurt" as causing grievous hurt with the intent or knowledge that such hurt is likely to be caused, with an Explanation clarifying that intending or knowing oneself likely to cause grievous hurt of one kind but actually causing another kind still constitutes the offence. Except for cases provided for by sub-section (2) of section 122, the offence is punishable by imprisonment of either description for a term which may extend to seven years, and shall also be liable to fine. If the commission causes permanent disability or a persistent vegetative state, the punishment is rigorous imprisonment for a term which shall not be less than ten years but which may extend to imprisonment for life, which shall mean imprisonment for the remainder of that person’s natural life. Additionally, if five or more persons acting in concert cause grievous hurt on the ground of race, caste or community, sex, place of birth, language, personal belief or similar grounds, each member shall be punished with imprisonment of either description for a term which may extend to seven years, and shall also be liable to fine.
BNS_S340:a193f5679bd8c893fcef82e8f5eceea: Section 340 defines a "forged document or electronic record" as a false document or electronic record made wholly or in part by forgery. It stipulates that whoever fraudulently or dishonestly uses as genuine any document or electronic record which he knows or has reason to believe to be a forged document or electronic record shall be punished in the same manner as if he had forged such document or electronic record.
BNS_S344:3bb2b709918ca6232cab2b1dae1e711: Section 344 penalizes any clerk, officer, servant, or person acting in such capacity who, wilfully and with intent to defraud, destroys, alters, mutilates, or falsifies any book, electronic record, paper, writing, valuable security, or account belonging to, possessed by, or received for their employer. It further prohibits wilfully and with intent to defraud making or abetting the making of any false entry in, or omitting, altering, or abetting the omission or alteration of any material particular from or in such records. The offence is punishable with imprisonment of either description for a term which may extend to seven years, or with fine, or with both. An Explanation clarifies that alleging a general intent to defraud is sufficient without naming a particular person, specifying a particular sum of money, or identifying the particular day the offence was committed.
BNS_S346:32c75ca245a3ad0d58738f92a5f4421: Section 346 criminalizes the act of whoever removes, destroys, defaces or adds to any property mark, intending or knowing it to be likely that he may thereby cause injury to any person. The offence is punishable with imprisonment of either description for a term which may extend to one year, or with fine, or with both.
BNS_S125:5914971bda36079506877d9a52313cb: Section 125 penalizes whoever does any act so rashly or negligently as to endanger human life or the personal safety of others with imprisonment of either description for a term which may extend to three months or with fine which may extend to two thousand five hundred rupees, or with both. Under clause (a), where hurt is caused, the punishment is imprisonment of either description for a term which may extend to six months, or with fine which may extend to five thousand rupees, or with both. Under clause (b), where grievous hurt is caused, the punishment is imprisonment of either description for a term which may extend to three years, or with fine which may extend to ten thousand rupees, or with both.
BNS_S350:fb8d565e182078cd9d9d0e08f04c8ae: Section 350(1) stipulates that whoever makes any false mark upon any case, package or other receptacle containing goods to cause any public servant or any other person to believe the receptacle contains goods it does not, does not contain goods it does, or that goods are of a nature or quality different from the real nature or quality, shall be punished with imprisonment of either description for a term which may extend to three years, or with fine, or with both, unless they prove they acted without intent to defraud. Under Section 350(2), whoever makes use of any false mark in any manner prohibited under sub-section (1) shall, unless they prove they acted without intent to defraud, be punished as if they had committed the offence under sub-section (1).
BNS_S354:45dc0fb6285fa28df201cae99fd2279: Section 354 penalizes whoever voluntarily causes or attempts to cause a person to do anything they are not legally bound to do, or omit anything they are legally entitled to do, by inducing or attempting to induce belief that the person or someone in whom they are interested will become or be rendered by the offender's act an object of Divine displeasure. The offence is punishable with imprisonment of either description for a term which may extend to one year, or with fine, or with both. The section includes Illustrations detailing specific scenarios, such as sitting dharna or threatening to kill one's own children to render another an object of Divine displeasure.
BNS_S356:75c45d8bc4f5c72e0bddcafa36d4ec4: Section 356 defines defamation as making or publishing any imputation via words spoken or intended to be read, signs, or visible representations, intending to harm or knowing/believing it will harm the reputation of a person. Four Explanations clarify that this extends to deceased persons, companies/associations, ironic/alternative imputations, and specify that harm involves lowering moral/intellectual character, caste, calling, credit, or attributing a loathsome/disgraceful bodily state. The section details ten Exceptions covering truth for public good, good faith opinions on public servants, public questions, decided Court proceedings (with an Explanation defining "Court"), public performances (with an Explanation on submission to judgment), lawful authority censure, accusations to lawful authorities, protection of interests, and good faith cautions. Punishments include simple imprisonment for a term which may extend to two years, or with fine, or with both, or with community service for defaming another, and simple imprisonment for a term which may extend to two years, or with fine, or with both for printing, engraving, selling, or offering for sale defamatory matter.
BNS_S358:8b0710293831e1a7dc14e2ce1b0b340: Section 358(1) repeals the Indian Penal Code (45 of 1860), while sub-section (2) stipulates that such repeal shall not affect the previous operation of the repealed Code, anything duly done or suffered thereunder, any right, privilege, obligation or liability acquired, accrued or incurred, or any penalty, punishment, investigation, remedy, or proceeding in respect of offences committed against the repealed Code. Under sub-section (2)(e), such proceedings or remedies may be instituted, continued or enforced, and penalties imposed as if the Indian Penal Code had not been repealed. Sub-section (3) deems any action taken under the repealed Code to have been taken under the corresponding provisions of this Sanhita, and sub-section (4) clarifies that sub-section (2) does not prejudice the general application of section 6 of the General Clauses Act, 1897 (10 of 1897).
BNS_S129:23a39b8d9634de4b83f2f83d84402e6: Section 129 defines criminal force as the intentional use of force to any person, without that person’s consent, in order to the committing of any offence, or intending by the use of such force to cause, or knowing it to be likely that by the use of such force he will cause, injury, fear or annoyance to the person. The section provides illustrations of mechanisms such as causing motion, change of motion, cessation of motion, bodily power, bodily contact, or the use of substances, objects, or animals to achieve these effects.
BNS_S135:92d9837ac5ca595850ee55d468f60d9: Section 135 of the Bharatiya Nyaya Sanhita, 2023, mandates punishment for whoever assaults or uses criminal force to any person, in attempting wrongfully to confine that person. The prescribed penalty is imprisonment of either description for a term which may extend to one year, or with fine which may extend to five thousand rupees, or with both.
BNSS_S2:3be5daf0de5e1f388f5c74fb9f3f4933: Section 2 defines "audio-video electronic," "electronic communication" (as specified by the Central Government), "judicial proceeding," and "notification," alongside "inquiry" (conducted by a Magistrate or Court, other than a trial) and "investigation" (evidence collection by a police officer or person authorized by a Magistrate), including an Explanation that provisions of a special Act prevail over this Sanhita if inconsistent. It defines "offence" (including section 20 of the Cattle Trespass Act, 1871), "cognizable offence/case" (arrest without warrant per First Schedule/law), and "non-cognizable offence/case," while designating "complaint" as an allegation to a Magistrate excluding police reports, with an Explanation that police reports disclosing non-cognizable offences are deemed complaints. The section details "bail," "bail bond" (with surety), "bond" (without surety), "bailable offence" (First Schedule/other law), and "non-bailable offence," as well as "High Court," "local jurisdiction," "Public Prosecutor" (appointed under section 18), "victim" (including guardian or legal heir), and distinguishes "summons-case" from "warrant-case" (punishable with death, imprisonment for life or imprisonment for a term exceeding two years). Finally, it defines "charge," "officer in charge of a police station," "place," "police report" (under sub-section (3) of section 193), "police station," and "sub-division," stipulating that undefined terms defined in the Information Technology Act, 2000 and the Bharatiya Nyaya Sanhita, 2023 shall retain those meanings.
BNSS_S4:214c53f9cb89bd57f23cb203b18dd7e2: Section 4 mandates that all offences under the Bharatiya Nyaya Sanhita, 2023, shall be investigated, inquired into, tried, and otherwise dealt with according to the provisions contained in this Sanhita. All offences under any other law shall be investigated, inquired into, tried, and otherwise dealt with according to the same provisions, subject to any enactment for the time being in force regulating the manner or place of investigating, inquiring into, trying, or otherwise dealing with such offences.
BNSS_S6:27e20b85f240f73dff1198aa66a3f0ab: Section 6 specifies that, besides High Courts and Courts constituted under any law other than this Sanhita, every State shall have specific Classes of Criminal Courts. These classes are defined as (i) Courts of Session, (ii) Judicial Magistrates of the first class, (iii) Judicial Magistrates of the second class, and (iv) Executive Magistrates.
BNS_S137:d6dd062977c2c27a8dbf5bf0d86a21a: Section 137 defines kidnapping as either kidnapping from India, by conveying any person beyond the limits of India without the consent of that person or a person legally authorised to consent, or kidnapping from lawful guardianship, by taking or enticing any child or any person of unsound mind out of the keeping of the lawful guardian without consent. The Explanation clarifies that “lawful guardian” includes any person lawfully entrusted with the care or custody of such child or other person, while an Exception applies to persons acting in good faith believing themselves to be the father of an illegitimate child or entitled to lawful custody, unless the act is committed for an immoral or unlawful purpose. Whoever kidnaps any person from India or from lawful guardianship shall be punished with imprisonment of either description for a term which may extend to seven years, and shall also be liable to fine.
BNSS_S8:aa11198b458ccab9b160d5fa571d115f: The State Government shall establish a Court of Session for every sessions division, presided over by a Judge appointed by the High Court, which may also appoint Additional Sessions Judges or appoint a Sessions Judge of one division as an Additional Sessions Judge of another. In the event of a vacancy, absence, or inability to act of the Sessions Judge, the High Court or Sessions Judge may authorize an Additional Sessions Judge or, if none exists, the Chief Judicial Magistrate to dispose of urgent applications. The Court ordinarily sits at places specified by High Court notification, but may sit elsewhere in the sessions division for the convenience of parties and witnesses with the consent of the prosecution and the accused, while the Sessions Judge distributes business among Additional Sessions Judges. This section includes an Explanation clarifying that “appointment” does not include the first appointment, posting or promotion of a person by the Government to any Service, or post in connection with the affairs of the Union or of a State, where required by law.
BNSS_S10:0bb0b44e0033de687699009c4e25303: The High Court shall appoint a Judicial Magistrate of the first class as the Chief Judicial Magistrate in every district and may appoint any Judicial Magistrate of the first class as an Additional Chief Judicial Magistrate with powers directed by the High Court under this Sanhita or any other law. The High Court may designate any Judicial Magistrate of the first class in any sub-division as the Sub-divisional Judicial Magistrate and relieve him of specified responsibilities as occasion requires. Subject to the general control of the Chief Judicial Magistrate, the Sub-divisional Judicial Magistrate exercises supervision and control over the work of Judicial Magistrates (excluding Additional Chief Judicial Magistrates) in the sub-division as specified by general or special orders of the High Court.
BNS_S145:c60eec985941a56e0360cfa6a419d9f: Section 145 of the Bharatiya Nyaya Sanhita, 2023, penalizes whoever habitually imports, exports, removes, buys, sells, traffics or deals in slaves. The offender shall be punished with imprisonment for life, or with imprisonment of either description for a term not exceeding ten years, and shall also be liable to fine.
BNSS_S18:ace80d902614949ba8186bcbce073fe: The Central or State Government appoints Public Prosecutors and Additional Public Prosecutors for High Courts after consultation with the High Court, with a Proviso specifically for the National Capital Territory of Delhi, while the Central Government may also appoint Public Prosecutors for any district or local area. For districts, the State Government appoints Public Prosecutors and Additional Public Prosecutors—who may be appointed for multiple districts per Proviso—from a panel prepared by the District Magistrate in consultation with the Sessions Judge, unless a "regular Cadre of Prosecuting Officers" exists (as defined in an Explanation), in which case appointments are from that Cadre unless no suitable person is available (per Proviso). Eligibility requires practice as an advocate for "not less than seven years" for standard appointments, while Special Public Prosecutors for specific cases or classes of cases require practice for "not less than ten years," subject to a Proviso allowing the Court to permit victims to engage an advocate of their choice to assist the prosecution. Under sub-section (9), service as a Public Prosecutor, Additional Public Prosecutor, Assistant Public Prosecutor, or other Prosecuting Officer is deemed to be the period during which such person has been in practice as an advocate.
BNS_S151:3cd4441f8603ec338c10476d3d6a5c0: Section 151 applies to whoever, with the intention of inducing or compelling the President of India, or Governor of any State, to exercise or refrain from exercising in any manner any of the lawful powers of such President or Governor, assaults or wrongfully restrains, or attempts wrongfully to restrain, or overawes, by means of criminal force or the show of criminal force, or attempts so to overawe. Such person shall be punished with imprisonment of either description for a term which may extend to seven years, and shall also be liable to fine.
BNSS_S20:a9e46c2ce16aae086c68c0c2ceb5ec1: The State Government may establish a Directorate of Prosecution (Director and Deputy Directors) and a District Directorate of Prosecution (Deputy Directors and Assistant Directors), with eligibility requiring not less than fifteen years as an advocate or being a Sessions Judge for Director/Deputy Director, and not less than seven years as an advocate or being a Magistrate of the first class for Assistant Director. Headed by the Director of Prosecution under the administrative control of the Home Department, the hierarchy subordinates Assistant Directors to Deputy Directors and Deputy Directors to the Director, with High Court prosecutors (u/s 18(1) or 18(8)) subordinate to the Director and District Court/Assistant Public Prosecutors (u/s 18(3), 18(8), or 19(1)) subordinate to Deputy or Assistant Directors. Functions include the Director monitoring cases punishable for ten years or more, life imprisonment, or death and giving opinions on appeals; the Deputy Director scrutinising police reports and monitoring cases punishable for seven years or more, but less than ten years; and the Assistant Director monitoring cases punishable for less than seven years, though all may be responsible for all proceedings under this Sanhita. Other powers and areas are specified via State Government notification, and these provisions shall not apply to the Advocate General for the State while performing the functions of a Public Prosecutor.
BNS_S155:b1319f622f8aca711d9a592f35ae62a: Section 155 pertains to whoever receives any property knowing the same to have been taken in the commission of the offences mentioned in sections 153 and 154 regarding property taken by war or depredation. Such an individual shall be punished with imprisonment of either description for a term which may extend to seven years, and shall also be liable to fine and to forfeiture of the property so received.
BNS_S157:d9697a915b6d5f4d6d45c72a4e6e63b: Under Section 157, a public servant having the custody of any State prisoner or prisoner of war commits an offence if they negligently suffer such prisoner to escape from any place of confinement in which such prisoner is confined. The offender shall be punished with simple imprisonment for a term which may extend to three years, and shall also be liable to fine.
BNSS_S24:4c3dcda8f73bd76a0d40864259fda89: The Court of a Magistrate may award a term of imprisonment in default of payment of fine as authorized by law. This is subject to a Proviso that the term is not in excess of the powers of the Magistrate under section 23 and, where imprisonment is part of the substantive sentence, shall not exceed one-fourth of the term of imprisonment which the Magistrate is competent to inflict as punishment for the offence otherwise than as imprisonment in default of payment of the fine. Furthermore, imprisonment awarded under this section may be in addition to a substantive sentence of imprisonment for the maximum term awardable by the Magistrate under section 23.
BNSS_S28:629cc4ba806841e602f39d981085ae1: Section 28 authorizes the High Court or the State Government to withdraw all or any of the powers conferred by it under this Sanhita on any person or by any officer subordinate to it. Additionally, any powers conferred by the Chief Judicial Magistrate or the District Magistrate may be withdrawn by the respective Magistrate by whom such powers were conferred.
BNS_S161:ea5c54f77b4052854351844ec638560: Section 161 concerns whoever abets an assault by an officer, soldier, sailor or airman, in the Army, Navy or Air Force of the Government of India, on any superior officer being in the execution of his office. Such person shall be punished with imprisonment of either description for a term which may extend to three years, and shall also be liable to fine.
BNSS_S32:dd2e9c75b7d822055d18d1eb6b17e4b: Section 32 stipulates that when a warrant is directed to a person other than a police officer, any other person may aid in the execution of such warrant. This aid is permissible provided the person to whom the warrant is directed is near at hand and acting in the execution of the warrant.
BNS_S163:5deab1241583b68559d31728d784a65: Section 163 stipulates that whoever abets the desertion of any officer, soldier, sailor or airman in the Army, Navy or Air Force of the Government of India shall be punished. The prescribed punishment is imprisonment of either description for a term which may extend to two years, or with fine, or with both.
BNS_S165:5ec8f93b704946496c5ec70ec78a221: The master or person in charge of a merchant vessel is liable to a penalty not exceeding three thousand rupees if any deserter from the Army, Navy or Air Force of the Government of India is concealed on board. This liability applies even if the master or person in charge is ignorant of such concealment, provided they might have known of it but for some neglect of duty as such master or person in charge, or but for some want of discipline on board the vessel.
BNSS_S36:c6919acb674b48fe5c7ca2b6fd3d829: Under Section 36, every police officer making an arrest must bear an accurate, visible and clear identification of his name. The officer shall prepare a memorandum of arrest attested by at least one witness—who must be a member of the person arrested's family or a respectable member of the locality—and countersigned by the person arrested. Unless the memorandum is attested by a family member, the officer must inform the person arrested of their right to have a relative, friend, or any other person named by them informed of the arrest.
BNSS_S40:30f383224c993170689f613c4290f44: A private person may arrest any person who commits a non-bailable and cognizable offence in his presence or any proclaimed offender, handing them over to a police officer or the nearest police station without unnecessary delay, but within six hours from such arrest. If the person comes under the provisions of sub-section (1) of section 35, a police officer shall take him in custody. In cases of a non-cognizable offence, if the person refuses to provide his name and residence or gives false details, he shall be dealt with under section 39; otherwise, if there is no sufficient reason to believe he committed any offence, he shall be at once released.
BNS_S167:521b9649ac1dd7c66168fcdb637e4c8: Section 167 stipulates that no person subject to the Air Force Act, 1950 (45 of 1950), the Army Act, 1950 (46 of 1950), and the Navy Act, 1957 (62 of 1957) shall be subject to punishment under this Sanhita. This provision specifically applies to any of the offences defined in this Chapter.
BNS_S169:1937789c889729228c418b7c599b53f: For the purposes of this Chapter, Section 169 defines “candidate” as a person who has been nominated as a candidate at any election. It defines “electoral right” as the right of a person to stand, or not to stand as, or to withdraw from being, a candidate or to vote or refrain from voting at an election.
BNS_S171:3510fa0435f319a349660c9e0b313bf: Section 171 defines the offence of undue influence at elections as whoever voluntarily interferes or attempts to interfere with the free exercise of any electoral right. Without prejudice to the generality of subsection (1), interference includes threatening any candidate or voter, or any person in whom a candidate or voter is interested, with injury of any kind, or inducing or attempting to induce a candidate or voter to believe that he or any person in whom he is interested will become or be rendered an object of Divine displeasure or of spiritual censure. Subsection (3) stipulates that a declaration of public policy, a promise of public action, or the mere exercise of a legal right without intent to interfere with an electoral right shall not be deemed to be interference.
BNSS_S48:da8c57df6421e3eb1ca7dcb69e30502: Every police officer or person making an arrest under this Sanhita shall forthwith inform the arrested person's relatives, friends, nominated persons, and the designated police officer in the district regarding the arrest and the place where the arrested person is being held. The police officer must inform the arrested person of his rights under sub-section (1) upon arrival at the police station, and an entry of who was informed shall be made in a book kept in the police station in a form provided by State Government rules. The Magistrate before whom the arrested person is produced must satisfy himself that the requirements of sub-section (2) and sub-section (3) have been complied with.
BNSS_S50:878d304df9c841b99da7315276a229c: Section 50 empowers a police officer or other person making any arrest under this Sanhita to take any offensive weapons from the person arrested immediately after the arrest is made. All weapons so taken shall be delivered to the Court or officer before which or whom the officer or person making the arrest is required by this Sanhita to produce the person arrested.
BNS_S173:f0978109ccf2dba88fcaf0405440029: Whoever commits the offence of bribery shall be punished with imprisonment of either description for a term which may extend to one year, or with fine, or with both. A Proviso stipulates that bribery by treating shall be punished with fine only. An Explanation defines “treating” as that form of bribery where the gratification consists in food, drink, entertainment, or provision.
BNSS_S56:acf6c33a1c493fdfc3c707988fc7874: Section 56 establishes a legal duty for the person having the custody of an accused. It mandates that such person shall take reasonable care of the health and safety of the accused.
BNS_S175:2f683fea9618d04f2b37175d3beddae: Under Section 175, whoever, with intent to affect the result of an election, makes or publishes any statement purporting to be a statement of fact which is false, in relation to the personal character or conduct of any candidate, shall be punished with fine. This applies if the person knows or believes the statement to be false or does not believe it to be true.
BNSS_S58:fe9ee3902b7b9a008b30a14168e72c4: Section 58 stipulates that a person arrested shall not be detained more than twenty-four hours. Officers in charge of police stations must report all cases of persons arrested without warrant within their respective station limits to the District Magistrate, or to the Sub-divisional Magistrate if so directed. This reporting requirement applies regardless of whether such persons have been admitted to bail or otherwise.
BNSS_S60:c3d24fa6ce2dab84691aa98b62704ab: Section 60 governs the discharge of a person apprehended. It mandates that no person arrested by a police officer shall be discharged except on his bond, or bail bond, or under the special order of a Magistrate.
BNS_S177:a63b7b7a22f0a592d8750936ef3ee6c: Section 177 addresses the failure to keep election accounts. Whoever being required by any law for the time being in force or any rule having the force of law to keep accounts of expenses incurred at or in connection with an election fails to keep such accounts shall be punished with a fine which may extend to five thousand rupees.
BNSS_S62:a392743f52d11973390cda0803f3a69: Section 62 mandates that no arrest shall be made except in accordance with the provisions of this Sanhita. It further specifies that arrests must be made according to any other law for the time being in force providing for arrest.
BNS_S179:0bdd0590ef9dd5aa01051e151ca3312: Under Section 179, whoever imports, exports, sells, delivers to, buys, receives from, otherwise traffics, or uses as genuine any forged or counterfeit coin, stamp, currency-note, or bank-note, knowing or having reason to believe the same to be forged or counterfeit, commits an offence. Such person shall be punished with imprisonment for life, or with imprisonment of either description for a term which may extend to ten years, and shall also be liable to fine.
BNSS_S64:944d0ab5b3736aadbef148fae1d4bff: Every summons shall be served by a police officer, or subject to State Government rules, by an officer of the Court issuing it or other public servant, with a Proviso requiring the police station or registrar in the Court to maintain a register of address, email address, phone number, and other details specified by State Government rules. Summons shall, if practicable, be served personally by delivering or tendering one of the duplicates, subject to a Proviso that summons bearing the image of Court's seal may be served by electronic communication in the form and manner provided by State Government rules. Any person served personally shall, if required by the serving officer, sign a receipt on the back of the other duplicate.
BNS_S181:9839f4b2408092442158259811bc239: Section 181 penalizes any person who makes or mends, performs any part of the process of making or mending, buys, sells, disposes of, or has in possession any machinery, die, instrument, or material for the purpose of being used, or knowing or having reason to believe that it is intended to be used, for forging or counterfeiting any coin, stamp issued by Government for the purpose of revenue, currency-note, or bank-note. Such person shall be punished with imprisonment for life, or with imprisonment of either description for a term which may extend to ten years, and shall also be liable to fine.
BNSS_S74:adb8b1e0f133ab09e113a36955d57a9: A warrant of arrest shall ordinarily be directed to one or more police officers. However, if immediate execution is necessary and no police officer is immediately available, the Court issuing such a warrant may direct it to any other person or persons, who shall execute the same. When a warrant is directed to more officers or persons than one, it may be executed by all, or by any one or more of them.
BNS_S183:436c5c5d30a0e3d24dff2c08ff85082: Section 183 penalizes whoever, fraudulently or with intent to cause loss to the Government, removes or effaces from any substance bearing any stamp issued by Government for the purpose of revenue, any writing or document for which such stamp has been used, or removes from any writing or document a stamp which has been used for such writing or document, in order that such stamp may be used for a different writing or document. Such person shall be punished with imprisonment of either description for a term which may extend to three years, or with fine, or with both.
BNSS_S78:02287ed3ccb4eda5cfbcc6e7df10d9f: The police officer or other person executing a warrant of arrest shall, subject to the provisions of section 73 as to security, bring the person arrested before the Court before which he is required by law to produce such person without unnecessary delay. A Proviso specifies that such delay shall not, in any case, exceed twenty-four hours exclusive of the time necessary for the journey from the place of arrest to the Magistrate's Court.
BNSS_S80:ec510b152a4fcebceeea8f48ba61aed: Section 80 stipulates that when a warrant is to be executed outside the local jurisdiction of the issuing Court, the Court may forward it by post or otherwise to an Executive Magistrate, District Superintendent of Police, or Commissioner of Police within the local limits of execution. The receiving official shall endorse his name thereon and, if practicable, cause the warrant to be executed. Furthermore, the issuing Court must forward the substance of the information against the person and relevant documents to enable the Court acting under section 83 to decide whether bail should or should not be granted.
BNS_S187:97f4cf72d3b47791622bca2dc772ace: Whoever, being employed in any mint lawfully established in India, does any act, or omits what he is legally bound to do, with the intention of causing any coin issued from that mint to be of a different weight or composition from the weight or composition fixed by law, commits an offence. Such person shall be punished with imprisonment of either description for a term which may extend to seven years, and shall also be liable to fine.
BNSS_S86:782c768d38cfbb25f8d2812d07cffa1: Upon a written request from a police officer not below the rank of the Superintendent of Police or Commissioner of Police, the Court may request assistance from a Court or an authority in the contracting State for the identification, attachment and forfeiture of property belonging to a proclaimed person. This process shall be conducted in accordance with the procedure provided in Chapter VIII. This section contains no Provisos, Exceptions, or Explanations.
BNSS_S88:6f7a7ec2fba28cc9eb2a542f49fa6ce: If the proclaimed person appears within the time specified in the proclamation, the Court shall order releasing the property from the attachment. If the proclaimed person does not appear within the specified time, the property is at the disposal of the State Government and shall not be sold until the expiration of six months from the date of the attachment and the disposal of any claim or objection under section 87, unless the property is subject to speedy and natural decay or the Court considers the sale would be for the benefit of the owner. If, within two years from the date of the attachment, the person appears voluntarily or is apprehended and proves they did not abscond or conceal themselves for the purpose of avoiding execution of the warrant and lacked notice of the proclamation, the property, net proceeds of the sale, or residue shall be delivered after satisfying all costs incurred in consequence of the attachment.
BNSS_S90:bb81c42ede2da1c89135d1a6131c526: A Court empowered by this Sanhita to issue a summons for the appearance of any person may, after recording its reasons in writing, issue a warrant for his arrest. This may occur if, before the issue of the summons or before the time fixed for appearance, the Court believes the person has absconded or will not obey the summons. Alternatively, a warrant may be issued if the person fails to appear, the summons is proved to have been duly served in time to admit of his appearing, and no reasonable excuse is offered for such failure.
BNSS_S92:8f70d3947fa1156a4015dbcc488c3fe: Under Section 92, when any person bound by a bond or bail bond taken under this Sanhita fails to appear before a Court, the officer presiding in such Court may issue a warrant. This warrant directs that such person be arrested and produced before the presiding officer.
BNSS_S94:64a6054f11efca5593c2b3f8e7aa468: Section 94(1) empowers a Court to issue a summons or an officer in charge of a police station to issue a written order, in physical or electronic form, requiring a person in possession or power of a document, electronic communication (including communication devices likely to contain digital evidence), or other thing to produce it for any investigation, inquiry, trial, or other proceeding under this Sanhita. Under subsection (2), a person is deemed to have complied with the requisition if they cause the document or thing to be produced without attending personally. Subsection (3) stipulates that this section does not affect sections 129 and 130 of the Bharatiya Sakshya Adhiniyam, 2023, the Bankers’ Books Evidence Act, 1891 (13 of 1891), or any letter, postcard, document, parcel, or thing in the custody of the postal authority.
BNSS_S96:c3aeb7686331adcfe1acfc8889f0cbe: A Court may issue a search-warrant if it believes a person issued a summons under section 94 or a requisition under sub-section (1) of section 95 will not produce the required document or thing, if the possession of such item is unknown to the Court, or if a general search or inspection serves the purposes of any inquiry, trial or other proceeding under this Sanhita. The Court may specify the particular place or part thereof to which the search or inspection shall extend, limiting the executing person to that specified area. Only a District Magistrate or Chief Judicial Magistrate is authorized to grant a warrant to search for a document, parcel or other thing in the custody of the postal authority.
BNSS_S100:be27fad295f7985ff54503fba11e1e: If a District Magistrate, Sub-divisional Magistrate, or Magistrate of the first class has reason to believe a person is confined under circumstances amounting to an offence, they may issue a search-warrant. The person to whom such warrant is directed shall search for the confined person and, if found, immediately take them before a Magistrate. The Magistrate shall then make such order as in the circumstances of the case seems proper.
BNSS_S104:133c84c5cf955168e79272a2086bcf: When things are found during the execution of a search-warrant beyond the local jurisdiction of the Court which issued the same, such things and the prepared list shall be immediately taken before the Court issuing the warrant. If the place is nearer to the Magistrate having jurisdiction therein than to the issuing Court, the list and things shall be immediately taken before such Magistrate. Unless there be good cause to the contrary, such Magistrate shall make an order authorising them to be taken to the Court issuing the warrant.
BNS_S189:727b4e2bc9f7a15be26038d3cc657da: An "unlawful assembly" comprises five or more persons with a common object to overawe by criminal force, or show of criminal force, the Central Government, any State Government, Parliament, the Legislature of any State, or any public servant in the exercise of the lawful power of such public servant; resist the execution of any law or legal process; commit mischief, criminal trespass, or other offence; by means of criminal force, or show of criminal force, to any person, to take or obtain possession of any property, or to deprive any person of the enjoyment of a right of way, or of the use of water or other incorporeal right of which he is in possession or enjoyment, or to enforce any right or supposed right; or by means of criminal force, or show of criminal force, to compel any person to do what he is not legally bound to do, or omit to do what he is legally entitled to do, with an Explanation that an assembly which was not unlawful when it assembled, may subsequently become an unlawful assembly. Those intentionally joining or continuing an unlawful assembly face imprisonment of either description for a term which may extend to six months, or with fine, or with both; however, those joining or continuing after a lawful command to disperse, or members armed with any deadly weapon or anything which, used as a weapon of offence, is likely to cause death, face imprisonment of either description for a term which may extend to two years, or with fine, or with both, with an Explanation that if the assembly is an unlawful assembly within the meaning of sub-section (1), the offender shall be punishable under sub-section (3). Joining or continuing an assembly of five or more persons likely to cause a disturbance of the public peace after a lawful command to disperse, or hiring, engaging, promoting, or conniving at the hiring of any person to join an unlawful assembly, or harbouring, receiving, or assembling hired or engaged persons in occupied premises, is punishable with imprisonment of either description for a term which may extend to six months, or with fine, or with both. Persons engaged, hired, or offering to be hired or engaged to do or assist in any of the acts specified in sub-section (1) face imprisonment of either description for a term which may extend to six months, or with fine, or with both, while such hired persons going armed, or engaging or offering to go armed, with any deadly weapon or anything which used as a weapon of offence is likely to cause death, face imprisonment of either description for a term which may extend to two years, or with fine, or with both.
BNSS_S106:bf6599425f43332190adea945a2099: Under Section 106, any police officer may seize property alleged or suspected to have been stolen or found under circumstances creating suspicion of the commission of any offence, with subordinate officers required to report such seizure to the officer in charge of a police station. Every police officer must report the seizure to the Magistrate having jurisdiction; however, if the property cannot be conveniently transported, lacks proper accommodation, or is not necessary for investigation, custody may be granted to a person executing a bond to produce the property and obey Court disposal orders. A Proviso states that property subject to speedy and natural decay, valued at less than five hundred rupees and where the entitled person is unknown or absent, may be sold by auction under orders of the Superintendent of Police, with sections 503 and 504 applying to the net proceeds.
BNSS_S110:fd596b57ff645d4b0c670397b8d4e6: Section 110 enables a Court in "the said territories" to send a summons to an accused person, a warrant for the arrest of an accused person, a summons to produce a document or other thing, or a search-warrant in duplicate for execution in India outside the said territories (via the presiding officer of that Court, applying section 70 for summons) or in a "contracting State" (via a Court, Judge or Magistrate and authority specified by Central Government notification). Courts in "the said territories" receiving such processes from Courts in India outside the said territories or from a Court, Judge or Magistrate in a "contracting State" shall execute them as if received from another Court in the said territories. Arrest warrants shall be dealt with per sections 82 and 83, and search-warrants per section 104. A Proviso mandates that for summons or search-warrants from a "contracting State," documents or things produced or found shall be forwarded to the issuing Court through the authority specified by Central Government notification.
BNS_S191:afe2d048e7b5643e46afe8b7a1870a9: Rioting is committed whenever force or violence is used by an unlawful assembly, or by any member thereof, in prosecution of the common object of such assembly, rendering every member guilty of the offence of rioting. Whoever is guilty of rioting shall be punished with imprisonment of either description for a term which may extend to two years, or with fine, or with both. If the person is guilty of rioting while being armed with a deadly weapon or with anything which, used as a weapon of offence, is likely to cause death, they shall be punished with imprisonment of either description for a term which may extend to five years, or with fine, or with both.
BNSS_S112:dc7f8e94d41a8533d7e6dbc2b7cfc6: Upon application by an investigating officer or any officer superior in rank stating evidence may be available in a country or place outside India, any Criminal Court may issue a letter of request to a competent Court or authority to orally examine persons, record statements, and require the production of any document or thing pertaining to the case. This letter of request shall be transmitted in the manner specified by the Central Government. Every statement recorded or document or thing received under sub-section (1) shall be deemed to be the evidence collected during the course of investigation under this Sanhita.
BNSS_S120:7f4c654a1d769b465fb521c294f5c6: The Court may record a finding on whether properties are proceeds of crime after considering available material and the explanation to the show-cause notice issued under section 119, provided the person affected (and any other person holding the property) is given a reasonable opportunity of being heard. Under a Proviso, if the affected person or third party does not appear or represent their case within "thirty days" specified in the notice, the Court may record a finding ex parte based on available evidence. Where specific identification is impossible, the Court may specify properties it judges as proceeds of crime, which shall then stand forfeited to the Central Government free from all encumbrances. For forfeited shares, the company shall forthwith register the Central Government as the transferee, notwithstanding the Companies Act, 2013 or the Articles of Association.
BNSS_S122:84559ee4413b6174ec416d8511819b: Under Section 122, any transfer by any mode whatsoever of property referred to in an order under sub-section (1) of section 117 or a notice under section 119 shall be ignored for the purposes of the proceedings under this Chapter. If such property is subsequently forfeited to the Central Government under section 120, the transfer of such property shall be deemed to be null and void.
BNSS_S124:e9c9ca2c9ca4516dfd01b302d0e075: The Central Government may, by notification in the Official Gazette, direct the application of this Chapter in relation to a contracting State with which reciprocal arrangements have been made. Such application shall be subject to the conditions, exceptions or qualifications specified in the said notification.
BNS_S197:5a49c55a6f04cc67eb0b14ded4f6a3d: Section 197 penalizes whoever, by words either spoken or written or by signs or by visible representations or through electronic communication or otherwise, (a) makes or publishes imputations that any class of persons, by reason of their being members of any religious, racial, language or regional group or caste or community, cannot bear true faith and allegiance to the Constitution of India or uphold its sovereignty and integrity; (b) asserts, counsels, advises, propagates or publishes that such classes shall be denied or deprived of rights as citizens; (c) makes or publishes any assertion, counsel, plea or appeal concerning the obligation of such classes causing or likely to cause disharmony or feelings of enmity or hatred or ill-will; or (d) publishes false or misleading information jeopardising the sovereignty, unity and integrity or security of India, punishable with imprisonment which may extend to three years, or with fine, or with both. Under sub-section (2), if an offence specified in sub-section (1) is committed in any place of worship or in any assembly engaged in the performance of religious worship or religious ceremonies, the offender shall be punished with imprisonment which may extend to five years and shall also be liable to fine.
BNSS_S126:51097bab5874d5b56279059dc8f465: An Executive Magistrate, upon receiving information and finding sufficient ground that a person is likely to commit a breach of the peace, disturb the public tranquillity, or do any wrongful act that may probably occasion a breach of the peace or disturb the public tranquillity, may require such person to show cause why they should not be ordered to execute a bond or bail bond for keeping the peace for a period not exceeding one year. Proceedings may be taken before any Executive Magistrate if the place where the breach of the peace or disturbance is apprehended is within his local jurisdiction, or if a person likely to commit such acts is within such jurisdiction, even if the act is to be committed beyond such jurisdiction.
BNSS_S128:5cbed21223a918c0e95890167190cd: When an Executive Magistrate receives information that a person within his local jurisdiction is taking precautions to conceal his presence and has reason to believe this is done with a view to committing a cognizable offence, the Magistrate may require such person to show cause. The person may be ordered to execute a bond or bail bond for his good behaviour for a period, not exceeding one year, as the Magistrate thinks fit.
BNSS_S130:d6a9307280e84e130a3260ffa19e00: When a Magistrate acting under section 126, section 127, section 128 or section 129 deems it necessary to require any person to show cause, he shall make an order in writing. This order must set forth the substance of the information received, the amount of the bond to be executed, the term for which it is to be in force, and the number of sureties. The order is made after the Magistrate considers the sufficiency and fitness of sureties.
BNS_S205:7bb9adfa775018a4a790808aea1e531: Section 205 penalizes whoever, not belonging to a certain class of public servants, wears any garb or carries any token resembling any garb or token used by that class of public servants. The offence is established with the intention that it may be believed, or with the knowledge that it is likely to be believed, that the individual belongs to that class of public servants. Punishment involves imprisonment of either description for a term which may extend to three months, or with fine which may extend to five thousand rupees, or with both.
BNSS_S132:3fc7f4e6f2f2f39db174f10f213d67: If a person is not present in Court, the Magistrate shall issue a summons requiring him to appear, or, if the person is in custody, a warrant directing the officer in whose custody he is to bring him before the Court. A Proviso stipulates that if the Magistrate determines, based on a police officer's report or other information (the substance of which shall be recorded), that there is reason to fear the commission of a breach of the peace which cannot be prevented otherwise than by immediate arrest, the Magistrate may issue a warrant for his arrest.
BNSS_S134:8013248206585358751366dd3cb4be: Section 134 empowers the Magistrate, upon seeing sufficient cause, to dispense with the personal attendance of any person called upon to show cause why he should not be ordered to execute a bond for keeping the peace or for good behaviour. In such instances, the Magistrate may permit the person to appear by an advocate.
BNS_S207:dbda59cc6188890d7209d15dfbcf53e: Section 207 penalizes anyone who intentionally prevents the serving of any summons, notice or order from a legally competent public servant, prevents the lawful affixing to any place, removes any such summons, notice or order from any place to which it is lawfully affixed, or prevents the lawful making of any proclamation under the authority of a legally competent public servant. Under clause (a), the punishment is simple imprisonment for a term which may extend to one month, or with fine which may extend to five thousand rupees, or with both. Under clause (b), where the summons, notice, order or proclamation is to attend in person or by agent, or to produce a document or electronic record in a Court, the punishment is simple imprisonment for a term which may extend to six months, or with fine which may extend to ten thousand rupees, or with both.
BNSS_S138:3e56a10b87041a5e18cf1fa2be4f54: Section 138 governs the commencement of the period for which security is required under section 125 or section 136. For any person sentenced to, or undergoing a sentence of, imprisonment at the time the order is made, the period shall commence on the expiration of such sentence. In other cases, the period shall commence on the date of such order, unless the Magistrate, for sufficient reason, fixes a later date.
BNSS_S146:4850a159c852a9d5021e604e9fa64a: Under Section 146, the Magistrate may alter a monthly allowance for maintenance or interim maintenance ordered under section 144 for a wife, child, father or mother upon proof of a change in circumstances, and shall cancel or vary such orders following a decision of a competent Civil Court. Regarding a divorced woman, the Magistrate shall cancel the order if she remarries (from date of remarriage), received the whole sum payable under customary or personal law (from the date of order if paid before, or from the date of expiry of the paid period if paid after), or voluntarily surrendered her rights to maintenance or interim maintenance (from date thereof). Furthermore, a Civil Court, when making a decree for the recovery of maintenance or dowry, shall take into account sums already paid or recovered under section 144.
BNS_S209:ab1268dc95ebc816685bdfebc0ec492: Section 209 penalizes failure to appear at the specified place and the specified time as required by a proclamation published under sub-section (1) of section 84 of the Bharatiya Nagarik Suraksha Sanhita, 2023, with imprisonment for a term which may extend to three years, or with fine, or with both, or with community service. Where a declaration has been made under sub-section (4) of section 84 pronouncing the person as a proclaimed offender, the individual shall be punished with imprisonment for a term which may extend to seven years and shall also be liable to fine.
BNSS_S148:ceb6fcb504634ab07203d3ba66befd: Under Section 148(1), any Executive Magistrate, officer in charge of a police station, or police officer not below the rank of a sub-inspector may command any unlawful assembly, or any assembly of five or more persons likely to cause a disturbance of the public peace, to disperse. Pursuant to sub-section (2), if such assembly fails to disperse or demonstrates a determination not to disperse, these officers may use force and require the assistance of any person, excluding officers or members of the armed forces acting as such. This mechanism allows for the arresting and confining of persons forming part of the assembly to effect dispersal or so they may be punished according to law.
BNSS_S150:69862e96092011f33a8f922db44c89: Section 150 authorizes any commissioned or gazetted officer of the armed forces to disperse an assembly using armed forces under his command when public security is manifestly endangered and no Executive Magistrate can be communicated with. Such officer may arrest and confine any persons forming part of the assembly to effect dispersal or for punishment according to law. If communication with an Executive Magistrate becomes practicable, the officer shall do so and thenceforward obey the Magistrate's instructions regarding whether he shall or shall not continue such action.
BNSS_S152:02518f9558c75be389aed8344062b8: A District Magistrate, Sub-divisional Magistrate, or State Government-specially empowered Executive Magistrate, upon a police officer's report or other information and evidence, may issue a conditional order to remove nuisances. These include unlawful obstructions in public places, ways, rivers, or channels; trades, occupations, or goods/merchandise injurious to the community's health or physical comfort; constructions or substance disposals likely to occasion conflagration or explosion; unstable buildings, tents, structures, or trees; unfenced tanks, wells, or excavations; and dangerous animals. The order requires the responsible person to remedy the situation within a fixed time or appear before the Magistrate or a subordinate Executive Magistrate to show cause why the order should not be made absolute. Such orders shall not be called in question in any Civil Court, and an Explanation defines "public place" to include State property, camping grounds, and grounds left unoccupied for sanitary or recreative purposes.
BNS_S211:3b8304bad101318ae57f9859da9f764: Section 211 penalizes any person who, being legally bound to give any notice or to furnish information on any subject to any public servant, as such, intentionally omits to do so in the manner and at the time required by law. Such omission is punishable with simple imprisonment for a term which may extend to one month, or fine which may extend to five thousand rupees, or both; if the notice or information respects the commission of an offence, is required for the purpose of preventing the commission of an offence, or is in order to the apprehension of an offender, the punishment is simple imprisonment for a term which may extend to six months, or fine which may extend to ten thousand rupees, or both. Where notice or information is required by an order passed under section 394 of the Bharatiya Nagarik Suraksha Sanhita, 2023, the punishment is imprisonment of either description for a term which may extend to six months, or fine which may extend to one thousand rupees, or both.
BNSS_S156:e83e691b55ab124e7c95d409a1d10a: Under Section 156(1), when an order is made under section 152 to prevent obstruction, nuisance or danger to the public in the use of any way, river, channel or place, the Magistrate must question the person against whom the order was made regarding the denial of any public right and, if denied, conduct an inquiry before proceeding under section 157. Pursuant to Section 156(2), the Magistrate shall stay proceedings until a competent Court decides the matter if reliable evidence supports the denial; otherwise, the Magistrate shall proceed as laid down in section 157. Per Section 156(3), any person who fails to deny the public right upon questioning or fails to adduce reliable evidence for such denial shall not be permitted to make any such denial in subsequent proceedings.
BNSS_S158:c2236a1fb351b72a21f99378f4409f: Under Section 158, for the purposes of an inquiry under section 156 or section 157, the Magistrate is empowered to direct a local investigation to be made by such person as he thinks fit. Additionally, the Magistrate may summon and examine an expert.
BNS_S213:84f40153fefa48ae6e920a447c551a8: Section 213 applies to whoever refuses to bind himself by an oath or affirmation to state the truth, when required so to bind himself by a public servant legally competent to require that he shall so bind himself. The offence is punishable with simple imprisonment for a term which may extend to six months, or with fine which may extend to five thousand rupees, or with both.
BNSS_S162:fb87fdfa70b39a21fea1bbc5e5a73d: Under Section 162, a District Magistrate, Sub-divisional Magistrate, any other Executive Magistrate, or Deputy Commissioner of Police empowered by the State Government or the District Magistrate may order any person not to repeat or continue a public nuisance. This "public nuisance" is defined in the Bharatiya Nyaya Sanhita, 2023, or any special or local law.
BNS_S215:5a919b137a4a3d77dadcf30d2b11ea6: Section 215 penalizes whoever refuses to sign any statement made by him when required to sign that statement by a public servant legally competent to require that he shall sign that statement. The prescribed punishment is simple imprisonment for a term which may extend to three months, or with fine which may extend to three thousand rupees, or with both.
BNSS_S164:07990aa8c6ba82c4b8293c4567d084: When an Executive Magistrate is satisfied via a police report or other information that a dispute concerning "land or water" (including buildings, markets, fisheries, crops or other produce of land, and rents or profits) or boundaries is likely to cause a "breach of the peace", they shall issue a written order requiring parties to attend and submit written statements regarding "actual possession". The Magistrate decides which party was in possession as of the order date without reference to merits, subject to a Proviso where a party forcibly and wrongfully dispossessed within "two months" before the report/information or before the order may be treated as being in possession. The Magistrate then declares such party entitled to possession until "evicted therefrom in due course of law", forbidding disturbance, while providing mechanisms for adding legal representatives of deceased parties and ordering the custody or sale of produce subject to "speedy and natural decay". Such proceedings may be cancelled if no dispute exists, otherwise the sub-section (1) order is final, and these powers are not in derogation of "section 126".
BNSS_S166:10a405205cc4656679fc405f081484: Upon being satisfied via police report or other information that a dispute likely to cause a breach of the peace exists regarding an alleged right of user of land or water (defined by Explanation per section 164(2)), whether as an easement or otherwise, an Executive Magistrate shall issue a written order requiring parties to attend Court and submit written statements. The Magistrate shall peruse statements, hear parties, and evaluate evidence to decide if the right exists, applying section 164 provisions, and may prohibit interference or order removal of obstruction. A Proviso restricts such orders unless the right was exercised within three months before the report/information (for year-round rights) or during the last of such seasons or on the last of such occasions (for seasonal/occasional rights). Additionally, the Magistrate may, after recording reasons, convert proceedings between section 164(1) and section 166(1).
BNS_S217:a3ee8037d560b49fcb7c7b54798de05: Section 217 applies to whoever gives to any public servant any information which he knows or believes to be false, intending or knowing it likely to cause such public servant (a) to do or omit anything which such public servant ought not to do or omit if the true state of facts respecting which such information is given were known by him, or (b) to use the lawful power of such public servant to the injury or annoyance of any person. The offender shall be punished with imprisonment of either description for a term which may extend to one year, or with fine which may extend to ten thousand rupees, or with both. The section includes illustrations regarding false reporting of misconduct to cause dismissal, false reporting of contraband to cause searches, or false reporting of robbery to cause police enquiries and annoyance to villagers.
BNSS_S168:22acc5f32c03f5c2c2897eedac5f3a: Section 168 mandates that every police officer shall, to the best of his ability, prevent the commission of any cognizable offence. Furthermore, every police officer may interpose for the purpose of preventing such a cognizable offence. This section contains no Provisos, Exceptions, or Explanations.
BNSS_S170:b7f1a828f086a2167e789206f220d6: A police officer knowing of a design to commit any cognizable offence may arrest the person so designing, without orders from a Magistrate and without a warrant, if it appears that the commission of the offence cannot be otherwise prevented. No person arrested under sub-section (1) shall be detained in custody for a period exceeding twenty-four hours from the time of his arrest, unless further detention is required or authorised under any other provisions of this Sanhita or of any other law for the time being in force.
BNS_S219:a2d192e5d92d089ed4206450950b70f: Section 219 penalizes whoever intentionally obstructs any sale of property offered for sale by the lawful authority of any public servant, as such. The punishment for this offence is imprisonment of either description for a term which may extend to one month, or with fine which may extend to five thousand rupees, or with both.
BNSS_S174:cd12f97fae75d225833cbc93cda413: Upon receiving information of a non-cognizable offence, the officer in charge of a police station shall enter the substance in a book prescribed by the State Government, refer the informant to the Magistrate, and forward the daily diary report fortnightly to the Magistrate. No police officer shall investigate a non-cognizable case without an order from a Magistrate having power to try such case or commit the case for trial, granting the officer powers identical to those in a cognizable case investigation, except the power to arrest without warrant. Where a case relates to two or more offences and at least one is cognizable, the case shall be deemed to be a cognizable case, notwithstanding that the other offences are non-cognizable.
BNS_S225:98bb0fc5099eccf40ac368a8b820796: Section 225 applies to whoever holds out any threat of injury to any person for the purpose of inducing that person to refrain or desist from making a legal application for protection against any injury to any public servant legally empowered as such to give such protection, or to cause such protection to be given. Such person shall be punished with imprisonment of either description for a term which may extend to one year, or with fine, or with both.
BNSS_S176:ddc202956e81c86723d7789903137e: An officer in charge of a police station suspecting an offence empowered under section 175 to investigate shall report to a Magistrate empowered to take cognizance and proceed in person or depute a subordinate officer of a State Government prescribed rank to investigate and arrest the offender, subject to a first proviso exempting spot investigation if the person is named and the case is not of a serious nature (a) or there is no sufficient ground (b), and a second proviso requiring rape victims' statements be recorded at her residence or place of choice, preferably by a woman police officer with parents, guardian, near relatives, or social worker, via audio-video electronic means. In cases under the first proviso clauses (a) and (b), the officer must state reasons for non-compliance in the report, forward the daily diary report fortnightly to the Magistrate, and notify the informant in clause (b) cases per State Government rules. For offences punishable for seven years or more, the officer shall, from a date notified within five years by the State Government, cause a forensic expert to collect evidence at the crime scene with videography via mobile phone or electronic device, subject to a proviso allowing the State Government to notify the use of another State's forensic facilities if unavailable.
BNSS_S180:387c59f514aa580cf0382c84adfe71: Under Section 180, an investigating police officer, or an officer of a rank prescribed by the State Government acting on requisition, may orally examine any person acquainted with the facts and circumstances of the case. Such persons are bound to answer truly all questions, except those tending to expose them to a criminal charge, penalty, or forfeiture. The officer may reduce statements into separate and true written records, subject to a Proviso allowing recording by audio-video electronic means. A further Proviso mandates that statements of women victims of offences under sections 64, 65, 66, 67, 68, 69, 70, 71, 74, 75, 76, 77, 78, 79 or 124 of the Bharatiya Nyaya Sanhita, 2023, be recorded by a woman police officer or any woman officer.
BNS_S231:1aeeabb22863a5c791725ca10ade55e: Whoever gives or fabricates false evidence intending to cause, or knowing it to be likely that he will thereby cause, any person to be convicted of an offence which is not capital but punishable with imprisonment for life, or imprisonment for a term of seven years or upwards, shall be punished as a person convicted of that offence would be liable to be punished. The section includes an illustration where giving false evidence to cause a conviction of dacoity—an offence punishable with imprisonment for life, or rigorous imprisonment for a term which may extend to ten years, with or without fine—renders the actor liable to imprisonment for life or imprisonment, with or without fine.
BNSS_S182:7fb554c678c1333d0c97b1a8aaed0f: Section 182(1) prohibits any police officer or other person in authority from offering, making, or causing to be offered or made any inducement, threat, or promise as mentioned in section 22 of the Bharatiya Sakshya Adhiniyam, 2023. Section 182(2) mandates that no police officer or other person shall prevent any person, by caution or otherwise, from making a statement of their own free will during any investigation under this Chapter. This sub-section contains a Proviso stating that nothing therein shall affect the provisions of sub-section (4) of section 183.
BNS_S233:0aa0beb833df2974c648bfc02d7d637: Section 233 applies to whoever corruptly uses or attempts to use as true or genuine evidence any evidence which he knows to be false or fabricated. Such an individual shall be punished in the same manner as if he gave or fabricated false evidence.
BNSS_S184:76d1ec4ffd59202de224be413bd60f: During the investigation of an offence of committing rape or attempt to commit rape, the woman shall be sent within twenty-four hours of receiving information for examination by a registered medical practitioner (employed by a Government or local authority hospital, or otherwise) provided consent is obtained from the woman or a competent person. The registered medical practitioner shall without delay prepare a report detailing the woman's name, address, age, material for DNA profiling, marks of injury, general mental condition, other material particulars, precise reasons for conclusions, record of consent, and exact time of commencement and completion. This report must be forwarded to the investigating officer within seven days, who shall forward it to the Magistrate referred to in section 193 as part of the documents under clause (a) of sub-section (6) of that section, with the caveat that examination without consent is not rendered lawful. An Explanation specifies that “examination” and “registered medical practitioner” carry the same meanings as assigned in section 51.
BNSS_S192:b08584a27681f7fe8be5cf9bd54fe5: Every police officer investigating under this Chapter must maintain a paginated volume diary, recording day-by-day the time information reached, investigation start and close times, places visited, circumstances ascertained, and witness statements recorded under section 180. A Criminal Court may send for and use such police diaries to aid inquiry or trial, although not as evidence. Neither the accused nor their agents are entitled to call for or see these diaries merely because they are referred to by the Court. However, if the diaries are used by the police officer to refresh memory or by the Court to contradict said officer, the provisions of section 148 or section 164 of the Bharatiya Sakshya Adhiniyam, 2023, shall apply.
BNS_S235:9594c41096b8d960285a0e3374c24fa: Section 235 applies to whoever corruptly uses or attempts to use any such certificate as a true certificate, knowing the same to be false in any material point. Such an individual shall be punished in the same manner as if he gave false evidence.
BNSS_S202:0858d223868915dc53571361762be0: Offences including cheating involving deception via electronic communications, letters, or telecommunication messages may be inquired into or tried by any Court where such communications, letters, or messages were sent or received. Cheating and dishonestly inducing delivery of property may be inquired into or tried by a Court where the property was delivered by the person deceived or received by the accused person. Offences punishable under section 82 of the Bharatiya Nyaya Sanhita, 2023, may be inquired into or tried where the offence was committed, where the offender last resided with his or her spouse by the first marriage, or where the wife by the first marriage has taken up permanent residence after the commission of the offence.
BNS_S237:d1b09adae4583aacb7a438c9e3b691e: Whoever corruptly uses or attempts to use any such declaration as true, knowing the same to be false in any material point, shall be punished in the same manner as if he gave false evidence. An Explanation provides that a declaration which is inadmissible merely upon the ground of some informality is a declaration within the meaning of section 236 and this section.
BNSS_S208:84716d6d2c1c0b5c99d5adfa92b2fb: An offence committed outside India by a citizen of India, whether on the high seas or elsewhere, or by a person, not being such citizen, on any ship or aircraft registered in India, may be dealt with as if it had been committed at any place within India at which the person may be found or where the offence is registered in India. The section includes a Proviso that, notwithstanding anything in any of the preceding sections of the Chapter, no such offence shall be inquired into or tried in India except with the previous sanction of the Central Government.
BNS_S7:65370f881aa14d38e42e380a3d57dc628: Section 7 stipulates that in every case where an offender is punishable with imprisonment which may be of either description, the sentencing Court is competent to direct the nature of the imprisonment. The Court may direct that such imprisonment shall be wholly rigorous, wholly simple, or that any part of such imprisonment shall be rigorous and the rest simple.
BNS_S13:c44d6aa8ef7ba9f89cf5f19a14beeae9: Section 13 mandates enhanced punishment for anyone previously convicted by a Court in India of an offence punishable under Chapter X or Chapter XVII with imprisonment of either description for a term of three years or upwards. If such a person is subsequently guilty of any offence punishable under either of those Chapters with like imprisonment for the like term, they shall be subject for every such subsequent offence to imprisonment for life or imprisonment of either description for a term which may extend to ten years. This section contains no Provisos, Exceptions, or Explanations.
BNS_S31:7f4c4b182b170a6366fb65b99f31f87d: Section 31 provides that no communication made in good faith is an offence by reason of any harm to the person to whom it is made, provided it is made for the benefit of that person. This is illustrated by a surgeon communicating a terminal opinion in good faith, where the surgeon commits no offence even if the patient dies from the resulting shock and the surgeon knew such a result was likely.
BNS_S2:5895ef7c4e5d00a1cafe546a1a7ff8ec7: Section 2 defines fundamental terms including "act", "animal", "child" (below eighteen years), "death", "life", "gender" (including "transgender" as defined in the Transgender Persons (Protection of Rights) Act, 2019 per Explanation), and "Government". It delineates "dishonestly", "fraudulently", "good faith", "voluntarily", "reason to believe", and "counterfeit", providing Explanations that exact imitation is not essential for counterfeiting and that a presumption of intent applies if deception is likely. The section specifies "document" (including electronic/digital records and mercantile usage via Explanations 1 and 2), "valuable security", "movable property", "Judge", "person", and "public servant" (including those regardless of appointment or legal defect per Explanations, and defining "election"). Finally, it defines "injury", "illegal", "local law", "special law", and "offence", noting that for specific provisions, "offence" extends to special or local laws punishable with imprisonment for a term of six months or more, while incorporating definitions from the Information Technology Act, 2000 and Bharatiya Nagarik Suraksha Sanhita, 2023.
BNS_S36:313577cf2d168aa80f79761fb07b7913: Section 36 stipulates that when an act which would otherwise be a certain offence is not that offence by reason of the youth, the want of maturity of understanding, the unsoundness of mind, the intoxication, or any misconception on the part of the person doing that act, every person has the same right of private defence against that act as they would if the act were that offence. This provision is supported by Illustrations involving an attempt to kill by a person of unsound mind and an attack resulting from a misconception.
BNS_S44:faa69615696af1e3437139b5d37b18fd: Section 44 stipulates that when exercising the right of private defence against an assault which reasonably causes the apprehension of death, the right extends to the running of risk of harm to an innocent person if the defender cannot effectually exercise that right without such risk. An illustration clarifies that a person attacked by a mob attempting murder commits no offence if, in exercising private defence by firing, they harm children mingled with the mob.
BNS_S1:3e83babf7d9479d54f768f14d899356fd: The Bharatiya Nyaya Sanhita, 2023, shall come into force on a date appointed by the Central Government via notification in the Official Gazette, with different dates potentially appointed for different provisions. Every person is liable for acts or omissions contrary to its provisions within India, and persons liable under any law in force in India for offences committed beyond India shall be dealt with as if such acts were committed within India. The Sanhita applies to any citizen of India in any place without and beyond India, any person on any ship or aircraft registered in India wherever it may be, and any person in any place without and beyond India targeting a computer resource located in India, where the Explanation clarifies that “offence” includes every act committed outside India which, if committed in India, would be punishable under this Sanhita. This Sanhita does not affect provisions for punishing mutiny and desertion of officers, soldiers, sailors or airmen in the service of the Government of India or the provisions of any special or local law.
BNS_S32:80b9bc8160ff9585f16aa0f7bf51f0be: Except murder and offences against the State punishable with death, nothing is an offence if done by a person compelled by threats that, at the time of doing it, reasonably cause the apprehension that instant death to that person will otherwise be the consequence. The Proviso stipulates that the person must not have, of his own accord or from a reasonable apprehension of harm to himself short of instant death, placed himself in the situation by which he became subject to such constraint. Explanation 1 clarifies that a person who joins a gang of dacoits of his own accord or by reason of a threat of being beaten is not entitled to the benefit, whereas Explanation 2 states that a person seized by a gang of dacoits and forced, by threat of instant death, to do a thing which is an offence by law is entitled to the benefit of this exception.
