If the person is under sentence of death, the punishment is imprisonment for life or imprisonment of either description for a term not exceeding ten years, and shall also be liable to fine.
BNS_S285:3ba0abe2b266d7616d62e0d30b4afb8: Section 285 penalizes whoever, by doing any act or omitting to take order with any property in his possession or under his charge, causes danger, obstruction or injury to any person in any public way or public line of navigation. The offence is punishable with fine which may extend to five thousand rupees. This section contains no Provisos, Exceptions, or Explanations.
BNS_S289:2203ce4f0f0e2461e5de499d5056a86: Section 289 penalizes whoever does any act with any machinery so rashly or negligently as to endanger human life or be likely to cause hurt or injury to any other person. It also covers whoever knowingly or negligently omits to take such order with any machinery in his possession or under his care as is sufficient to guard against any probable danger to human life. Punishment consists of imprisonment of either description for a term which may extend to six months, or with fine which may extend to five thousand rupees, or with both.
BNS_S269:6ccf55828e57914dfa5b84871193cf2: Whoever, having been charged with an offence and released on bail bond or on bond, fails without sufficient cause (the burden of proving which shall lie upon him) to appear in Court in accordance with the terms of the bail or bond, shall be punished with imprisonment of either description for a term which may extend to one year, or with fine, or with both. The Explanation clarifies that this punishment is (a) in addition to the punishment to which the offender would be liable on a conviction for the offence with which he has been charged, and (b) without prejudice to the power of the Court to order forfeiture of the bond.
BNS_S287:f8e0cea733ea68928a9da522343b065: Section 287 applies to whoever, with fire or any combustible matter, performs any act so rashly or negligently as to endanger human life or be likely to cause hurt or injury to any other person. The offence further includes whoever knowingly or negligently omits to take such order with any fire or any combustible matter in his possession as is sufficient to guard against any probable danger to human life. Such person shall be punished with imprisonment of either description for a term which may extend to six months, or with fine which may extend to two thousand rupees, or with both.
BNS_S293:2f54dd98f81fd50beaa5b918aa4e106: Section 293 penalizes whoever repeats or continues a public nuisance after having been enjoined by any public servant who has lawful authority to issue such injunction not to repeat or continue such nuisance. The punishment is simple imprisonment for a term which may extend to six months, or with fine which may extend to five thousand rupees, or with both.
BNS_S253:2fe117bf92fad8138d4d11fa0e6fc5a: Section 253 punishes any person who, knowing of an escape from lawful custody or an order for apprehension by a public servant, harbours or conceals a person convicted of or charged with an offence with the intention of preventing apprehension. Penalties include: (a) if the offence is punishable with death, imprisonment of either description for a term which may extend to seven years, and shall also be liable to fine; (b) if punishable with imprisonment for life or imprisonment for ten years, imprisonment of either description for a term which may extend to three years, with or without fine; and (c) if punishable with imprisonment which may extend to one year and not to ten years, imprisonment of the description provided for the offence for a term which may extend to one-fourth part of the longest term of the imprisonment provided for such offence, or with fine, or with both. The Explanation clarifies that “offence” includes any act or omission committed outside India which would be punishable in India and for which the person is, under any law relating to extradition, or otherwise, liable to be apprehended or detained in custody in India. An Exception states that the provisions do not extend to the case in which the harbour or concealment is by the spouse of the person to be apprehended.
BNS_S291:f5ba70428f1ba2965666fd40cf73849: Section 291 addresses negligent conduct with respect to animal, occurring when whoever knowingly or negligently omits to take sufficient measures with any animal in his possession to guard against any probable danger to human life, or any probable danger of grievous hurt from such animal. The offence is punishable with imprisonment of either description for a term which may extend to six months, or with fine which may extend to five thousand rupees, or with both.
BNS_S297:ff909410985d60ed13e9659c685a391: Section 297(1) stipulates that whoever keeps any office or place for the purpose of drawing any lottery, excluding a State lottery or a lottery authorised by the State Government, shall be punished with imprisonment of either description for a term which may extend to six months, or with fine, or with both. Under Section 297(2), whoever publishes any proposal to pay any sum, deliver any goods, or do or forbear from doing anything for the benefit of any person on any event or contingency relative or applicable to the drawing of any ticket, lot, number or figure in any such lottery, shall be punished with fine which may extend to five thousand rupees.
BNS_S299:c96c9e8b44e00719ddc2207d926d9fc: Whoever, with deliberate and malicious intention of outraging the religious feelings of any class of citizens of India, insults or attempts to insult the religion or the religious beliefs of that class by words, either spoken or written, or by signs or by visible representations or through electronic means or otherwise, shall be punished. The prescribed punishment is imprisonment of either description for a term which may extend to three years, or with fine, or with both.
BNS_S305:2f4bed83d73873c12ecbe636fd8be52: Section 305 penalizes whoever commits theft in any building, tent or vessel used as a human dwelling or used for the custody of property, or of any means of transport used for the transport of goods or passengers, or of any article or goods from such means of transport. The offence further includes the theft of an idol or icon in any place of worship, or any property of the Government or of a local authority. Punishment consists of imprisonment of either description for a term which may extend to seven years, and the offender shall also be liable to fine.
BNS_S307:0a8b504713286d0bdfb95dcc14bc6d8: Section 307 defines the offence of theft after preparation made for causing death, hurt or restraint in order to committing of theft. It applies to whoever commits theft having made preparation for causing death, or hurt, or restraint, or fear of death, or of hurt, or of restraint, to any person in order to the committing of such theft, the effecting of his escape after the committing of such theft, or the retaining of property taken by such theft. The penalty is rigorous imprisonment for a term which may extend to ten years, and the offender shall also be liable to fine. The section includes Illustrations to clarify the application of these conditions.
BNS_S306:1fb58072d8af02e0dcf14181210a6a2: Section 306 pertains to a person being a clerk or servant, or being employed in the capacity of a clerk or servant, committing theft in respect of any property in the possession of his master or employer. The offender shall be punished with imprisonment of either description for a term which may extend to seven years, and shall also be liable to fine.
BNS_S303:d109f08e9b793ab39c8f93240fc09e1: Theft is committed by whoever, intending to take dishonestly any movable property out of the possession of any person without that person’s consent, moves that property in order to such taking, subject to Explanations regarding property severed from the earth, movement via removing obstacles or causing animals to move, and express or implied consent. A person shall be punished with imprisonment of either description for a term which may extend to three years, or with fine, or with both, while second or subsequent convictions mandate rigorous imprisonment for a term which shall not be less than one year but which may extend to five years and with fine. A Proviso stipulates that for first-time convictions where the value of the stolen property is less than five thousand rupees, upon return of the value of property or restoration of the stolen property, the person shall be punished with community service.
BNS_S313:ba7b9d3a627786865c09d437b681058: Section 313 prescribes punishment for whoever belongs to any gang of persons associated in habitually committing theft or robbery, specifically excluding those who are a gang of dacoits. Such individuals shall be punished with rigorous imprisonment for a term which may extend to seven years, and shall also be liable to fine.
BNS_S319:19d6d808dd3b9724f5cea7c7ecab2fe: Section 319 defines "Cheating by personation" as cheating by pretending to be some other person, knowingly substituting one person for another, or representing that he or any other person is a person other than he or such other person really is. An Explanation specifies that the offence is committed whether the individual personated is a real or imaginary person. Whoever cheats by personation shall be punished with imprisonment of either description for a term which may extend to five years, or with fine, or with both.
BNS_S315:039d1ea058961f783db827831b40240: Section 315 penalizes whoever dishonestly misappropriates or converts to his own use any property, knowing it was in the possession of a deceased person at the time of that person’s decease and has not since been in the possession of any person legally entitled to such possession. The punishment is imprisonment of either description for a term which may extend to three years, and the offender shall also be liable to fine. If the offender was employed by the deceased as a clerk or servant at the time of such person’s decease, the imprisonment may extend to seven years.
BNS_S311:91626de586b7bf7be5bb7764f634078: Section 311 stipulates that if an offender, at the time of committing robbery or dacoity, uses any deadly weapon, causes grievous hurt to any person, or attempts to cause death or grievous hurt to any person, specific penal consequences apply. The imprisonment with which such offender shall be punished shall not be less than seven years.
BNS_S295:b4ef755eb50c735b7cf568df928eac2: Whoever sells, lets to hire, distributes, exhibits or circulates to any child any such obscene object as is referred to in section 294, or offers or attempts so to do, shall be punished on first conviction with imprisonment of either description for a term which may extend to three years, and with fine which may extend to two thousand rupees. In the event of a second or subsequent conviction, the punishment is imprisonment of either description for a term which may extend to seven years, and also with fine which may extend to five thousand rupees.
BNS_S321:7f636392f8fa9a038bae851e70631da: Section 321 penalizes whoever dishonestly or fraudulently prevents any debt or demand due to himself or to any other person from being made available according to law for payment of his debts or the debts of such other person. The offence is punishable with imprisonment of either description for a term which may extend to two years, or with fine, or with both. This section contains no Provisos, Exceptions, or Explanations.
BNS_S325:1b4b7df99c550e72d2e0df2dbcb04d8: Section 325 stipulates that whoever commits mischief by killing, poisoning, maiming or rendering useless any animal shall be punished. The prescribed penalty is imprisonment of either description for a term which may extend to five years, or with fine, or with both.
BNS_S323:67f969aabf143fb1be8bf7b2b199959: Section 323 applies to whoever dishonestly or fraudulently conceals or removes any property of himself or any other person, or dishonestly or fraudulently assists in the concealment or removal thereof. The offence also encompasses individuals who dishonestly release any demand or claim to which they are entitled. Punishment for these acts is imprisonment of either description for a term which may extend to three years, or with fine, or with both.
BNS_S320:eca94244464bc51d38971ee9cc8d776: Section 320 applies to any person who dishonestly or fraudulently removes, conceals, delivers to any person, or transfers or causes to be transferred without adequate consideration any property. The offence requires the intent to prevent, or knowledge that it is likely to prevent, the distribution of that property according to law among his creditors or the creditors of any other person. Punishment is imprisonment of either description for a term which shall not be less than six months but which may extend to two years, or with fine, or with both.
BNS_S329:637e3d07efc7046983eb464ec6be3bf: Criminal trespass occurs when a person enters or unlawfully remains upon property in the possession of another with intent to commit an offence or to intimidate, insult or annoy any person in possession of such property. House-trespass is committed when criminal trespass occurs by entering or remaining in any building, tent or vessel used as a human dwelling, or any building used as a place for worship or for the custody of property, with an Explanation stating that the introduction of any part of the criminal trespasser’s body constitutes entering. Criminal trespass is punishable with imprisonment of either description for a term which may extend to three months, or with fine which may extend to five thousand rupees, or with both. House-trespass is punishable with imprisonment of either description for a term which may extend to one year, or with fine which may extend to five thousand rupees, or with both.
BNS_S309:b2615f858c6982417df0c1152477cc9: Robbery is defined as either theft, where the offender voluntarily causes or attempts to cause death, hurt, wrongful restraint, or fear of instant death, instant hurt, or instant wrongful restraint to commit or carry away property, or extortion, where the offender is in the presence of the person—defined by the Explanation as being sufficiently near to put the person in fear of instant death, instant hurt, or instant wrongful restraint—and induces the person then and there to deliver up the thing extorted by putting them in fear of instant death, instant hurt, or instant wrongful restraint to that person or another. Whoever commits robbery shall be punished with rigorous imprisonment for a term which may extend to ten years and shall also be liable to fine, or if committed on the highway between sunset and sunrise, the imprisonment may be extended to fourteen years; an attempt to commit robbery is punishable with rigorous imprisonment for a term which may extend to seven years and a fine. If any person, or any other person jointly concerned in committing or attempting to commit robbery, voluntarily causes hurt, they shall be punished with imprisonment for life, or with rigorous imprisonment for a term which may extend to ten years, and shall also be liable to fine.
BNS_S333:179a7d3930d1abac6ce23fc0c05daf4: Section 333 of the Bharatiya Nyaya Sanhita, 2023, addresses house-trespass after preparation for hurt, assault or wrongful restraint. The offence is committed by whoever commits house-trespass, having made preparation for causing hurt to any person, for assaulting any person, for wrongfully restraining any person, or for putting any person in fear of hurt, of assault, or of wrongful restraint. Such person shall be punished with imprisonment of either description for a term which may extend to seven years, and shall also be liable to fine.
BNS_S332:3e07818aa02af125ef04522b28aed7a: Section 332 penalizes whoever commits house-trespass in order to the committing of any offence. Punishments are categorized as: (a) if the offence is punishable with death, imprisonment for life or rigorous imprisonment for a term not exceeding ten years and fine; (b) if punishable with imprisonment for life, imprisonment of either description for a term not exceeding ten years and fine; and (c) if punishable with imprisonment, imprisonment of either description for a term which may extend to two years and fine. A Proviso specifies that if the offence intended to be committed is theft, the term of the imprisonment may be extended to seven years.
BNS_S338:bfef4e191d7ffd12312a403b52423d5: Section 338 penalizes whoever forges a document purporting to be a valuable security, a will, an authority to adopt a son, or an authority to make or transfer any valuable security, or to receive the principal, interest or dividends thereon, or to receive or deliver any money, movable property, or valuable security. It further encompasses the forgery of any document purporting to be an acquittance or receipt acknowledging the payment of money, or an acquittance or receipt for the delivery of any movable property or valuable security. The offence is punishable with imprisonment for life, or with imprisonment of either description for a term which may extend to ten years, and shall also be liable to fine.
BNS_S335:ca0466449ab25ac17f8351fa5b9af36: Section 335 defines making a false document or false electronic record as dishonestly or fraudulently making, signing, sealing, executing, transmitting, or affixing an electronic signature or mark of authenticity to cause belief that it was authorized by another person. It further encompasses the unauthorized, dishonest, or fraudulent alteration of any material part of a document or electronic record, or causing a person to sign, seal, execute, alter, or affix an electronic signature knowing they cannot or do not understand the contents due to deception, intoxication, or unsoundness of mind. The section includes Explanation 1 regarding a person's own signature amounting to forgery, Explanation 2 regarding the use of names of fictitious or deceased persons, and Explanation 3 defining “affixing electronic signature” per clause (d) of sub-section (1) of section 2 of the Information Technology Act, 2000.
BNS_S341:6f535bc1258786b644c09f5fa6ccc4b: Section 341 prescribes punishments for whoever makes, counterfeits, or possesses (knowing it to be counterfeit) any seal, plate, or other instrument for making an impression with intent to commit forgery: if punishable under section 338, the penalty is imprisonment for life, or imprisonment of either description for a term which may extend to seven years, and fine; if punishable under any section of this Chapter other than section 338, the penalty is imprisonment of either description for a term which may extend to seven years, and fine. Whoever possesses any seal, plate, or other instrument knowing the same to be counterfeit shall be punished with imprisonment of either description for a term which may extend to three years, and shall also be liable to fine. Additionally, whoever fraudulently or dishonestly uses as genuine any seal, plate, or other instrument knowing or having reason to believe the same to be counterfeit shall be punished in the same manner as if he had made or counterfeited such seal, plate, or other instrument.
BNS_S343:4b7bba8e449b885458e93fbb71f3532: Section 343 penalizes whoever fraudulently or dishonestly, or with intent to cause damage or injury to the public or to any person, cancels, destroys or defaces, or attempts to cancel, destroy or deface, or secretes or attempts to secrete any document which is or purports to be a will, or an authority to adopt a son, or any valuable security, or commits mischief in respect of such document. The punishment for this offence is imprisonment for life, or with imprisonment of either description for a term which may extend to seven years, and shall also be liable to fine.
BNS_S317:dc16e155fdd44ea51ac28a71d66fac7: Stolen property is defined as property transferred by theft, extortion, robbery, or cheating, or property criminally misappropriated or subject to criminal breach of trust (whether committed within or without India), though an exception applies if such property subsequently comes into the possession of a person legally entitled to the possession thereof. Dishonestly receiving or retaining stolen property, knowing or having reason to believe it is stolen, is punishable by imprisonment of either description for a term which may extend to three years, or with fine, or with both; however, receiving property transferred by dacoity, or from a person known or believed to belong to a gang of dacoits, is punishable by imprisonment for life, or with rigorous imprisonment for a term which may extend to ten years, and shall also be liable to fine. Habitually receiving or dealing in stolen property is punishable by imprisonment for life, or with imprisonment of either description for a term which may extend to ten years, and shall also be liable to fine, while voluntarily assisting in concealing, disposing of, or making away with property known or believed to be stolen is punishable by imprisonment of either description for a term which may extend to three years, or with fine, or with both.
BNS_S339:a130804c897bbdd49df844b31363859: Section 339 applies to whoever has in his possession any document or electronic record, knowing the same to be forged and intending that the same shall fraudulently or dishonestly be used as genuine. If the document or electronic record is of the description mentioned in section 337, the offender shall be punished with imprisonment of either description for a term which may extend to seven years, and shall also be liable to fine. If the document is of the description mentioned in section 338, the offender shall be punished with imprisonment for life, or with imprisonment of either description, for a term which may extend to seven years, and shall also be liable to fine.
BNS_S347:3e72924082b7c95f2f85d836c119328: Section 347(1) stipulates that whoever counterfeits any property mark used by any other person shall be punished with imprisonment of either description for a term which may extend to two years, or with fine, or with both. Section 347(2) penalizes whoever counterfeits any property mark used by a public servant, or any mark used by a public servant to denote manufacture by a particular person or at a particular time or place, particular quality, passage through a particular office, or entitlement to any exemption, or uses as genuine any such mark knowing the same to be counterfeit. Such offences under subsection (2) are punishable with imprisonment of either description for a term which may extend to three years, and shall also be liable to fine.
BNS_S349:0610e758e407aee17d9e591113b1671: Section 349 penalizes whoever sells, exposes, or has in possession for sale any goods or things with a counterfeit property mark affixed to or impressed upon the goods or any case, package, or other receptacle containing them. Liability is avoided if the actor proves they took all reasonable precautions and had no reason to suspect the genuineness of the mark, provided all information in their power regarding the source upon demand by or on behalf of the prosecutor, or otherwise acted innocently. Punishment consists of imprisonment of either description for a term which may extend to one year, or with fine, or with both.
BNS_S355:ed8118a70e75a03c68ab4ccba13569c: Section 355 penalizes "Misconduct in public by a drunken person," specifically whoever, in a state of intoxication, appears in any public place or any place which it is a trespass in him to enter, and conducts himself in such a manner as to cause annoyance to any person. The punishment is simple imprisonment for a term which may extend to twenty-four hours, or with fine which may extend to one thousand rupees, or with both or with community service. This section contains no Provisos, Exceptions, or Explanations.
BNSS_S5:d00a41df18cd0a5af6d916f6a40194a8: Section 5 (Saving) stipulates that, in the absence of a specific provision to the contrary, nothing contained in this Sanhita shall affect any special or local law for the time being in force. This preservation extends to any special jurisdiction, power conferred, or special form of procedure prescribed by any other law for the time being in force.
BNS_S337:b721732f9dd11b422f6ab585a5576fe: Whoever forges a document or an electronic record purporting to be a record or proceeding of or in a Court, an identity document issued by Government including voter identity card or Aadhaar Card, a register of birth, marriage or burial, a register kept by a public servant as such, a certificate or document purporting to be made by a public servant in his official capacity, an authority to institute or defend a suit, to take any proceedings therein, to confess judgment, or a power of attorney, shall be punished with imprisonment of either description for a term which may extend to seven years, and shall also be liable to fine. An Explanation clarifies that “register” includes any list, data or record of any entries maintained in the electronic form as defined in clause (r) of sub-section (1) of section 2 of the Information Technology Act, 2000 (21 of 2000).
BNS_S348:988a297f60e0ed5f76eabdaf9670b2e: Section 348 penalizes whoever makes or has in his possession any die, plate or other instrument for the purpose of counterfeiting a property mark, or has in his possession a property mark for the purpose of denoting that any goods belong to a person to whom they do not belong. Such persons shall be punished with imprisonment of either description for a term which may extend to three years, or with fine, or with both.
BNSS_S1:14841565603f63d2abf7b1813657eb52: The Act is titled the Bharatiya Nagarik Suraksha Sanhita, 2023. Provisions other than Chapters IX, XI and XII shall not apply to the State of Nagaland or tribal areas, unless the State Government, by notification, applies them with supplemental, incidental or consequential modifications. An Explanation defines “tribal areas” as territories included in the tribal areas of Assam per paragraph 20 of the Sixth Schedule to the Constitution immediately before the 21st day of January, 1972, excluding the municipality of Shillong. The Sanhita shall come into force on a date appointed by the Central Government via notification in the Official Gazette.
BNSS_S3:839c721b099550afc672df106eb1af12: Section 3 mandates that, unless the context otherwise requires, any reference in any law to a Magistrate, Magistrate of the first class, or Magistrate of the second class shall be construed as a reference to a Judicial Magistrate of the first class or Judicial Magistrate of the second class exercising jurisdiction in such area. For functions under laws other than this Sanhita, matters involving the appreciation or shifting of evidence or the formulation of decisions exposing a person to punishment, penalty, or detention in custody pending investigation, inquiry, or trial shall, subject to the provisions of this Sanhita, be exercisable by a Judicial Magistrate. Matters administrative or executive in nature, such as the granting, suspension, or cancellation of a licence or sanctioning or withdrawing from a prosecution, shall, subject to the provisions of clause (a), be exercisable by an Executive Magistrate.
BNSS_S9:29cfa1f3864133f06dcaea7fd7699e53: The State Government, after consultation with the High Court, shall establish Courts of Judicial Magistrates of the first class and of the second class in every district via notification. A Proviso allows the State Government, after consultation with the High Court, to establish Special Courts of Judicial Magistrates of the first class or of the second class for any particular case or particular class of cases, granting such Special Courts exclusive jurisdiction in the local area. The High Court appoints the presiding officers of these Courts and may confer the powers of a Judicial Magistrate of the first class or of the second class on any member of the Judicial Service of the State functioning as a Judge in a Civil Court.
BNSS_S11:c6aa2ca6469f0954ed2a3b202db08d1: Upon request by the Central or State Government, the High Court may confer all or any powers conferred or conferrable under this Sanhita on a Judicial Magistrate of the first class or of the second class upon any person who holds or has held any post under the Government in respect to particular cases or particular classes of cases in any local area. A Proviso stipulates that no such power shall be conferred unless the person possesses such qualification or experience in relation to legal affairs as the High Court may, by rules, specify. These individuals, called Special Judicial Magistrates, shall be appointed by the High Court by general or special order for a term not exceeding one year at a time.
BNS_S331:fa6a174a2bd57ae5460b20883026957: Section 331 mandates imprisonment of either description for a term which may extend to two years and fine for lurking house-trespass or house-breaking, or a term which may extend to three years and fine if committed after sunset and before sunrise. If committed to commit an offence punishable with imprisonment, punishments extend to a term which may extend to three years (or ten years if theft), or a term which may extend to five years (or fourteen years if theft) if committed after sunset and before sunrise; furthermore, preparations for causing hurt, assaulting, wrongfully restraining, or putting any person in fear of hurt, assault, or wrongful restraint carry imprisonment of either description or a term which may extend to ten years, or fourteen years if after sunset and before sunrise, and fine. Causing grievous hurt or attempting to cause death or grievous hurt warrants imprisonment for life or imprisonment of either description for a term which may extend to ten years and fine; if, during lurking house-trespass or house-breaking after sunset and before sunrise, any person voluntarily causes or attempts to cause death or grievous hurt, every person jointly concerned shall be punished with imprisonment for life or imprisonment of either description for a term which may extend to ten years and fine.
BNSS_S12:aa8e7eb0c036f469bc0f8b9ab0fd692: Subject to the control of the High Court, the Chief Judicial Magistrate may define the local limits within which Magistrates appointed under section 9 or under section 11 exercise powers under this Sanhita; provided that the Court of Special Judicial Magistrate may hold its sitting at any place within the local area for which it is established. Absent such definition, the jurisdiction and powers of every such Magistrate shall extend throughout the district. Where the local jurisdiction of a Magistrate appointed under section 9 or section 11 extends beyond the district in which he ordinarily holds Court, references to the Court of Session or Chief Judicial Magistrate shall be construed as those exercising jurisdiction in relation to the said district, unless the context otherwise requires.
BNSS_S16:31c00e6be434ffd3bd4618545e7a9e1: Subject to the control of the State Government, the District Magistrate may define the local limits of the areas within which Executive Magistrates may exercise all or any of the powers invested under this Sanhita. Except as otherwise provided by such definition, the jurisdiction and powers of every such Magistrate shall extend throughout the district.
BNSS_S7:c18313554c750860efacf310bfd06d22: Every State shall be a sessions division or consist of sessions divisions, where every sessions division shall be a district or consist of districts. The State Government, after consultation with the High Court, may alter the limits or the number of such divisions and districts, and may divide any district into sub-divisions and alter the limits or the number of such sub-divisions. All sessions divisions, districts, and sub-divisions existing in a State at the commencement of this Sanhita shall be deemed to have been formed under this section.
BNS_S327:2399638979477cf2fc3a0f3ee94d09e: Subsection (1) states that whoever commits mischief to any rail, aircraft, or a decked vessel or any vessel of a burden of twenty tons or upwards, intending to destroy or render unsafe, or knowing it to be likely that he will thereby destroy or render unsafe, shall be punished with imprisonment of either description for a term which may extend to ten years, and shall also be liable to fine. Subsection (2) states that whoever commits, or attempts to commit, by fire or any explosive substance, such mischief as is described in sub-section (1), shall be punished with imprisonment for life or with imprisonment of either description for a term which may extend to ten years, and shall also be liable to fine.
BNS_S351:771ed77c4d6395f3a498929a86ed1fe: Criminal intimidation is committed by threatening another's person, reputation, or property, or those in whom they are interested, to cause alarm or compel an act or omission to avoid the threat, with an Explanation that includes threats to the reputation of a deceased person in whom the threatened person is interested. The offence is punishable with imprisonment of either description for a term which may extend to two years, or fine, or both. If the threat involves causing death, grievous hurt, destruction of property by fire, an offence punishable with death, imprisonment for life, or imprisonment for a term which may extend to seven years, or imputing unchastity to a woman, the punishment is imprisonment of either description for a term which may extend to seven years, or fine, or both. Criminal intimidation via anonymous communication or by concealing the name or abode of the person carries a punishment of imprisonment of either description for a term which may extend to two years, in addition to the punishment provided for the offence under sub-section (1).
BNSS_S15:214d17d13bc453db0e1c5cbf1ccfee9: The State Government may appoint, for such term as it may think fit, Executive Magistrates or any police officer not below the rank of Superintendent of Police or equivalent, to be known as Special Executive Magistrates. Such appointments are made for particular areas or for the performance of particular functions. The State Government may confer on such Special Executive Magistrates such of the powers as are conferrable under this Sanhita on Executive Magistrates, as it may deem fit.
BNS_S342:1ba78092dfd71287672679c06669e1a: Section 342(1) punishes whoever counterfeits upon, or in the substance of, any material, any device or mark used for authenticating any document described in section 338, intending that such device or mark shall be used for the purpose of giving the appearance of authenticity to any document then forged or thereafter to be forged on such material, or who possesses such material with such intent, with imprisonment for life, or with imprisonment of either description for a term which may extend to seven years, and shall also be liable to fine. Section 342(2) punishes whoever counterfeits upon, or in the substance of, any material, any device or mark used for authenticating any document or electronic record other than the documents described in section 338, intending that such device or mark shall be used for the purpose of giving the appearance of authenticity to any document then forged or thereafter to be forged on such material, or who possesses such material with such intent, with imprisonment of either description for a term which may extend to seven years, and shall also be liable to fine.
BNSS_S14:e1580cbf0397e695643f69f494ccb50: The State Government may appoint Executive Magistrates in every district, designating one as the District Magistrate and others potentially as Additional District Magistrates with powers as directed. An officer temporarily succeeding to the executive administration of a district shall exercise all powers and perform all duties of the District Magistrate pending State Government orders, while the State Government may appoint and relieve a Sub-divisional Magistrate in charge of a sub-division. The State Government may delegate its powers under sub-section (4) to the District Magistrate via general or special order and may confer all or any powers of an Executive Magistrate on a Commissioner of Police under any law for the time being in force.
BNS_S353:874fc32b2775d2f65582654d8b2d60f: Section 353(1) punishes making, publishing, or circulating any statement, false information, rumour, or report, including through electronic means, with intent to or likely to cause an officer, soldier, sailor, or airman in the Army, Navy, or Air Force of India to mutiny or otherwise disregard or fail in his duty; cause fear or alarm to the public to induce offences against the State or public tranquillity; or incite any class or community to commit offences against another class or community, with imprisonment which may extend to three years, or with fine, or with both. Sub-section (2) prescribes imprisonment which may extend to three years, or with fine, or with both, for circulating false information, rumour, or alarming news, including through electronic means, to promote enmity, hatred, or ill will on grounds of religion, race, place of birth, residence, language, caste, community, or any other ground, while sub-section (3) mandates imprisonment which may extend to five years and shall also be liable to fine if such an offence is committed in any place of worship or assembly engaged in religious worship or ceremonies. An Exception is provided if the person has reasonable grounds for believing the statement, false information, rumour, or report is true and makes, publishes, or circulates it in good faith and without the specified intent.
BNSS_S22:f2e75ceae73a0353c69c5c5fb9c9660: A High Court may pass any sentence authorised by law. A Sessions Judge or Additional Sessions Judge may also pass any sentence authorised by law, provided that any sentence of death passed by such Judge shall be subject to confirmation by the High Court.
BNSS_S13:97380b2db660d0b184040898838ea9a: Every Chief Judicial Magistrate shall be subordinate to the Sessions Judge, and every other Judicial Magistrate shall, subject to the general control of the Sessions Judge, be subordinate to the Chief Judicial Magistrate. Additionally, the Chief Judicial Magistrate may, from time to time, make rules or give special orders, consistent with this Sanhita, as to the distribution of business among the Judicial Magistrates subordinate to him.
BNS_S357:e000bb2c80b32e9cd3225f5e337eabf: Section 357 penalizes any person who, being bound by a lawful contract to attend on or to supply the wants of any person who, by reason of youth, or of unsoundness of mind, or of a disease or bodily weakness, is helpless or incapable of providing for his own safety or of supplying his own wants, voluntarily omits so to do. The punishment is imprisonment of either description for a term which may extend to three months, or with fine which may extend to five thousand rupees, or with both.
BNSS_S19:d87559822130dc18df8909d58ab5a53: The State Government shall appoint one or more Assistant Public Prosecutors in every district for conducting prosecutions in the Courts of Magistrates, while the Central Government may appoint one or more Assistant Public Prosecutors for conducting any case or class of cases in the Courts of Magistrates. Where no Assistant Public Prosecutor is available for a particular case, the District Magistrate may appoint any other person as the Assistant Public Prosecutor in charge of that case after giving notice of fourteen days to the State Government. A Proviso stipulates that no police officer is eligible for such appointment if they have taken part in the investigation into the offence with respect to which the accused is being prosecuted or are below the rank of Inspector.
BNSS_S27:8af51a17fc699f3bdaed5ca49f19a00: Section 27 provides that a person holding an office in the service of Government, invested by the High Court or the State Government with powers under this Sanhita throughout any local area, shall exercise the same powers if appointed to an equal or higher office of the same nature within a like local area under the same State Government. This occurs unless the High Court or the State Government, as the case may be, otherwise directs or has otherwise directed.
BNSS_S30:dfdb32766197605979c81a87b81118d: Section 30 provides that police officers superior in rank to an officer in charge of a police station may exercise the same powers as may be exercised by such officer within the limits of his station. This authority is applicable throughout the local area to which the superior officer is appointed.
BNSS_S17:251ae68ed390220a2d19c095f78cbf8: All Executive Magistrates shall be subordinate to the District Magistrate, and every Executive Magistrate, with the exception of the Sub-divisional Magistrate, exercising powers in a sub-division shall also be subordinate to the Sub-divisional Magistrate, subject to the general control of the District Magistrate. Additionally, the District Magistrate may, from time to time, make rules or give special orders, consistent with this Sanhita, as to the distribution or allocation of business among the Executive Magistrates subordinate to him.
BNS_S345:76b8c1a6e4e6118623804400e648578: A property mark is a mark used for denoting that movable property belongs to a particular person. Using a false property mark involves marking movable property, goods, or any case, package or other receptacle containing them, or using any case, package or other receptacle having any mark thereon, in a manner reasonably calculated to cause it to be believed that the property or goods belong to a person to whom they do not belong. Subject to an exception where the actor proves he acted without intent to defraud, the punishment for using any false property mark is imprisonment of either description for a term which may extend to one year, or with fine, or with both.
BNSS_S31:861fe80a8ca9226973685d83e0d94a9: Every person is bound to assist a Magistrate or police officer who is reasonably demanding his aid. This assistance is required in the taking or preventing the escape of any other person whom such Magistrate or police officer is authorised to arrest, in the prevention or suppression of a breach of the peace, or in the prevention of any injury attempted to be committed to any public property.
BNSS_S26:d522b2f7b44a57854df44ac82e2ac13: Under Section 26, the High Court or the State Government may, by order, confer powers under this Sanhita by empowering persons specially by name, in virtue of their offices, or classes of officials generally by their official titles. Every such order shall take effect from the date on which it is communicated to the person so empowered.
BNSS_S21:a364a756904a4ad10d1ca38c48e73ab: Subject to other provisions of this Sanhita, offences under the Bharatiya Nyaya Sanhita, 2023 may be tried by the High Court, the Court of Session, or any other Court shown in the First Schedule to be triable. A proviso stipulates that any offence under section 64, section 65, section 66, section 67, section 68, section 69, section 70, or section 71 of the Bharatiya Nyaya Sanhita, 2023 shall be tried as far as practicable by a Court presided over by a woman. Offences under any other law shall be tried by the Court mentioned in such law, or if no Court is so mentioned, by the High Court or any other Court shown in the First Schedule to be triable.
BNSS_S38:0e35dda4aeed07b045a4c21ec633e62: Section 38 stipulates that any person arrested and interrogated by the police shall be entitled to meet an advocate of his choice during interrogation. This entitlement is granted during interrogation, though specifically not throughout interrogation.
BNSS_S29:a306a03fb55e985c47321f5359494dc: Subject to the other provisions of this Sanhita, the powers and duties of a Judge or Magistrate may be exercised or performed by his successor-in-office. If there is doubt as to who the successor-in-office is, the Sessions Judge shall determine by order in writing the Judge deemed to be the successor-in-office for the purposes of this Sanhita or any proceedings or order thereunder. Where doubt exists regarding the successor-in-office of any Magistrate, the Chief Judicial Magistrate, or the District Magistrate, as the case may be, shall determine by order in writing the Magistrate deemed to be the successor-in-office for the purposes of this Sanhita or any proceedings or order thereunder.
BNSS_S33:88cd51a14865ee9b6088825ab01c613: Under Section 33(1), every person aware of the commission of, or the intention of any other person to commit, specific offences under the Bharatiya Nyaya Sanhita, 2023, must forthwith give information to the nearest Magistrate or police officer. These offences include sections 103 to 105 (both inclusive), 111 to 113 (both inclusive), 140 to 144 (both inclusive), 147 to 154 (both inclusive) and 158, 178 to 182 (both inclusive), 189 and 191, 274 to 280 (both inclusive), 307, 309 to 312 (both inclusive), sub-section (5) of section 316, 326 to 328 (both inclusive), and 331 and 332. Section 33(2) clarifies that the term “offence” includes any act committed at any place out of India which would constitute an offence if committed in India.
BNSS_S41:1c1a682b3028bfd6ec20094f8edb487: Under Section 41(1), an Executive or Judicial Magistrate may personally arrest or order the arrest of an offender if an offence is committed in his presence within his local jurisdiction, and may commit the offender to custody subject to bail provisions. Under Section 41(2), an Executive or Judicial Magistrate may arrest or direct the arrest, in his presence and within his local jurisdiction, of any person for whose arrest he is competent at the time and in the circumstances to issue a warrant.
BNSS_S45:1cd9a0ad8d226a87c15657e713b0abd: Section 45 empowers a police officer to pursue any person whom he is authorised to arrest into any place in India. This mechanism is specifically for the purpose of arresting without warrant. This section contains no Provisos, Exceptions, or Explanations.
BNSS_S23:04022d3964c55aa0ad9d911b9b81a2c: The Court of a Chief Judicial Magistrate may pass any sentence authorised by law except a sentence of death or of imprisonment for life or of imprisonment for a term exceeding seven years. The Court of a Magistrate of the first class may pass a sentence of imprisonment for a term not exceeding three years, or of fine not exceeding fifty thousand rupees, or of both, or of community service, whereas the Court of a Magistrate of the second class may pass a sentence of imprisonment for a term not exceeding one year, or of fine not exceeding ten thousand rupees, or of both, or of community service. An Explanation defines “community service” as work which the Court may order a convict to perform as a form of punishment that benefits the community, for which he shall not be entitled to any remuneration.
BNSS_S37:1bdd6661f890793deca08f77982a4b5: The State Government shall establish a police control room in every district and at State level and designate a police officer, not below the rank of Assistant Sub-Inspector of Police, in every district and in every police station. This officer is responsible for maintaining information about the names and addresses of the persons arrested and the nature of the offence with which charged. This information must be prominently displayed in any manner, including in digital mode, in every police station and at the district headquarters.
BNSS_S43:b48b040da4c4cf8191c64b0d81512ae: Arrest is effected by the police officer or other person actually touching or confining the body, unless submission occurs by word or action; a Proviso specifies that for women, submission on oral intimation is presumed, and touching is prohibited unless the police officer is female or circumstances otherwise require. If a person forcibly resists or attempts to evade arrest, "all means necessary" may be used, though there is no right to cause the death of a person not accused of an offence punishable with death or with imprisonment for life. Handcuffs may be used, considering the nature and gravity of the offence, for habitual or repeat offenders, those who escaped custody, or those committing organized crime, terrorist act, drug related crime, illegal possession of arms and ammunition, murder, rape, acid attack, counterfeiting of coins and currency-notes, human trafficking, sexual offence against children, or offence against the State. Save in exceptional circumstances, no woman shall be arrested after sunset and before sunrise, and in such cases, a woman police officer must obtain prior permission from the Magistrate of the first class via a written report.
BNSS_S47:d14a4359b40be97e8e0cce0413217bd: Under Section 47, every police officer or other person arresting any person without warrant shall forthwith communicate full particulars of the offence or other grounds for such arrest. Where a police officer arrests without warrant any person other than a person accused of a non-bailable offence, he shall inform the arrested person that he is entitled to be released on bail and may arrange for sureties on his behalf.
BNSS_S35:d31a7b0457b3de8192d50d86b71bc6f: Under Section 35(1), a police officer may arrest without a warrant or Magistrate's order any person committing a cognizable offence in their presence, proclaimed offenders, those in possession of suspected stolen property, those obstructing police duty or escaping lawful custody, suspected deserters from the Armed Forces of the Union, persons committing extraditable acts abroad, released convicts breaching section 394(5) rules, or those subject to a valid police requisition. For cognizable offences punishable with imprisonment for a term which may be less than seven years or which may extend to seven years, arrest requires "reason to believe" and specific necessity (preventing further offence, proper investigation, preventing evidence disappearance/tampering, preventing inducement/threat/promise, or ensuring Court presence), with a Proviso requiring written reasons for both making and not making the arrest; for offences punishable with imprisonment extending to more than seven years or death sentence, only "reason to believe" is required. Non-cognizable offences generally require a warrant or Magistrate's order (subject to section 39), and where arrest is not required under sub-section (1), a notice to appear must be issued, with subsequent arrest permitted only upon non-compliance or recorded necessity. Finally, prior permission from an officer not below the rank of Deputy Superintendent of Police is mandatory for arrests involving offences punishable for imprisonment of less than three years where the person is infirm or above sixty years of age.
BNSS_S39:b76df9284d4291dffc477f119cb106c: A police officer may arrest a person who, in their presence, has committed or is accused of committing a non-cognizable offence and refuses on demand to give their name and residence or provides a name or residence which the officer has reason to believe to be false. Upon ascertaining the true name and residence, the person shall be released on a bond or bail bond to appear before a Magistrate if so required, provided that if the person is not resident in India, the bail bond shall be secured by a surety or sureties resident in India. If the true name and residence is not ascertained within twenty-four hours from the time of arrest, or if the person fails to execute the bond or bail bond or furnish sufficient sureties, they shall forthwith be forwarded to the nearest Magistrate having jurisdiction.
BNSS_S42:ec5ca458215cb5524619e99def131ba: Notwithstanding anything contained in section 35 and sections 39 to 41 (both inclusive), no member of the Armed Forces of the Union shall be arrested for anything done or purported to be done in the discharge of his official duties except after obtaining the consent of the Central Government. Additionally, the State Government may, by notification, direct that these provisions apply to specified classes or categories of members of the Force charged with the maintenance of public order, substituting the expression “Central Government” with “State Government”.
BNSS_S34:485b49dba98b70cd679822a2d70373f: Every officer employed in connection with the affairs of a village and every person residing in a village must forthwith communicate to the nearest Magistrate or the officer in charge of the nearest police station, whichever is nearer, information regarding notorious receivers or vendors of stolen property; the resort or passage of robbers, escaped convicts, or proclaimed offenders; the commission or intention to commit non-bailable offences or offences under sections 189 and 191 of the Bharatiya Nyaya Sanhita, 2023; sudden, unnatural, or suspicious deaths, discovery of corpses, or disappearances suggesting non-bailable offences; acts committed outside India punishable under BNS sections 103, 105, 111, 112, 113, 178 to 181 (both inclusive), 305, 307, 309 to 312 (both inclusive), clauses (f) and (g) of section 326, 331, or 332; and matters directed by the District Magistrate via order with State Government sanction. Explanatory provisions specify that “village” includes village lands; “proclaimed offender” includes persons proclaimed by a Court or authority in territories where this Sanhita does not extend for acts punishable under the Bharatiya Nyaya Sanhita, 2023 with imprisonment for ten years or more, imprisonment for life, or death; and “officer employed in connection with the affairs of the village” means a member of the panchayat, including the headman and any person appointed to perform functions connected with village administration.
BNSS_S51:7300bd9d3493eb93dec4c698f97dcaf: Section 51 permits a registered medical practitioner, acting at a police officer's request, to conduct a reasonably necessary examination of an arrested person using reasonably necessary force if there are reasonable grounds to believe such examination will afford evidence as to the commission of an offence. Examinations of females must be conducted by or under the supervision of a female registered medical practitioner, and the resulting examination report must be forwarded to the investigating officer without delay. The Explanation defines “examination” to include blood, blood stains, semen, swabs in case of sexual offences, sputum, sweat, hair samples, and finger nail clippings using modern and scientific techniques including DNA profiling, and defines “registered medical practitioner” as one possessing qualifications recognised under the National Medical Commission Act, 2019 and registered in the National Medical Register or a State Medical Register.
BNSS_S46:a66dd8491c9070747840c3eba7d474e: Section 46 stipulates that "the person arrested" shall not be subjected to "more restraint than is necessary to prevent his escape." This section contains no Provisos, Exceptions, or Explanations.
BNSS_S53:7f3db6b1391c4d98a49ae5ce5c87a26: Section 53 mandates that any arrested person shall be examined soon after arrest by a medical officer in the service of the Central Government or a State Government, or if unavailable, by a registered medical practitioner. The section includes a Proviso allowing for additional examinations if deemed necessary and a second Proviso requiring that female arrested persons be examined only by or under the supervision of a female medical officer or, if unavailable, a female registered medical practitioner. The examining medical officer or registered medical practitioner must prepare a record mentioning any injuries or marks of violence and the approximate time of their infliction. Finally, a copy of the examination report shall be furnished to the arrested person or their nominee.
BNSS_S55:cf0905df832ea5611ace31993b8a30c: When an officer in charge of a police station or any police officer making an investigation under Chapter XIII requires an officer subordinate to him to arrest without a warrant (otherwise than in his presence) any person who may lawfully be arrested without a warrant, he shall deliver a written order specifying the person to be arrested and the offence or other cause for the arrest. The subordinate officer must notify the person of the substance of the order before making the arrest and show the order if so required by such person. Nothing in sub-section (1) affects the power of a police officer to arrest a person under section 35.
BNSS_S49:9392b1486d71dd3a727bb928807b46e: When a person is arrested by a police officer under a warrant (where bail is not provided or cannot be furnished) or arrested without warrant/by a private person (where the person cannot legally be admitted to bail or is unable to furnish bail), the arresting officer or the officer receiving the person from a private person may search the arrested person. The officer may place in safe custody all articles found upon the person, with the exception of "necessary wearing-apparel," and must provide a receipt showing the articles taken in possession. If it is necessary to cause a female to be searched, the search shall be conducted by another female with "strict regard to decency."
BNSS_S54:abfa0ac3f3b4cbf2904af60624bc875: Where a person is arrested on a charge of committing an offence and identification by any other person or persons is considered necessary for investigation, the Court having jurisdiction may, on the request of the officer in charge of a police station, direct the arrested person to subject themselves to identification by any person or persons in such manner as the Court may deem fit. A Proviso stipulates that if the person identifying the person arrested is mentally or physically disabled, the identification process shall take place under the supervision of a Magistrate. Such Magistrate shall ensure the person identifies the arrested person using methods that person is comfortable with, and the identification process shall be recorded by any audio-video electronic means.
BNSS_S57:2a9067457abe5fbd5d965392bda34db: A police officer making an arrest without warrant shall, without unnecessary delay, take or send the person arrested before a Magistrate having jurisdiction in the case or before the officer in charge of a police station. This mandate is subject to the provisions herein contained as to bail.
BNSS_S63:9733621e8e1162de2ca151365b660ac: Every summons issued by a Court under this Sanhita shall be in writing, in duplicate, signed by the presiding officer of such Court or by such other officer as the High Court may direct, and shall bear the seal of the Court. Alternatively, it may be in an encrypted or any other form of electronic communication and shall bear the image of the seal of the Court or digital signature.
BNSS_S68:d6b7c43abeccc7447038284d21640ab: For a person summoned in the active service of the Government, the Court issuing the summons shall ordinarily send it in duplicate to the head of the office in which such person is employed. The head of the office shall cause the summons to be served in the manner provided by section 64 and return it to the Court under his signature with the required endorsement. Such signature shall be evidence of due service.
BNSS_S61:9132bf92ead9a928efc6181a3737f42: If a person in lawful custody escapes or is rescued, the person from whose custody he escaped or was rescued may immediately pursue and arrest him in any place in India. The provisions of section 44 shall apply to arrests under sub-section (1) although the person making any such arrest is not acting under a warrant and is not a police officer having authority to arrest.
BNSS_S52:b4874052656865534cd873500480413: Upon arrest for an offence of rape or an attempt to commit rape where examination affords evidence, a registered medical practitioner employed in a hospital run by the Government or a local authority shall, at the request of a police officer, examine the accused, using reasonable force if necessary; if no such practitioner is within a radius of sixteen kilometres from the place of offence, any other registered medical practitioner, or any person acting in good faith in their aid and under their direction, may conduct the examination. The report must detail the accused's name, address, age, marks of injury, description of material taken for DNA profiling, other material particulars, precise reasons for each conclusion, and the exact time of commencement and completion. Without any delay, the practitioner shall forward the report to the investigating officer, who shall forward it to the Magistrate referred to in section 193 as part of the documents referred to in clause (a) of sub-section (6) of that section.
BNSS_S70:3a8881c16bbb0ed21c8cc2dd355433f: When a summons is served outside local jurisdiction or the serving officer is not present at the hearing, an affidavit made before a Magistrate and a duplicate summons endorsed as per section 64 or section 66 are admissible in evidence and deemed correct unless the contrary is proved. Such affidavit may be attached to the duplicate summons and returned to the Court. Summons served through electronic communication under sections 64 to 71 (both inclusive) are considered duly served, with an attested copy maintained as proof of service.
BNSS_S72:6759abe773d3af10f0d29a0f25fc873: Section 72 mandates that every warrant of arrest issued by a Court under this Sanhita shall be in writing, signed by the presiding officer of such Court, and bear the seal of the Court. Every such warrant remains in force until it is cancelled by the Court which issued it or until it is executed.
BNSS_S65:56cc54cf30542a837f0c9f4fcbf700c: Summons on a company or corporation may be effected by serving the Director, Manager, Secretary, or other officer, or by letter sent by registered post addressed to them in India, which shall be deemed to have been effected when the letter would arrive in ordinary course of post. An Explanation specifies that “company” means a body corporate and “corporation” means an incorporated company or other body corporate registered under the Companies Act, 2013 (18 of 2013) or a society registered under the Societies Registration Act, 1860 (21 of 1860). Summons on a firm or other association of individuals may be effected by serving any partner or by letter sent by registered post addressed to such partner, deemed to have been effected when the letter would arrive in ordinary course of post.
BNSS_S77:cf98aa0af33abcdabcda334f3232cb3: The police officer or other person executing a warrant of arrest shall notify the substance of the warrant to the person to be arrested. Furthermore, they shall show the warrant to the person to be arrested if so required.
BNSS_S67:a997d8996528c0f9a8cb4243bd87f90: If service cannot, by the exercise of due diligence, be effected as provided in section 64, section 65, or section 66, the serving officer shall affix one of the duplicates of the summons to some conspicuous part of the house or homestead in which the person summoned ordinarily resides. Thereupon, the Court, after making such inquiries as it thinks fit, may either declare that the summons has been duly served or order fresh service in such manner as it considers proper.
BNSS_S75:78e23116dea94efbb5f014cc860ed6a: The Chief Judicial Magistrate or a Magistrate of the first class may direct a warrant to any person within his local jurisdiction for the arrest of any escaped convict, proclaimed offender, or person accused of a non-bailable offence and evading arrest. The recipient must acknowledge receipt in writing and execute the warrant if the target is on any land or other property under his charge. Upon arrest, the individual shall be made over with the warrant to the nearest police officer for production before a Magistrate having jurisdiction, unless security is taken under section 73.
BNSS_S73:438bc17e0e7abf7e7fceac3845acf5b: Any Court issuing a warrant for the arrest of any person may, in its discretion, direct by endorsement on the warrant that the person be released from custody upon executing a bail bond with sufficient sureties for attendance before the Court at a specified time and thereafter until otherwise directed. Such endorsement shall state the number of sureties, the amount in which the sureties and the person are respectively bound, and the time at which the person is to attend before the Court. Whenever security is taken under this section, the officer to whom the warrant is directed shall forward the bond to the Court.
BNSS_S76:288d92815c53f5b725180f2e934c611: Section 76 stipulates that a warrant directed to any police officer may also be executed by any other police officer. This execution is permitted provided the name of the alternative police officer is endorsed upon the warrant by the officer to whom the warrant is directed or endorsed.
BNSS_S69:7347007f7e029eeefa6ca765b9f002b: When a Court desires that a summons issued by it shall be served at any place outside its local jurisdiction, it shall ordinarily send such summons in duplicate to a Magistrate. This Magistrate must be within whose local jurisdiction the person summoned resides, or is, to be there served.
BNSS_S79:1b5f4c0b7985db984029d7f85f715d7: Section 79 of the Bharatiya Nagarik Suraksha Sanhita, 2023, addresses the location where a warrant may be executed. It specifies that a warrant of arrest may be executed at any place in India.
BNSS_S83:f1d3d9459600f7cd124badfc096a8ce: The Executive Magistrate, District Superintendent of Police, or Commissioner of Police shall direct the removal in custody of an arrested person to the Court that issued the warrant if the person appears to be the intended individual. Under the first Proviso, if the offence is bailable and the person provides a bail bond to the satisfaction of the aforementioned officials, or fulfills security directions endorsed under section 73, the official shall take and forward said bond or security to the issuing Court. The second Proviso permits the Chief Judicial Magistrate (subject to section 480) or the Sessions Judge of the district where the arrest is made to release a person on bail for non-bailable offences after considering information and documents referred to in sub-section (2) of section 80. Sub-section (2) specifies that nothing in this section prevents a police officer from taking security under section 73.
BNSS_S89:93a46549c9d3f42f7385ec066e94a92: Section 89 provides that any person referred to in sub-section (3) of section 88 who is aggrieved by any refusal to deliver property or the proceeds of the sale thereof may appeal. This appeal is directed to the Court to which appeals ordinarily lie from the sentences of the first-mentioned Court.
BNSS_S85:93d25971daa3d3dda091d7184223a12: The Court issuing a proclamation under section 84 may, for reasons recorded in writing, order the attachment of movable or immovable property belonging to the proclaimed person; provided that simultaneous attachment may be ordered if the Court is satisfied, by affidavit or otherwise, that the person is about to dispose of or remove property from the local jurisdiction of the Court. Such orders authorize attachment within the district or, upon endorsement by the District Magistrate, outside that district, with movable property or debts attached via seizure, appointment of a receiver, or written orders prohibiting delivery. Immovable property is attached through the Collector (for land paying revenue to the State Government) or via possession, receiver appointment, or written orders prohibiting rent payment or delivery, while livestock or perishable property may be ordered for immediate sale with proceeds abiding the order of the Court. The powers, duties and liabilities of a receiver appointed under this section are the same as those under the Code of Civil Procedure, 1908.
BNSS_S91:9c34bfa5617885e094dee1e60c52e52: When any person for whose appearance or arrest the officer presiding in any Court is empowered to issue a summons or warrant is present in such Court, the officer may require such person to execute a bond or bail bond. This mechanism is for the person's appearance in such Court or any other Court to which the case may be transferred for trial.
BNSS_S87:c10df1788043cb86aa4b9bb607546af: Within six months from the date of attachment under section 85, any person other than the proclaimed person may prefer a claim or objection regarding property interest not liable to attachment to be inquired into and allowed or disallowed in whole or in part; a proviso allows a legal representative to continue such claim or objection in the event of the claimant or objector's death. Such claims or objections are preferred in the Court issuing the attachment order or, for property attached under an order endorsed under section 85(2), in the Court of the Chief Judicial Magistrate of the district, with a proviso that the Chief Judicial Magistrate may make the matter over for disposal to any subordinate Magistrate. Any person whose claim or objection is disallowed in whole or in part may, within one year from the date of such order, institute a suit to establish the claimed right in respect of the property in dispute, which shall make the order conclusive subject to the result of such suit.
BNSS_S71:8780a17779d25885d7e9c6eefc0f0ee: Notwithstanding preceding sections, a Court issuing a summons to a witness may, in addition to and simultaneously with the issue of such summons, direct a copy to be served by electronic communication or registered post to the witness at the place where he ordinarily resides or carries on business or personally works for gain. The Court may deem the summons duly served upon receipt of an acknowledgement purporting to be signed by the witness, an endorsement purporting to be made by a postal employee that the witness refused to take delivery of the summons, or proof of delivery under sub-section (3) of section 70 by electronic communication to the satisfaction of the Court.
BNSS_S84:2e9956778cd63e8d557e938b0e895cf: If a Court believes a person against whom a warrant has been issued has absconded or is concealing himself, it may publish a written proclamation requiring appearance at a specified place and time not less than thirty days from the date of publishing, via public reading in a conspicuous place of residence, affixing to a conspicuous part of the residence, town/village, or Court-house, and optionally through a daily newspaper. A written statement by the Court regarding publication per clause (i) of sub-section (2) shall be conclusive evidence of compliance. For persons accused of offences punishable with imprisonment of ten years or more, imprisonment for life, or death under the Bharatiya Nyaya Sanhita, 2023 or any other law, failure to appear allows the Court, after inquiry, to pronounce them a proclaimed offender and make a declaration, with the requirements of sub-sections (2) and (3) applying to such declaration.
BNSS_S93:6e2ffc24da4bfddb6eeb04bbffa03a9: Section 93 stipulates that the provisions contained in this Chapter relating to summons and warrant shall, so far as may be, apply to every summons and every warrant of arrest issued under this Sanhita. This application specifically encompasses the issue, service and execution of said summons and warrants.
BNSS_S102:bf3cb53b4ad2911a175d75f334c0b7: Section 102 stipulates that the provisions of sections 32, 72, 74, 76, 79, 80 and 81 shall, so far as may be, apply to all search-warrants. Specifically, these provisions govern search-warrants issued under section 96, section 97, section 98 or section 100.
BNSS_S105:d98dcf1aaebb612c47e239bc909d2d: Section 105 mandates that the process of conducting search of a place or taking possession of any property, article or thing under this Chapter or section 185, including the preparation of the list of all things seized and the signing of such list by witnesses, shall be recorded through audio-video electronic means, preferably mobile phone. The police officer shall without delay forward such recording to the District Magistrate, Sub-divisional Magistrate, or Judicial Magistrate of the first class.
BNSS_S108:6050500799faf5206a16ac653f9a70: Under Section 108, any Magistrate may direct a search to be made in his presence. This authority applies to any place for the search of which the Magistrate is competent to issue a search-warrant.
BNSS_S82:afa1cc151c98bc0fbea617cb61a08ff: When a warrant of arrest is executed outside the issuing district, the person arrested shall be taken before the Magistrate, District Superintendent, or Commissioner, subject to exceptions where the issuing Court is within thirty kilometres of the place of arrest, is nearer than the Executive Magistrate, District Superintendent of Police, or Commissioner of Police within the local limits of whose jurisdiction the arrest was made, or security is taken under section 73. Upon such arrest, the police officer shall forthwith give information regarding the arrest and the place where the arrested person is being held to the designated police officer in the district and to the officer of another district where the arrested person normally resides.
BNSS_S44:cc0a49602ee92f787b379eb6f500fa9: If a person acting under a warrant of arrest or a police officer having authority to arrest believes the person to be arrested is within a place, any person residing in or in charge of such place shall, on demand, allow free ingress and afford all reasonable facilities for a search. If ingress cannot be obtained under sub-section (1), it shall be lawful in any case for a person acting under a warrant and in any case in which a warrant may issue, but cannot be obtained without affording the person to be arrested an opportunity of escape, for a police officer to enter and search by breaking open any outer or inner door or window of any house or place after notification of authority and purpose and a demand of admittance. A proviso specifies that if the place is an apartment in the actual occupancy of a female (not being the person to be arrested) who, according to custom, does not appear in public, the person or police officer shall, before entering, give notice to such female to withdraw and afford her every reasonable facility for withdrawing before breaking open the apartment and entering. Additionally, any police officer or other person authorised to make an arrest may break open any outer or inner door or window of any house or place to liberate himself or any other person who, having lawfully entered for the purpose of making an arrest, is detained therein.
BNSS_S97:b63e034642f3f2431563dc97fdfa5c3: A District Magistrate, Sub-divisional Magistrate, or Magistrate of the first class, upon information and inquiry, may issue a warrant if they have "reason to believe" a place is used for the deposit or sale of stolen property or "objectionable articles." This warrant authorizes a police officer above the rank of a constable to enter, search, take possession of suspected property or articles, convey them before a Magistrate (or guard/dispose of them in a place of safety), and take into custody any person appearing privy to the deposit, sale, or production of such items. "Objectionable articles" are defined as counterfeit coin; metal pieces contravening the Coinage Act, 2011 or section 11 of the Customs Act, 1962; counterfeit currency notes; counterfeit stamps; forged documents; false seals; obscene objects referred to in section 294 of the Bharatiya Nyaya Sanhita, 2023; and instruments or materials used for their production.
BNSS_S95:11bb86b688bb3a20d54fdf767be5c52: Under sub-section (1), if a document, parcel or thing in the custody of a postal authority is, in the opinion of the District Magistrate, Chief Judicial Magistrate, Court of Session or High Court, wanted for any investigation, inquiry, trial or other proceeding under this Sanhita, such Magistrate or Court may require the postal authority to deliver it to a person as directed. Under sub-section (2), if such document, parcel or thing is, in the opinion of any other Magistrate, whether Executive or Judicial, or of any Commissioner of Police or District Superintendent of Police, wanted for any such purpose, they may require the postal authority to cause search to be made for and to detain such item. This detention under sub-section (2) is pending the order of a District Magistrate, Chief Judicial Magistrate or Court under sub-section (1).
BNSS_S101:a93eab01536bbe9513dbf7ca9c6bdc: Upon a complaint made on oath of the abduction or unlawful detention of a woman, or a female child for any unlawful purpose, a District Magistrate, Sub-divisional Magistrate, or Magistrate of the first class may order the immediate restoration of such woman to her liberty, or of such female child to her parent, guardian, or other person having the lawful charge of such child. The Magistrate may compel compliance with such order, using such force as may be necessary.
BNSS_S98:e0bbb33d743ade639199308328e8e0f: Where a State Government determines a newspaper, book, or document contains matter punishable under section 152, 196, 197, 294, 295, or 299 of the Bharatiya Nyaya Sanhita, 2023, it may, by notification stating the grounds of its opinion, declare such copies forfeited to the Government. Upon such declaration, any police officer may seize the items anywhere in India, and any Magistrate may by warrant authorize any police officer not below the rank of sub-inspector to enter and search premises for suspected copies. The section provides definitions where “newspaper” and “book” have the same meanings as in the Press and Registration of Books Act, 1867, and “document” includes any painting, drawing, photograph, or other visible representation. Furthermore, a procedural limitation stipulates that no order passed or action taken under this section shall be called in question in any Court otherwise than in accordance with the provisions of section 99.
BNSS_S114:25bb7b5762e41f830af5f673937891: Section 114 provides that a Court in India, regarding a criminal matter, may send warrants in duplicate to a specified Court, Judge or Magistrate in a contracting State, via Central Government notification, to secure the arrest, attendance, or production of a document or other thing, including summons or warrants issued upon application by an investigating officer or superior during investigation or inquiry. Conversely, warrants issued by a Court, Judge or Magistrate in a contracting State for arrest, attendance, or production of a document or other thing shall be executed by a Court in India as if received from another Court in India. For prisoners, the Court in India or Central Government may impose conditions for persons transferred to a contracting State, while for prisoners transferred to India, the Court in India shall ensure compliance with transfer conditions and maintain custody as directed in writing by the Central Government.
BNSS_S109:9bbf874e985450183328245f98fafe: Section 109 empowers any Court to impound any document or thing produced before it under this Sanhita. This power may be exercised by the Court if it thinks fit.
