BNSS_S116:4e42cb4aa0b7ded400019ee0f50bf0: Under Section 116, the Court shall, via sub-section (1) or upon receipt of a letter of request under sub-section (3) of section 115, direct a police officer not below the rank of Sub-Inspector of Police to take all steps necessary for tracing and identifying unlawfully acquired property. Such steps may include any inquiry, investigation, or survey regarding any person, place, property, assets, documents, or books of account in any bank or public financial institutions or other relevant matters. Any such inquiry, investigation, or survey shall be carried out by the aforementioned officer in accordance with directions issued by the Court.
BNSS_S118:d3270524b1c5b94d160be114071138: The Court may appoint the District Magistrate of the area where the property is situated, or an officer nominated by the District Magistrate, as the Administrator of property seized or forfeited under this Chapter. The Administrator shall receive and manage property ordered under sub-section (1) of section 117 or under section 120, subject to the manner and conditions specified by the Central Government. Additionally, the Administrator shall take measures, as directed by the Central Government, to dispose of property forfeited to the Central Government.
BNSS_S115:fca7b27e1e14335840cb316d0717fc: Where a Court in India has reasonable grounds to believe any property is derived or obtained, directly or indirectly, from the commission of an offence, it may order attachment or forfeiture under sections 116 to 122 (both inclusive). If such property is suspected to be in a contracting State, the Court may issue a letter of request to a Court or an authority in the contracting State for execution of such order. Additionally, if the Central Government receives a letter of request from a Court or authority in a contracting State regarding property in India derived or obtained, directly or indirectly, from an offence committed in that State, it may forward the request to the Court for execution under sections 116 to 122 (both inclusive) or any other law for the time being in force.
BNSS_S113:128d874a83ab33786a98eae8e2513f: Upon receipt of a letter of request from a competent Court or authority in a country or place outside India for the examination of any person or production of any document or thing regarding an offence under investigation, the Central Government may, if it thinks fit, forward the request to the Chief Judicial Magistrate or a Judicial Magistrate appointed in this behalf to summon the person and record a statement or cause the document or thing to be produced, or send the letter to a police officer to investigate the offence as if it had been committed within India. All evidence taken or collected under sub-section (1), including authenticated copies thereof or the thing so collected, shall be forwarded by the Magistrate or police officer to the Central Government for transmission to the issuing Court or authority in such manner as the Central Government may deem fit.
BNSS_S121:850ba14d3996624e15fc28fbf563d9: Where the Court declares property forfeited to the Central Government under section 120 and the source of only a part of such property is not proved to the satisfaction of the Court, it shall order an option for the affected person to pay a fine equal to the market value of such part in lieu of forfeiture. Before imposing such a fine, the affected person shall be given a reasonable opportunity of being heard. If the fine is paid within the allowed time, the Court may revoke the declaration of forfeiture under section 120 and release the property.
BNSS_S133:01ba7c34232c27321710f46f98e933: Every summons or warrant issued under section 132 shall be accompanied by a copy of the order made under section 130. The officer serving or executing such summons or warrant shall deliver said copy to the person served with, or arrested under, the summons or warrant.
BNSS_S117:18577671266cf54357ef7278006894: An officer conducting an inquiry or investigation under section 116, having reason to believe property is likely to be concealed, transferred, or dealt with in any manner which will result in disposal of such property, may order the seizure of such property or, if seizure is not practicable, an order of attachment directing that the property shall not be transferred or otherwise dealt with, except with the prior permission of the officer making such order. A copy of such order shall be served on the person concerned. Any order made under sub-section (1) shall have no effect unless confirmed by an order of the said Court within a period of thirty days of its being made.
BNSS_S119:54e5b9591a456dee7200e882298727: If an inquiry, investigation or survey under section 116 leads the Court to believe all or any properties are proceeds of crime, the Court may serve a notice upon the person affected to show cause within thirty days why such properties should not be declared proceeds of crime and forfeited to the Central Government. The notice requires the person affected to indicate the source of income, earnings or assets used to acquire the property, the evidence relied upon, and other relevant information and particulars. Where the notice specifies property held on behalf of the person affected by any other person, a copy of the notice shall also be served upon such other person.
BNSS_S123:e2fb77259819fa0e3ffa2995aa1a07: Every letter of request, summons or warrant received by the Central Government from, and every letter of request, summons or warrant to be transmitted to a contracting State under this Chapter, shall be transmitted to a contracting State or sent to the concerned Court in India. Such transmission shall be conducted in such form and in such manner as the Central Government may, by notification, specify in this behalf.
BNSS_S125:36a5c5d494d96175fa4c5eb18cffd7: A Court of Session or Court of a Magistrate of the first class may, upon convicting a person of specified offences or abetting them, order the execution of a bond or bail bond for keeping the peace for a period not exceeding three years. Specified offences include those punishable under Chapter XI of the Bharatiya Nyaya Sanhita, 2023 (excluding section 193(1), section 196, or section 197), assault, using criminal force, committing mischief, criminal intimidation, or any offence causing, intending, or likely to cause a breach of the peace. If the conviction is set aside on appeal or otherwise, the bond or bail bond becomes void, and such orders may also be issued by an Appellate Court or a Court exercising its powers of revision.
BNSS_S136:9f46aba7e349ec8fdd5f6a9c137b9f: If an inquiry proves it necessary for keeping the peace or maintaining good behaviour, the Magistrate shall order the person to execute a bond or bail bond. This is subject to Provisos stating that no person shall be ordered to give security of a nature different from, or of an amount larger than, or for a period longer than, that specified in the order made under section 130. Additionally, the amount of every bond or bail bond shall be fixed with due regard to the circumstances of the case and shall not be excessive, and if the person is a child, the bond shall be executed only by his sureties.
BNSS_S103:4d4340db5e3cbf8ef31523347760e0: Upon production of a warrant, any person residing in or in charge of a closed place must allow free ingress and reasonable facilities for search, failing which the officer may proceed under sub-section (2) of section 44; if a person is reasonably suspected of concealing an article, they may be searched, provided that if the person is a woman, the search is conducted by another woman with strict regard to decency. The officer must call two or more independent and respectable inhabitants of the locality, or another locality if none are available or willing, via a written order to witness the search, ensuring the search is conducted in their presence, the occupant or their representative is permitted to attend, and a signed list of all things seized and their locations is delivered to the occupant or searched person. Witnesses must sign the list of all things seized but are not required to attend Court unless specially summoned. Any person who, without reasonable cause, refuses or neglects to attend and witness a search when called via a written order commits an offence under section 222 of the Bharatiya Nyaya Sanhita, 2023 (45 of 2023).
BNSS_S107:dcb7f503f2460f7f1d505100267c58: A police officer, with the approval of the Superintendent of Police or Commissioner of Police, may apply to the Court or Magistrate for the attachment of property derived or obtained, directly or indirectly, as a result of a criminal activity or from the commission of any offence. If properties are believed to be proceeds of crime, the Court or Magistrate may issue a show-cause notice for an explanation within fourteen days (including service to any person holding property on behalf of the subject), subject to a proviso allowing an ex parte order if no appearance occurs within fourteen days and a provision for interim ex parte attachment or seizure if notice would defeat the object of attachment. Upon finding properties to be proceeds of crime, the Court or Magistrate shall direct the District Magistrate to rateably distribute such proceeds to affected persons within sixty days. If there are no claimants, no ascertainable claimant, or a surplus exists, such proceeds of crime shall stand forfeited to the Government.
BNSS_S139:988c12dc4cacc26feb35b7acba2512: Section 139 provides that a bond or bail bond executed by any such person shall bind them to keep the peace or to be of good behaviour, as the case may be. In the latter case, the commission, attempt to commit, or abetment of any offence punishable with imprisonment, wherever it may be committed, constitutes a breach of the bond or bail bond.
BNSS_S137:13fe8f58b8c975464f43566b4e6f47: If, on an inquiry under section 135, it is not proved that it is necessary for keeping the peace or maintaining good behaviour that the person in respect of whom the inquiry is made should execute a bond, the Magistrate shall make an entry on the record to that effect. If such person is in custody only for the purposes of the inquiry, the Magistrate shall release him, or if such person is not in custody, shall discharge him.
BNSS_S131:7e7b12e7f1822cc639a3d53c45f17d: If the person in respect of whom such order is made is present in Court, the order shall be read over to him. If the person so desires, the substance thereof shall be explained to him.
BNSS_S127:61af85c6f6b91d16dad3d8ea589637: Upon receiving information that any person within or without their local jurisdiction intentionally disseminates, attempts to disseminate, or abets the dissemination of matter punishable under section 152, 196, 197, or 299 of the Bharatiya Nyaya Sanhita, 2023, or matter concerning a Judge amounting to criminal intimidation or defamation under the Bharatiya Nyaya Sanhita, 2023, or circulates obscene matter referred to in section 294 of the Bharatiya Nyaya Sanhita, 2023, an Executive Magistrate may require such person to show cause why they should not execute a bond or bail bond for good behaviour for a period not exceeding one year. An exception is provided whereby no proceedings shall be taken under this section against the editor, proprietor, printer, or publisher of any publication registered under and in conformity with the Press and Registration of Books Act, 1867, except by the order or under the authority of the State Government or some officer empowered by the State Government in this behalf.
BNSS_S143:7d9fee19308e830ce1a8911937c371: When a person for whose appearance a summons or warrant has been issued under the proviso to sub-section (3) of section 140 or under sub-section (10) of section 142 appears or is brought before the Magistrate or Court, the Magistrate or Court shall cancel the bond or bail bond and order fresh security of the same description for the unexpired portion of the term. Such an order shall, for the purposes of sections 139 to 142 (both inclusive), be deemed an order made under section 125 or section 136, as the case may be.
BNSS_S141:8ebcad94c41ac4f3980c038431a912: Persons failing to give security under section 125 or section 136, or breaching a bond or bail bond under section 136, shall be committed to or detained in prison until the period expires or security is provided, without prejudice to other punishments or forfeitures. For security periods exceeding one year, the Magistrate refers the case to the Sessions Judge (who may transfer proceedings to an Additional Sessions Judge) to pass an order after a hearing, subject to a Proviso that imprisonment for failure to give security shall not exceed three years. Such references under sub-section (2) shall include other persons ordered to give security in the same proceeding—whose imprisonment shall not exceed the period for which they were ordered to give security—and any security tendered to the officer in charge of the jail must be referred to the ordering Court or Magistrate. Imprisonment for failure to give security for keeping the peace shall be simple; for good behaviour, it shall be simple under section 127, or rigorous or simple under section 128 or section 129 as the Court or Magistrate directs.
BNSS_S145:fb3e4be6408e2a4ac6a7b590646def: Proceedings under section 144 may be taken in any district where the person is, where he or his wife resides, where he last resided with his wife or the mother of the illegitimate child, or where his father or mother resides. Evidence shall be recorded in the manner prescribed for summons-cases in the presence of the person or their advocate; however, a Proviso allows the Magistrate to proceed *ex parte* if the person is wilfully avoiding service or wilfully neglecting to attend the Court. Such *ex parte* orders may be set aside for good cause shown via application made within three months, subject to terms and costs. The Court further possesses the power to make such orders as to costs as may be just regarding applications under section 144.
BNSS_S153:858caced3e9350bb19a4bf41a4a3cb: Section 153 mandates that an order shall, if practicable, be served on the person against whom it is made in the manner provided for service of summons. If such order cannot be so served, it shall be notified by proclamation published in the manner directed by State Government rules. Additionally, a copy of the order shall be stuck up at such place or places as may be fittest for conveying the information to such person.
BNSS_S135:a7549e1466bb8724b05351bc73dae3: Upon a section 130 order being read under section 131 or a person appearing via summons or warrant under section 132, the Magistrate shall inquire into the truth of the information following the manner prescribed for conducting trial and recording evidence in summons-cases. To prevent breach of the peace, disturbance of the public tranquillity, commission of any offence, or for public safety, the Magistrate may, for reasons recorded in writing, direct the execution of a bond or bail bond for keeping the peace or maintaining good behaviour, subject to Provisos that no person under sections 127, 128, or 129 shall be directed to execute a bond for maintaining good behaviour and bond conditions shall not be more onerous than the section 130 order. Evidence of general repute or otherwise may prove a person is a habitual offender or so desperate and dangerous as to render being at large without security hazardous to the community, and multiple persons may be dealt with in same or separate inquiries. The inquiry must be completed within a period of six months, or proceedings stand terminated unless the Magistrate records special reasons in writing to continue, subject to a Proviso that proceedings against a person in detention stand terminated on the expiry of a period of six months of such detention and a provision for a Sessions Judge to vacate such directions if they lack special reasons or are perverse.
BNSS_S155:ca1baedde0c6d8b7335e296b3594fd: Section 155 prescribes the penalty for failure to comply with section 154. If the person against whom an order is made under section 154 does not perform such act or appear and show cause, they shall be liable to the penalty specified in that behalf in section 223 of the Bharatiya Nyaya Sanhita, 2023. Consequently, the order shall be made absolute.
BNSS_S154:00b247cada396048cf5b2958657a94: The person against whom such order is made shall either (a) perform, within the time and in the manner specified in the order, the act directed thereby, or (b) appear in accordance with such order and show cause against the same. Such appearance or hearing may be permitted through audio-video conferencing.
BNSS_S149:5a62d58e5596dcfbbc99b10d453e40: Under Section 149, if an assembly referred to in sub-section (1) of section 148 cannot otherwise be dispersed and it is necessary for the public security, the District Magistrate or any authorised Executive Magistrate may cause it to be dispersed by the armed forces. The Magistrate may require the officer in command of the armed forces to disperse the assembly and arrest and confine persons as directed, or as necessary to disperse the assembly or ensure they are punished according to law. The officer in command shall obey such requisition as he thinks fit, while using as little force and doing as little injury to person and property as is consistent with dispersing the assembly and arresting and detaining such persons.
BNSS_S147:e1e98610c97aea8695197f5ff6a2c8: A copy of the order of maintenance or interim maintenance and expenses of proceedings, as the case may be, shall be given without payment to the person in whose favour it is made, his guardian, if any, or the person to whom the allowance for the maintenance or the allowance for the interim maintenance and expenses of proceeding, as the case may be, is to be paid. Such order may be enforced by any Magistrate in any place where the person against whom it is made may be, upon the Magistrate being satisfied as to the identity of the parties and the non-payment of the allowance, or as the case may be, expenses, due.
BNSS_S161:e4fe48211fb599209aae54b4fa7b6f: A Magistrate making an order under section 152 may issue an injunction to the person against whom the order was made to prevent "imminent danger or injury of a serious kind to the public" pending the determination of the matter. In default of such person "forthwith obeying" the injunction, the Magistrate may use, or cause to be used, such means as he thinks fit to obviate such danger or prevent such injury. No suit shall lie in respect of anything done in "good faith" by a Magistrate under this section.
BNSS_S160:85b44a50f623ad6b36c6dc6bf2f530: Upon an order being made absolute under section 155 or section 157, the Magistrate shall give notice to the person against whom the order was made, requiring the performance of the directed act within a fixed time and notifying them of liability under section 223 of the Bharatiya Nyaya Sanhita, 2023, for disobedience. If the act is not performed, the Magistrate may cause it to be performed and recover costs through the sale of any building, goods, or other property removed by the order, or via the distress and sale of any other movable property within or without the Magistrate's local jurisdiction, which requires endorsement by the Magistrate within whose local jurisdiction the property is found if located outside. The section further provides that no suit shall lie in respect of anything done in good faith under this section.
BNSS_S157:d477ab4ed3a324932780f1f32c3793: If a person against whom an order under section 152 is made appears and shows cause, the Magistrate shall take evidence in the matter as in a summons-case. If the Magistrate is satisfied that the order, either as originally made or subject to modification as considered necessary, is reasonable and proper, the order shall be made absolute; if the Magistrate is not so satisfied, no further proceedings shall be taken. The proviso stipulates that proceedings under this section shall be completed, as soon as possible, within a period of ninety days, which may be extended to one hundred and twenty days if reasons are recorded in writing.
BNSS_S165:6b134e669dbe7df9e3525c7183a63e: Under Section 165, if the Magistrate considers a case an emergency, or if possession referred to in section 164 is absent or undetermined, he may attach the subject of dispute until a competent Court determines the parties' rights; a Proviso allows the Magistrate to withdraw this attachment if there is no longer any likelihood of breach of the peace. If no receiver has been appointed by any Civil Court, the Magistrate may arrange for property care or appoint a receiver with all the powers of a receiver under the Code of Civil Procedure, 1908 (5 of 1908), subject to the Magistrate's control. A second Proviso mandates that if a Civil Court subsequently appoints a receiver, the Magistrate shall order the handover of possession to the Civil Court's receiver and discharge his own receiver, while potentially making incidental or consequential orders.
BNSS_S140:c3f257ec63b11b108b218e98e666dc: A Magistrate may refuse to accept any offered surety or reject any surety previously accepted by himself or his predecessor if the surety is an unfit person for the purposes of the bail bond, provided that the Magistrate conducts an inquiry on oath or causes a subordinate Magistrate to hold such an inquiry and make a report. Before the inquiry, the Magistrate shall give reasonable notice to the surety and the person by whom the surety was offered and record the substance of the evidence adduced. If satisfied that the surety is an unfit person for the purposes of the bail bond, the Magistrate shall make an order refusing or rejecting the surety and record his reasons. The Proviso to sub-section (3) requires that before rejecting a previously accepted surety, the Magistrate shall issue a summons or warrant, as he thinks fit, to cause the person for whom the surety is bound to appear or be brought before him.
BNSS_S171:ea3f299c825526af112b3fa4328e9e: A police officer may, of his own authority, interpose to prevent any injury attempted to be committed in his view to any public property, whether movable or immovable. This authority extends to preventing the removal or injury of any public landmark, buoy or other mark used for navigation.
BNSS_S151:0bb99d1e54270dd99be7fe1358b055: Section 151 stipulates that no prosecution for acts under sections 148, 149, or 150 shall be instituted in any Criminal Court except with the sanction of the Central Government for officers or members of the armed forces, or with the sanction of the State Government in any other case. No offence is deemed committed by an Executive Magistrate or police officer acting in good faith under said sections, any person acting in good faith in compliance with a requisition under section 148 or 149, an officer of the armed forces acting under section 150 in good faith, or a member of the armed forces acting in obedience to any order which he was bound to obey. The section also provides definitions for "armed forces" (army, naval and air forces, and other Union forces), "officer" (commissioned, gazetted, or in pay, including junior commissioned, warrant, petty, non-commissioned, and non-gazetted officers), and "member" (any person in the armed forces other than an officer).
BNSS_S175:1485e2e991029cbe5bd0435a63caeb: An officer in charge of a police station may, without a Magistrate's order, investigate any cognizable case within the jurisdiction of a Court empowered under Chapter XIV, subject to a Proviso that the Superintendent of Police may require a Deputy Superintendent of Police to investigate based on the nature and gravity of the offence. No proceeding shall be questioned on the ground that the officer was not empowered under this section to investigate. A Magistrate empowered under section 210 may order investigation after considering an application supported by an affidavit under sub-section (4) of section 173, conducting necessary inquiry, and reviewing a police officer's submission. Furthermore, a Magistrate empowered under section 210 may order investigation into complaints against a public servant acting in the course of official duties, provided they receive a report from the superior officer and consider the public servant's assertions.
BNSS_S178:8f7608c63b133a7d8759a9d541e9a5: Upon receiving a report under section 176, the Magistrate may direct an investigation or, if he thinks fit, at once proceed or depute any Magistrate subordinate to him to proceed. This authority allows the Magistrate or his subordinate to hold a preliminary inquiry into, or otherwise dispose of, the case. Such actions must be conducted in the manner provided in this Sanhita.
BNSS_S177:6fe24a41fb4de4dcd8f9290f09c35e: Every report sent to a Magistrate under section 176 shall, if the State Government so directs by general or special order, be submitted through an appointed superior officer of police. Such superior officer may give instructions to the officer in charge of the police station as he thinks fit and, after recording such instructions on the report, shall transmit the same without delay to the Magistrate.
BNSS_S181:f33b74fcd06b2cfa52aa1d9a748e81: Section 181(1) stipulates that no statement made by any person to a police officer during an investigation under this Chapter shall be signed if reduced to writing, nor shall such statements or records in a police diary be used at any inquiry or trial regarding the offence under investigation, save as provided. The Proviso specifies that if a prosecution witness's written statement is duly proved, the accused (and the prosecution with Court permission) may use it to contradict the witness per section 148 of the Bharatiya Sakshya Adhiniyam, 2023, with further limited use in re-examination to explain matters referred to in cross-examination. Sub-section (2) clarifies that these restrictions do not apply to statements under clause (a) of section 26 of the Bharatiya Sakshya Adhiniyam, 2023, nor affect the proviso to sub-section (2) of section 23 of that Adhiniyam. The Explanation states that an omission of a significant and relevant fact or circumstance in the statement may amount to a contradiction, which shall be a question of fact.
BNSS_S179:3758b4a3e0305e9e8bf3533c1196f4: A police officer making an investigation under this Chapter may, by order in writing, require the attendance before himself of any person within the limits of his own or any adjoining station who appears to be acquainted with the facts and circumstances of the case. A proviso stipulates that no male person under the age of fifteen years or above the age of sixty years, a woman, a mentally or physically disabled person, or a person with acute illness shall be required to attend at any place other than the place in which such person resides, though a further proviso permits such persons to attend at the police station if they are willing. Additionally, the State Government may, by rules, provide for the payment by the police officer of the reasonable expenses of every person attending under sub-section (1) at any place other than his residence.
BNSS_S185:3745721bc1c255f1deefd81dc3f65b: An officer in charge of a police station or an investigating officer authorized to investigate an offence may search a place within their limits/attachment if they have reasonable grounds to believe something necessary for the investigation is present and cannot be obtained without undue delay, provided they record the grounds of belief and the item sought in writing in the case-diary. The search shall be conducted in person if practicable, subject to a Proviso requiring the search be recorded through audio-video electronic means, preferably by mobile phone, or may be delegated to a subordinate officer via a written order after recording reasons for the inability to search in person. Provisions regarding search-warrants and general provisions under section 103 apply, and copies of records made under sub-section (1) or (3) must be sent to the nearest Magistrate empowered to take cognizance of the offence within forty-eight hours. The owner or occupier of the searched place shall be furnished a free copy of such records by the Magistrate upon application.
BNSS_S188:43a2cceeb0e7518073dcf882e14534: When any subordinate police officer has made any investigation under this Chapter, he shall report the result of such investigation. The report must be submitted to the officer in charge of the police station.
BNSS_S189:3b626b6502e03732eab4680d0caf78: Under Section 189, if the officer in charge of the police station determines upon investigation that there is not sufficient evidence or reasonable round of suspicion to justify the forwarding of the accused to a Magistrate, such officer shall release the accused if in custody. This release is contingent upon the execution of a bond or bail bond to appear, if and when required, before a Magistrate empowered to take cognizance of the offence on a police report, and to try the accused or commit him for trial.
BNSS_S142:18fcc90e87d0ff45e1f163c4c094fe: The District Magistrate (for orders passed by an Executive Magistrate under section 136) or the Chief Judicial Magistrate (in any other case) may discharge persons imprisoned for failing to give security if there is no hazard to the community or any other person, either unconditionally or upon conditions, subject to the Proviso that any condition imposed shall cease to be operative when the period for which such person was ordered to give security has expired. The High Court, Court of Session, or appropriate Magistrate may reduce the amount of security, number of sureties, or the time required, while the State Government prescribes rules for conditional discharge. If conditions are not fulfilled, the District Magistrate or Chief Judicial Magistrate may cancel the discharge, allowing police to arrest without warrant and produce the person before the relevant Magistrate for remand to prison for the unexpired portion (the period between the breach and the date they would have been entitled to release) unless security is provided per the original order. The High Court or Court of Session may, for sufficient reasons to be recorded in writing, cancel bonds for keeping the peace or for good behaviour, as may the District Magistrate or Chief Judicial Magistrate, and a surety may apply to the Court to cancel such bonds, requiring the issuance of a summons or warrant as it thinks fit.
BNSS_S191:960a34c7a173fcd91214230f166437: Section 191 mandates that no complainant or witness on their way to any Court shall be required to accompany a police officer, subjected to unnecessary restraint or inconvenience, or required to give security for appearance other than their own bond. A Proviso stipulates that if any complainant or witness refuses to attend or execute a bond as directed in section 190, the officer in charge of the police station may forward them in custody to the Magistrate. The Magistrate may then detain them in custody until such bond is executed or until the hearing of the case is completed.
BNSS_S173:d27bf490a77706d1f27cdaf6f90dfb: Information regarding a cognizable offence may be given orally (reduced to writing, read over, and signed) or via electronic communication (signed within three days and entered in a prescribed book) to an officer in charge, who must provide a copy forthwith, free of cost, to the informant or victim. Provisos mandate that for offences under BNS sections 64, 65, 66, 67, 68, 69, 70, 71, 74, 75, 76, 77, 78, 79, or 124, information shall be recorded by a woman police officer or any woman officer, and if the victim is temporarily or permanently mentally or physically disabled, recording must occur at their residence or chosen place with an interpreter or special educator, be videographed, and include a statement recorded by a Magistrate under section 183(6)(a) as soon as possible. For offences punishable for three years or more but less than seven years, the officer in charge may, with prior permission from an officer not below the rank of Deputy Superintendent of Police, conduct a preliminary enquiry within fourteen days or proceed with investigation; if an officer refuses to record information, the aggrieved person may send the substance in writing by post to the Superintendent of Police or apply to the Magistrate.
BNSS_S197:855c7795a431a4df32c0f385eed480: Section 197 stipulates that every offence shall ordinarily be inquired into and tried by a Court. This inquiry and trial must be conducted by a Court within whose local jurisdiction the offence was committed.
BNSS_S194:d23604bc10b118fdc0e74423f38718: Upon receiving information of suicide, killing by another, animal, machinery, accident, or death raising reasonable suspicion of an offence, the officer in charge of a police station or State Government empowered officer shall immediately intimate the nearest Executive Magistrate empowered to hold inquests and investigate the body with two or more respectable inhabitants to report the apparent cause of death, describing wounds, fractures, bruises, and the weapon or instrument used. This report must be signed by the police officer and concurring persons and forwarded to the District Magistrate or Sub-divisional Magistrate within twenty-four hours. The body shall be forwarded to the nearest Civil Surgeon or State Government appointed qualified medical person if there is doubt regarding cause of death, the officer considers it expedient, or it involves a woman's suicide or death within seven years of her marriage (including cases of reasonable suspicion or relative's request), subject to State Government rules and risks of putrefaction. District Magistrates, Sub-divisional Magistrates, and specially empowered Executive Magistrates are the officials empowered to hold inquests.
BNSS_S186:d0f7c0fe998c99d13ef6d951587bed: An officer in charge of a police station or a police officer not being below the rank of sub-inspector making an investigation may require an officer in charge of another police station to cause a search under section 185 and forward any found thing to the requesting officer. If delay might result in evidence of the commission of an offence being concealed or destroyed, an officer in charge of a police station or an investigating officer may search a place in the limits of another police station under section 185 as if it were within their own limits. For searches under sub-section (3), the officer must forthwith send notice to the local officer in charge with a section 103 list and send records referred to in sub-sections (1) and (3) of section 185 to the nearest Magistrate empowered to take cognizance, while the owner or occupier shall, on application, be furnished free of cost with a copy of any such records.
BNSS_S200:9e177d10702762157316bb00cb416b: Section 200 governs the place of trial when an act is an offence by reason of its relation to another act which is also an offence or would be an offence if the doer were capable of committing an offence. Under this provision, the first-mentioned offence may be inquired into or tried by a Court within whose local jurisdiction either act was done.
BNSS_S196:5d149dfccc599e1f399ad1cb0d01b3: The nearest Magistrate empowered to hold inquests shall, in cases referred to in section 194(3) clause (i) or (ii), or may, in other cases mentioned in section 194(1), hold an inquiry into the cause of death instead of or in addition to police investigations, recording evidence and exercising all powers applicable to an inquiry into an offence. A mandatory inquiry by the Magistrate within whose local jurisdiction the offence occurred is required if any person dies or disappears, or rape is alleged to have been committed on any woman, while in police custody or any other custody authorised by the Magistrate or the Court. The Magistrate may cause a dead body to be disinterred and examined, shall inform and allow the presence of relatives wherever practicable, and the Magistrate, Executive Magistrate, or police officer shall forward the body to the nearest Civil Surgeon or State Government-appointed qualified medical person within twenty-four hours of death, unless reasons for impossibility are recorded in writing. An Explanation specifies that the expression "relative" means parents, children, brothers, sisters and spouse.
BNSS_S163:ed9e8196c12880cc2a81582fe3b942: A District Magistrate, Sub-divisional Magistrate, or any Executive Magistrate specially empowered by the State Government may, by a written order stating material facts and served per section 153, direct an individual, persons in a particular area, or the public generally to abstain from an act or take orders regarding property to prevent obstruction, annoyance, or injury to any person lawfully employed, danger to human life, health, or safety, disturbance of the public tranquillity, riot, or affray; such orders may be passed *ex parte* in emergencies or where circumstances do not admit of serving notice in due time. While orders generally remain in force for no more than two months, a Proviso allows the State Government, by notification, to extend an order for a further period not exceeding six months from the date on which the order would have, but for such order, expired to prevent danger to human life, health, or safety, or a riot or affray. A Magistrate may, either on his own motion or on the application of any person aggrieved, rescind or alter orders made by himself, a subordinate, or a predecessor-in-office, and the State Government may rescind or alter orders made under the Proviso to sub-section (4), provided the applicant is afforded an early opportunity to appear in person or by an advocate to show cause and any rejection is accompanied by recorded written reasons.
BNSS_S167:419f5b30334be1d289ff444e99f68d: Whenever a local inquiry is necessary for the purposes of section 164, section 165 or section 166, a District Magistrate or Sub-divisional Magistrate may depute any Magistrate subordinate to him to make the inquiry, furnish such written instructions as may seem necessary for his guidance, and declare by whom the whole or any part of the necessary expenses of the inquiry shall be paid. The report of the person so deputed may be read as evidence in the case. The Magistrate passing a decision may direct by whom costs incurred by any party to a proceeding under section 164, section 165 or section 166 shall be paid, whether by such party or by any other party to the proceeding, and whether in whole or in part or proportion; such costs may include any expenses incurred in respect of witnesses and of advocates' fees, which the Court may consider reasonable.
BNSS_S187:3731ab0913efcdcdf1879103bd9a41: When investigation cannot be completed in twenty-four hours, an officer (not below sub-inspector) forwards the accused and diary entries to a Magistrate, who may authorize detention for a term not exceeding fifteen days in the whole or in parts during the initial forty days or sixty days of the total detention period. Total detention is capped at ninety days for offences punishable with death, imprisonment for life or imprisonment for a term of ten years or more, and sixty days for other offences, after which the accused is released on bail if they furnish bail under Chapter XXXV. Police custody requires in-person production (unlike judicial custody via audio-video electronic means), cannot be authorized by a second class Magistrate unless specially empowered by the High Court, and is subject to Explanation I (detention until bail furnished), Explanation II (proof of production), and Provisos concerning women under eighteen years (remand home/recognized social institution) and approved detention locations. An Executive Magistrate may authorize up to seven days in the aggregate (subject to a Proviso for record transmission), Magistrates must record reasons for police custody (forwarding orders to the Chief Judicial Magistrate if not the CJM), and for summons-cases, investigation is stopped if not concluded within six months unless special reasons exist, which the Sessions Judge may vacate.
BNSS_S199:3a7d4f58a4ee9fab35f75ac39f082a: Section 199 stipulates that when an act is an offence by reason of anything which has been done and of a consequence which has ensued, the offence may be inquired into or tried by a Court. Such inquiry or trial is permissible by a Court within whose local jurisdiction such thing has been done or such consequence has ensued.
BNSS_S205:adf35d0cdae8f7c998ca9516fb8a30: Notwithstanding anything contained in the preceding provisions of the Chapter, the State Government may direct that any case or class of cases committed for trial in any district be tried in any sessions division. This power is subject to a Proviso that such direction must not be repugnant to any direction previously issued by the High Court or the Supreme Court under the Constitution, under this Sanhita, or any other law for the time being in force.
BNSS_S183:0ec1173f21b9a3dd918359e63578f4: A District Magistrate may record confessions or statements during investigation or before inquiry/trial, provided confessions may be recorded via audio-video electronic means in the presence of the accused's advocate and no confession shall be recorded by a police officer conferred with Magistrate powers. The Magistrate must ensure confessions are voluntary after explaining that the person is not bound to confess and it may be used as evidence; if the person refuses, the Magistrate shall not authorise detention in police custody, and the confession must be recorded per section 316 with a signed memorandum. For offences under sections 64, 65, 66, 67, 68, 69, 70, 71, 74, 75, 76, 77, 78, 79, or 124 of the Bharatiya Nyaya Sanhita, 2023, the Magistrate shall record the victim's statement as soon as the offence is noticed, subject to provisos that statements be recorded by a woman Magistrate (or a male Magistrate in the presence of a woman) and, for offences punishable with imprisonment for ten years or more, life, or death, the witness statement shall be recorded. Additional provisos mandate that for disabled persons, the Magistrate shall use an interpreter or special educator and audio-video electronic means (preferably mobile phone), such that the statement serves as examination-in-chief under section 142 of the Bharatiya Sakshya Adhiniyam, 2023, before the record is forwarded to the inquiring or trial Magistrate.
BNSS_S204:5638ae0c5f89214014ab3488dcd139: Under Section 204, offences may be inquired into or tried by any Court competent to inquire into or try any of the offences. This applies where offences committed by any person may be charged with, and tried at one trial for, each such offence by virtue of the provisions of section 242, section 243, or section 244. Additionally, it applies where the offence or offences committed by several persons may be charged with and tried together by virtue of the provisions of section 246.
BNSS_S193:050a3eb1538f596346627e9d7f68dc: Investigations must be completed without unnecessary delay, specifically within two months for offences under BNS sections 64, 65, 66, 67, 68, 70, 71 or POCSO Act sections 4, 6, 8, or 10. The officer in charge must forward a report to an empowered Magistrate detailing party names, nature of information, acquaintance, arrest/bond/bail status, section 190 custody, medical reports for BNS 64, 65, 66, 67, 68, 70, or 71, and electronic device custody, while informing the informant or victim of progress within ninety days. For section 190 cases, the report must include prosecution documents and section 180 statements, subject to the exclusion of irrelevant parts per sub-section (7) and a proviso that electronic communication for document supply under section 230 is considered duly served. Further investigation is permitted, with a proviso that investigation during trial requires the permission of the Court and must be completed within ninety days, which may be extended with the permission of the Court.
BNSS_S209:be2eb2a4736c84353584f826ed6299: When an offence alleged to have been committed in a territory outside India is being inquired into or tried under section 208, the Central Government may direct that copies of depositions made or exhibits produced, in physical form or in electronic form, before a judicial officer or a diplomatic or consular representative of India in or for that territory be received as evidence. This applies to any Court holding such inquiry or trial in which such Court might issue a commission for taking evidence as to the matters to which such depositions or exhibits relate.
BNSS_S195:ad01bc1946d58c195681e825e0696e: A police officer proceeding under section 194 may, by order in writing, summon two or more persons or any person acquainted with the facts of the case, who shall be bound to attend and answer truly all questions, except those exposing the person to a criminal charge, a penalty, or forfeiture. A proviso stipulates that no male person under the age of fifteen years or above the age of sixty years, a woman, a mentally or physically disabled person, or a person with acute illness shall be required to attend at any place other than where they reside, though a second proviso permits attendance at the police station if the person is willing. Finally, if the facts do not disclose a cognizable offence to which section 190 applies, such persons shall not be required by the police officer to attend a Magistrate's Court.
BNSS_S144:ed7344ae7c41e4e671e004eedd9d5f: A Magistrate of the first class may order a person having sufficient means to pay a monthly allowance, at such monthly rate as the Magistrate thinks fit, for a wife (including a divorced woman who has not remarried per the Explanation), a legitimate or illegitimate child (including a married or unmarried child unable to maintain itself, or a majority child, not being a married daughter, with physical or mental abnormality or injury), or a father or mother unable to maintain themselves, subject to a proviso that the father of a female child may be ordered to pay until she attains her majority if the husband, if married, is not possessed of sufficient means. Under the second and third provisos, the Magistrate may order interim maintenance and expenses of proceeding which the Magistrate considers reasonable, to be disposed of as far as possible within sixty days from the date of the service of notice, payable from the date of the order or the date of the application. For every breach without sufficient cause, the Magistrate may issue a warrant for levying the amount due or sentence the person to imprisonment for a term which may extend to one month or until payment if sooner made, subject to a proviso that an application is made within a period of one year from the date on which it became due and a further proviso that maintenance may be ordered despite a wife's refusal to live with her husband if there is just ground, which an Explanation defines as a husband contracting marriage with another woman or keeping a mistress. Per the fourth and fifth sub-sections, the Magistrate shall cancel the order, and no wife is entitled to an allowance, if she is living in adultery, refuses to live with her husband without sufficient reason, or they are living separately by mutual consent.
BNSS_S212:8b1fa22261c760fdc25fc45f74c013: Section 212 provides that any Chief Judicial Magistrate may, after taking cognizance of an offence, make over the case for inquiry or trial to any competent Magistrate subordinate to him. Additionally, any Magistrate of the first class empowered in this behalf by the Chief Judicial Magistrate may, after taking cognizance of an offence, make over the case for inquiry or trial to such other competent Magistrate as the Chief Judicial Magistrate may, by general or special order, specify.
BNSS_S214:1ef27f0c8e54b65a7ec57de4002ad5: Section 214 mandates that an Additional Sessions Judge shall try cases as the Sessions Judge of the division may make over to him for trial by general or special order. Furthermore, an Additional Sessions Judge shall try cases as the High Court may direct him to try by special order.
BNSS_S211:8f6c1137529affb8ab7d4bc0d20577: When a Magistrate takes cognizance of an offence under clause (c) of sub-section (1) of section 210, the accused shall, before any evidence is taken, be informed of their entitlement to have the case inquired into or tried by another Magistrate. If the accused or any of the accused objects to further proceedings before the Magistrate taking cognizance, the case shall be transferred to such other Magistrate as may be specified by the Chief Judicial Magistrate in this behalf.
BNSS_S207:f98e673ce4e933e61370d0848296fd: A Magistrate of the first class, seeing reason to believe a person within his local jurisdiction committed an offence outside such jurisdiction (whether within or outside India) which is triable in India but cannot be inquired into or tried locally under sections 197 to 205 (both inclusive) or any other law, may inquire into the offence and compel appearance. The Magistrate may send the person to the Magistrate having jurisdiction or, if the offence is not punishable with death or imprisonment for life and the person is ready and willing to give bail, take a bond or bail bond for appearance before the Magistrate having such jurisdiction. Where multiple Magistrates have such jurisdiction and the acting Magistrate cannot satisfy himself as to whom the person should be sent or bound to appear, the case shall be reported for the orders of the High Court.
BNSS_S216:51418723119d8a1a8a7e371cb1d1a3: Section 216 outlines the "Procedure for witnesses in case of threatening, etc," permitting a witness or any other person to file a complaint. This complaint relates specifically to an offence under section 232 of the Bharatiya Nyaya Sanhita, 2023.
BNSS_S190:08751a2616ef0d2c2ea85d5818145f: If investigation reveals sufficient evidence or reasonable ground, the officer in charge of the police station shall forward the accused under custody to a Magistrate empowered to take cognizance of the offence upon a police report and to try the accused or commit him for trial, or, if the offence is bailable and the accused is able to give security, shall take security for his appearance on a day fixed and for his attendance from day to day, provided that if the accused is not in custody, the officer shall take security and the Magistrate shall not refuse the report on the ground the accused is not in custody. The officer shall also send any necessary weapon or article and require the complainant or persons acquainted with the facts and circumstances of the case to execute a bond to appear and prosecute or give evidence. If the Court of the Chief Judicial Magistrate is mentioned in the bond, it includes any Court to which such Magistrate refers the case for inquiry or trial, provided reasonable notice of such reference is given, and the officer shall deliver a copy of the bond to one of the persons who executed it and send the original with his report to the Magistrate.
BNSS_S220:0ef6aab68a44b22f4bfeed0e236ba4: Section 220 stipulates that no Court shall take cognizance of an offence punishable under section 85 of the Bharatiya Nyaya Sanhita, 2023, except upon a police report of facts constituting such offence or a complaint. The complaint must be made by the person aggrieved, or her father, mother, brother, sister, or her father’s or mother’s brother or sister. Additionally, with the leave of the Court, any other person related to her by blood, marriage, or adoption may file the complaint.
BNSS_S224:d02ef0dd3d12c4d72ab2293783703d: Section 224 mandates the procedure when a complaint is made to a Magistrate not competent to take cognizance of the offence. If the complaint is in writing, the Magistrate shall return it for presentation to the proper Court with an endorsement to that effect. If the complaint is not in writing, the Magistrate shall direct the complainant to the proper Court.
BNSS_S221:accc92c55d14349974c720ee7c31a1: Section 221 mandates that no Court shall take cognizance of an offence punishable under section 67 of the Bharatiya Nyaya Sanhita, 2023, where the persons are in a marital relationship. An exception is provided only upon prima facie satisfaction of the facts which constitute the offence. Such cognizance is contingent upon a complaint having been filed or made by the wife against the husband.
BNSS_S218:c849a8a9ff4a981be716df67027ba7: No Court shall take cognizance of any offence alleged to have been committed by a Judge, Magistrate, or public servant (not removable from office save by or with Government sanction) while acting or purporting to act in the discharge of official duty without previous sanction from the Central Government (for Union affairs) or State Government (for State affairs), save as provided in the Lokpal and Lokayuktas Act, 2013. This provision includes a Proviso substituting "Central Government" for "State Government" during a Proclamation under article 356(1) of the Constitution, a Proviso requiring a sanction decision within "one hundred and twenty days" (otherwise deemed accorded), and a Proviso exempting sanctions for offences under sections 64, 65, 66, 68, 69, 70, 71, 74, 75, 76, 77, 78, 79, 143, 199 or 200 of the Bharatiya Nyaya Sanhita, 2023. Cognizance of offences by members of the Armed Forces of the Union, or specified members of Forces charged with the maintenance of public order (via State Government notification), requires Central Government sanction, which remains the mandatory authority during a Proclamation under article 356(1) of the Constitution. The Central or State Government may determine the person, manner, and offence(s) for prosecution, and specify the Court before which the trial is to be held.
BNSS_S213:c975c55cd8caf46905dbd29c2199e7: Except as otherwise expressly provided by this Sanhita or by any other law for the time being in force, no Court of Session shall take cognizance of any offence as a Court of original jurisdiction. Such cognizance is permitted only if the case has been committed to the Court of Session by a Magistrate under this Sanhita.
BNSS_S206:f9fada7472ac5f78c1c93a355ba886: Where two or more Courts have taken cognizance of the same offence and a question arises as to which ought to inquire into or try that offence, the High Court shall decide. If the Courts are subordinate to the same High Court, that High Court decides; if the Courts are not subordinate to the same High Court, the High Court within the local limits of whose appellate criminal jurisdiction the proceedings were first commenced shall decide. In the latter case, all other proceedings in respect of that offence shall be discontinued.
BNSS_S226:8a25abd287596dc7a29fe94342047c: Under Section 226, if the Magistrate, after considering statements on oath (if any) of the complainant and witnesses and the result of the inquiry or investigation (if any) under section 225, is of the opinion that there is no sufficient ground for proceeding, he shall dismiss the complaint. In every such case, the Magistrate shall briefly record his reasons for so doing.
BNSS_S225:6a8672c5b0ac3128658dfcfc7d6aa0: Upon receiving a complaint authorized for cognizance or made over under section 212, a Magistrate may postpone the issue of process, particularly if the accused resides beyond the Magistrate's jurisdiction, to determine if there is sufficient ground for proceeding through an inquiry or an investigation by a police officer or other person. A proviso to sub-section (1) prohibits such investigation if the offence is triable exclusively by the Court of Session, or if the complaint was not made by a Court and the complainant and witnesses have not been examined on oath under section 223. Under sub-section (2), the Magistrate may take evidence on oath, subject to a proviso that if the offence is triable exclusively by the Court of Session, the Magistrate shall call upon the complainant to produce all witnesses and examine them on oath. Sub-section (3) stipulates that if an investigation is conducted by a person not being a police officer, they shall have all powers conferred on an officer in charge of a police station, except the power to arrest without warrant.
BNSS_S222:fbfdc82968c29ff96f6f32541bffec: Courts take cognizance of offences under section 356 of the Bharatiya Nyaya Sanhita, 2023 only upon a complaint by the aggrieved person, with a Proviso allowing another person to complain with Court leave if the aggrieved is a child, of unsound mind, has intellectual disability, is incapacitated by sickness or infirmity, or is a woman restricted by local customs from appearing in public. For offences under section 356 of the Bharatiya Nyaya Sanhita, 2023 against the President of India, Vice-President of India, Governor, Administrator of a Union territory, Union/State/UT Minister, or public servants regarding their public functions, a Court of Session may take cognizance without commitment upon a written complaint by the Public Prosecutor detailing the facts, nature of the offence, and notice particulars. Such complaints require previous sanction from the State Government (for Governors, State Ministers, or State public servants) or the Central Government (all other cases) and must be made within six months from the date the offence was alleged to have been committed. This section does not affect the aggrieved person's right to complain before a Magistrate or the Magistrate's power to take cognizance.
BNSS_S215:1aff6f7cf58826aed90477c031ad85: Section 215 stipulates that no Court shall take cognizance of any offence punishable under sections 206 to 223 (both inclusive but excluding section 209) of the Bharatiya Nyaya Sanhita, 2023, or any abetment of, attempt to commit, or criminal conspiracy to commit such offence, except on a complaint in writing from the concerned public servant, a public servant administratively subordinate to him, or an authorized public servant. Cognizance is similarly restricted for offences under sections 229 to 233 (both inclusive), 236, 237, 242 to 248 (both inclusive), and 267 committed in or in relation to any Court proceeding, or offences described in sub-section (1) of section 336, or punishable under sub-section (2) of section 340 or section 342 regarding documents produced or given in evidence. For these offences, including any abetment, attempt, or criminal conspiracy to commit them, cognizance is permitted only upon a complaint in writing of that Court, by an officer of the Court authorized in writing by that Court, or of a subordinate Court.
BNSS_S217:7a7fc6d4cd6be6d30f058a9702d436: No Court shall take cognizance of offences punishable under Chapter VII, section 196, section 299, or sub-section (1) of section 353 of the Bharatiya Nyaya Sanhita, 2023, including their criminal conspiracy or abetment under section 47, except with previous sanction of the Central Government or State Government; similarly, offences under section 197 or sub-sections (2) or (3) of section 353 and their criminal conspiracy require sanction from the Central Government, State Government, or District Magistrate. For criminal conspiracy punishable under sub-section (2) of section 61 of the Bharatiya Nyaya Sanhita, 2023—excluding conspiracies to commit offences punishable with death, imprisonment for life or rigorous imprisonment for a term of two years or upwards—written consent from the State Government or District Magistrate is required, except as per the Proviso where no consent is necessary if section 215 applies. Before according sanction under sub-sections (1) or (2), or giving consent under sub-section (3), the Central Government, State Government, or District Magistrate may order a preliminary investigation by a police officer not being below the rank of Inspector, who shall have the powers referred to in sub-section (3) of section 174.
BNSS_S201:489508824cd4cb8c9678db54d6d911: Section 201 stipulates that offences of dacoity, dacoity with murder, belonging to a gang of dacoits, or escaping from custody may be inquired into or tried where the offence was committed or the accused person is found. Kidnapping or abduction may be tried where the person was kidnapped, abducted, conveyed, concealed, or detained, while theft, extortion, or robbery may be tried where the offence was committed or the stolen property which is the subject of the offence was possessed by any person committing it or by any person who received or retained such property knowing or having reason to believe it to be stolen property. Criminal misappropriation or criminal breach of trust may be tried where the offence was committed or where any part of the property which is the subject of the offence was received or retained, or was required to be returned or accounted for, by the accused person, and offences including the possession of stolen property may be tried where the offence was committed or where the stolen property was possessed by any person who received or retained it knowing or having reason to believe it to be stolen property.
BNSS_S223:2b0a71a7c2a3c0a31520b9f77af235: A Magistrate taking cognizance of an offence on complaint shall examine the complainant and witnesses upon oath, reducing the substance to writing signed by the complainant, witnesses, and the Magistrate. Provisos state that no cognizance shall be taken without giving the accused an opportunity of being heard, and exempt examination for written complaints from a Court or a public servant acting in the discharge of official duties, or cases made over under section 212 (where the receiving Magistrate need not re-examine). Furthermore, cognizance against a public servant for offences alleged to have been committed in the discharge of official functions or duties requires the public servant be given an opportunity to make assertions as to the situation and a report containing facts and circumstances from their superior officer be received.
BNSS_S229:fa54dfd6f0c86d2749fc0ac439d5cc: If a Magistrate taking cognizance of a "petty offence" determines a case may be summarily disposed of under section 283 or section 284, they shall issue summons requiring the accused to appear in person or by an advocate, or allow a plea of guilty via post, messenger, or authorized advocate, subject to a proviso that the specified fine shall not exceed five thousand rupees. A "petty offence" is defined as any offence punishable only with fine not exceeding five thousand rupees, excluding offences under the Motor Vehicles Act, 1988 (59 of 1988) or any law providing for convicting the accused in his absence on a plea of guilty. Additionally, the State Government may, by notification, empower a Magistrate to exercise these powers for offences compoundable under section 359 or offences punishable with imprisonment for a term not exceeding three months, or with fine, or with both, where the Magistrate deems that the imposition of fine only would meet the ends of justice.
BNSS_S232:c2c78f458a69f13602060ed95516c0: When a case instituted on a police report or otherwise involves an offence triable exclusively by the Court of Session, the Magistrate shall, after complying with section 230 or section 231, commit the case to the Court of Session, remand the accused to custody subject to bail provisions until commitment and during/until the conclusion of trial, send the case record, documents, and articles to the Court, and notify the Public Prosecutor. The first Proviso stipulates that proceedings shall be completed within ninety days from the date of taking cognizance, extendable by the Magistrate for a period not exceeding one hundred and eighty days for reasons recorded in writing. The second Proviso mandates that any application filed by the accused, the victim, or any person authorised by such person be forwarded to the Court of Session with the committal of the case.
BNSS_S231:5e9c5ca9b8990daff42456601bfb4b: In a case instituted otherwise than on a police report where the Magistrate issuing process under section 227 determines the offence is triable exclusively by the Court of Session, the Magistrate shall forthwith furnish to the accused, free of cost, copies of statements recorded under section 223 or section 225, statements and confessions recorded under section 180 or section 183, and any documents produced before the Magistrate on which the prosecution proposes to rely. The section includes a proviso that if a document is voluminous, the Magistrate may instead direct that the accused be allowed to inspect it personally or through an advocate in Court, and a further proviso stating that supply of documents in electronic form shall be considered as duly furnished.
BNSS_S233:bb92288eadac23517f74690f694dbc: If a Magistrate, during an inquiry or trial of a complaint case, finds a police investigation is in progress regarding the subject-matter offence, the Magistrate shall stay the proceedings and call for a report from the investigating police officer. If a report is made under section 193 and the Magistrate takes cognizance of an offence against an accused in the complaint case, the Magistrate shall inquire into or try together the complaint case and the case arising from the police report as if both cases were instituted on a police report. However, if the police report does not relate to any accused in the complaint case or if the Magistrate does not take cognizance of any offence on the police report, the Magistrate shall proceed with the stayed inquiry or trial in accordance with the provisions of this Sanhita.
BNSS_S238:b316c913ae74d24b65c1584986f679: Section 238 provides that no error in stating the offence or the particulars required to be stated in the charge, and no omission to state the offence or those particulars, shall be regarded as material at any stage of the case. Such errors or omissions are only material if the accused was in fact misled by them and it has occasioned a failure of justice. The section includes Illustrations (a) through (e) regarding charges under the Bharatiya Nyaya Sanhita, 2023—specifically involving counterfeit coin, cheating, and murder—to demonstrate when the Court may infer materiality.
BNSS_S234:40d6e299bafb37c46104c2adc33368: Section 234 mandates that every charge under this Sanhita must state the offence, using its specific name if provided by law, or sufficient detail from the definition to give the accused notice, and must specify the law and section of the law violated, written in the language of the Court. The making of a charge is equivalent to a statement that every legal condition required by law to constitute the offence was fulfilled. Where an accused is liable to "enhanced punishment" or "punishment of a different kind" due to a "previous conviction," the "fact, date and place" of such conviction must be stated in the charge, which the Court may add at any time before sentence is passed. Illustrations clarify that charges are equivalent to statements that acts fall within specific sections of the Bharatiya Nyaya Sanhita, 2023 (e.g., sections 100, 101, 118, 122, 219) and do not fall within "general exceptions," "exceptions," or "provisos."
BNSS_S236:1b2a08ca9f11c70b5dafcf0438f7ef: Section 236 stipulates that if particulars under sections 234 and 235 do not provide the accused sufficient notice of the matter charged, the charge must additionally contain particulars of the manner in which the alleged offence was committed. Illustrations clarify that while the manner is not required for theft or murder, it is mandatory for cheating, obstructing a public servant in the discharge of public functions, and giving false evidence (where the specific false portion must be stated). For charges involving disobeying a direction of the law with intent to save another from punishment, the charge must explicitly set out the disobedience charged and the law infringed.
BNSS_S240:733e275346a25986f1da91cab7b4c8: Upon a charge being altered or added to by the Court after the commencement of the trial, the prosecutor and the accused are permitted to recall or re-summon, and examine with reference to such alteration or addition, any witness already examined. The Court may refuse this request if, for reasons to be recorded in writing, it considers the recall or re-examination is for the purpose of vexation or delay or for defeating the ends of justice. Additionally, the prosecutor and the accused are allowed to call any further witness whom the Court may think to be material.
BNSS_S227:2b762abf319c3a12b179348c19c123: If a Magistrate taking cognizance of an offence finds sufficient ground for proceeding, he shall issue summons in a summons-case, or may issue a warrant or a summons in a warrant-case to cause the accused to appear before himself or some other Magistrate having jurisdiction, with a proviso that summons or warrants may also be issued through electronic means. No summons or warrant shall be issued until a list of the prosecution witnesses has been filed, and in proceedings instituted upon a complaint made in writing, every summons or warrant issued under sub-section (1) shall be accompanied by a copy of such complaint. No process shall be issued until process-fees or other fees are paid, failing which the Magistrate may dismiss the complaint if not paid within a reasonable time, and nothing in this section shall be deemed to affect the provisions of section 90.
BNSS_S235:e2ef4d3cf04df851af81369ab3d1d2: Section 235(1) mandates that the charge contain particulars regarding the time, place, the person (if any) against whom, or the thing (if any) in respect of which the alleged offence was committed, to give the accused reasonable notice. For charges of criminal breach of trust or dishonest misappropriation of money or other movable property, subsection (2) provides it shall be sufficient to specify the gross sum or describe the movable property and the dates between which the offence is alleged to have been committed without specifying particular items or exact dates, which shall be deemed a charge of one offence within the meaning of section 242. This is subject to a Proviso stating that the time included between the first and last of such dates shall not exceed one year.
BNSS_S241:287e264475369c637ca9cbff20fd32: Under Section 241(1), for every distinct offence of which any person is accused, there shall be a separate charge, and every such charge shall be tried separately. A Proviso allows the Magistrate to try all or any number of the charges framed against such person together, provided the accused person, by an application in writing, so desires and the Magistrate is of opinion that such person is not likely to be prejudiced thereby. Sub-section (2) stipulates that nothing in sub-section (1) shall affect the operation of the provisions of sections 242, 243, 244 and 246.
BNSS_S244:26c178d9e15ddd008e20c9215d36d9: Section 244 provides that where a single act or series of acts makes it doubtful which of several offences the provable facts constitute, the accused may be charged with all, any, or in the alternative with some of such offences, and any number of such charges may be tried at once. If the accused is charged with one offence but evidence shows they committed a different offence for which they might have been charged under sub-section (1), they may be convicted of the offence shown, even if they were not charged with it. This section includes three Illustrations.
BNSS_S248:126ee49017a30ded998bc159ac0792: Section 248 stipulates that in every trial before a Court of Session, the prosecution shall be conducted by a Public Prosecutor. This section contains no Provisos, Exceptions, or Explanations.
BNSS_S242:17a098b971cec202fd07fd37dc7db4: A person accused of multiple offences of the same kind committed within twelve months from the first to the last, whether in respect of the same person or not, may be charged with and tried at one trial for any number not exceeding five. Offences are of the same kind if they are punishable with the same amount of punishment under the same section of the Bharatiya Nyaya Sanhita, 2023 or any special or local law. The Proviso specifies that an offence punishable under sub-section (2) of section 303 of the Bharatiya Nyaya Sanhita, 2023 is deemed the same kind as an offence under section 305, and any offence under the said Sanhita or special or local law is deemed the same kind as an attempt to commit such offence, when such an attempt is an offence.
BNSS_S219:e42af9335ed970d32136990cf9308f: No Court shall take cognizance of offences punishable under sections 81 to 84 of the Bharatiya Nyaya Sanhita, 2023, except upon a complaint by an aggrieved person, subject to provisos allowing others to complain with Court leave for children, persons of unsound mind, persons with intellectual disabilities requiring higher support needs, those of sickness or infirmity, or women exempt from public appearance according to local customs and manners; specifically, section 82 allows complaints by certain relatives or those related by blood, marriage or adoption, while section 84 limits the aggrieved person to the husband. Under sub-section (3), the Court must provide notice and a reasonable opportunity of being heard to any appointed/declared guardian in proviso (a) cases, and under proviso (b), a husband in the Armed Forces may authorize another person to complain if a Commanding Officer certifies leave cannot be granted, via a written, countersigned authorization and certificate presumed genuine under sub-section (5). No Court shall take cognizance of an offence under section 64 of the Bharatiya Nyaya Sanhita, 2023, involving sexual intercourse by a man with his own wife, the wife being under eighteen years of age, if more than one year has elapsed from the date of the commission of the offence.
BNSS_S250:0452f20042f20d89740a8251ad483e: Under Section 250(1), the accused may prefer an application for discharge within a period of sixty days from the date of commitment of the case under section 232. Pursuant to Section 250(2), if the Judge, after considering the record of the case, submitted documents, and hearing submissions from the accused and the prosecution, finds there is not sufficient ground for proceeding against the accused, he shall discharge the accused. The Judge must record his reasons for so doing.
BNSS_S252:4e2b1c4271412579c5b1c73568330d: Under Section 252, if the accused pleads guilty, the Judge shall record the plea. The Judge may then, in his discretion, convict the accused thereon.
BNSS_S239:37f44016b2e7a9813fdfbbf04bf204: Any Court may alter or add to any charge at any time before judgment is pronounced, provided every such alteration or addition is read and explained to the accused. If the alteration or addition is not likely, in the opinion of the Court, to prejudice the accused in his defence or the prosecutor in the conduct of the case, the Court may proceed with the trial as if the altered or added charge had been the original charge; however, if it is likely to prejudice the accused or the prosecutor, the Court may direct a new trial or adjourn the trial for such period as may be necessary. If the offence stated in the altered or added charge is one for the prosecution of which previous sanction is necessary, the case shall not be proceeded with until such sanction is obtained, unless sanction has been already obtained for a prosecution on the same facts as those on which the altered or added charge is founded.
BNSS_S243:0ad5ef8e07bf4721726c92ccd00aa3: Section 243 provides that if more than one offence is committed by the same person in one series of acts so connected together as to form the same transaction, they may be charged with, and tried at one trial for, every such offence, including offences of falsification of accounts committed to facilitate or conceal criminal breach of trust or dishonest misappropriation of property as provided in sub-section (2) of section 235 or sub-section (1) of section 242. A person may also be charged and tried at one trial if acts fall within two or more separate definitions of any law in force, or when several acts combined constitute a different offence as well as the individual offences constituted by those acts. This section is subject to the provision that nothing contained therein shall affect section 9 of the Bharatiya Nyaya Sanhita, 2023.
BNSS_S253:2e01dd8f15427f93aa2c881a91bff3: If the accused refuses to plead, does not plead, claims to be tried, or is not convicted under section 252, the Judge shall fix a date for the examination of witnesses. Additionally, on the application of the prosecution, the Judge may issue any process for compelling the attendance of any witness or the production of any document or other thing.
BNSS_S247:f0750f98d0d17e61b1e26ad74e774d: When a charge containing more than one head is framed against the same person and a conviction has been had on one or more of them, the complainant or the officer conducting the prosecution may, with the consent of the Court, withdraw the remaining charge or charges, or the Court may, of its own accord, stay the inquiry into or trial of such charge or charges. Such withdrawal shall have the effect of an acquittal on those charge or charges, unless the conviction be set aside. If the conviction is set aside, the Court, subject to the order of the Court setting aside the conviction, may proceed with the inquiry into or trial of the charge or charges so withdrawn.
BNSS_S237:e948ae83fea2d616327920901d7e82: Section 237 stipulates that in every charge, words used in describing an offence shall be deemed to have been used in the sense attached to them respectively. Such words shall be taken in the sense attached to them by the law under which such offence is punishable.
BNSS_S249:46cedb8685cfc1d5aedc0084e44cee: When the accused appears or is brought before the Court in pursuance of a commitment of the case under section 232, or under any other law for the time being in force, the prosecutor shall open his case. The prosecutor shall open the case by describing the charge brought against the accused and stating by what evidence he proposes to prove the guilt of the accused.
BNSS_S254:c4716f6b293458d8be2955599bbe7c: On the fixed date, the Judge shall proceed to take all evidence produced in support of the prosecution, with a Proviso stating that evidence of a witness may be recorded by audio-video electronic means. The deposition of evidence of any public servant may also be taken through audio-video electronic means. The Judge may, in his discretion, permit the cross-examination of any witness to be deferred until any other witness or witnesses have been examined or recall any witness for further cross-examination.
BNSS_S245:0dfbff3ab7258d1cf76ad87dcc8c91: Section 245 provides that a person may be convicted of a minor offence, even if not separately charged, if a proved combination of particulars from a charged offence constitutes a complete minor offence or if proved facts reduce the charged offence to a minor offence. Furthermore, a person charged with an offence may be convicted of an attempt to commit such offence even if the attempt is not separately charged.
