However, subsection (4) stipulates that no conviction of a minor offence is authorized where the conditions requisite for the initiation of proceedings in respect of that minor offence have not been satisfied.
BNSS_S256:cad668f038d9f3621b9fc8d1519b2d: Where the accused is not acquitted under section 255, he shall be called upon to enter on his defence and adduce any evidence in support thereof. Any written statement put in by the accused shall be filed with the record by the Judge. The Judge shall issue process for compelling the attendance of any witness or the production of any document or thing upon the accused's application, unless reasons are recorded that such application is made for the purpose of vexation or delay or for defeating the ends of justice.
BNSS_S258:296113427b2676bb6123b7a2da32ef: Following the hearing of arguments and points of law (if any), the Judge shall deliver a judgment within thirty days from the date of completion of arguments, which may be extended to forty-five days for reasons to be recorded in writing. If the accused is convicted, the Judge shall, unless proceeding in accordance with the provisions of section 401, hear the accused on the questions of sentence and then pass sentence according to law.
BNSS_S257:a3ddb3b54667ff317ed1914bcd72a7: Upon completion of the examination of the witnesses (if any) for the defence, the prosecutor shall sum up his case, and the accused or his advocate shall be entitled to reply. The section includes a Proviso stating that where any point of law is raised by the accused or his advocate, the prosecution may, with the permission of the Judge, make submissions with regard to such point of law.
BNSS_S262:73c98c52128b75d71c77b1e84d0bf8: The accused may prefer an application for discharge within a period of sixty days from the date of supply of copies of documents under section 230. If the Magistrate, upon considering the police report and documents sent under section 193, conducting any necessary examination of the accused physically or through audio-video electronic means, and granting the prosecution and accused an opportunity of being heard, considers the charge against the accused to be groundless, he shall discharge the accused and record his reasons.
BNSS_S261:ea9ea281e722563e3e13c38d5bb6f9: In any warrant-case instituted on a police report, when the accused appears or is brought before a Magistrate at the commencement of the trial, the Magistrate must ensure compliance. Specifically, the Magistrate shall satisfy himself that he has complied with the provisions of section 230.
BNSS_S259:013e0eaea6a1eb5dfaa0b6ba7cb1e2: If a previous conviction is charged under sub-section (7) of section 234 and the accused does not admit the alleged previous conviction, the Judge, after convicting the accused under section 252 or section 258, may take evidence in respect of the alleged previous conviction and shall record a finding thereon. The section includes a proviso that no such charge shall be read out, the accused shall not be asked to plead, and the previous conviction shall not be referred to by the prosecution or in any evidence adduced until the accused has been convicted under section 252 or section 258.
BNSS_S251:1a756e200d73adfd28476cb287a949: If the Judge finds grounds for presuming an offence not exclusively triable by the Court of Session, he may frame a charge and transfer the case to the Chief Judicial Magistrate or any other Judicial Magistrate of the first class, directing the accused to appear for trial according to the procedure for warrant-cases instituted on a police report. For offences exclusively triable by the Court, the Judge shall frame a charge in writing within a period of sixty days from the date of first hearing on charge. Under sub-section (2), charges framed under clause (b) of sub-section (1) shall be read and explained to the accused, physically or through audio-video electronic means, and the accused shall be asked whether he pleads guilty or claims to be tried.
BNSS_S264:517540c5cdd85371838079e2245ac1: Under Section 264, if the accused pleads guilty, the Magistrate shall record the plea. The Magistrate may then, in his discretion, convict the accused thereon.
BNSS_S266:59b28e008213aa76ce1d68dd39405e: The accused shall enter upon his defence and produce evidence, with any written statement filed by the Magistrate with the record. The Magistrate shall issue process for compelling the attendance of any witness for examination or cross-examination, or the production of any document or other thing, unless the application is recorded in writing as being for the purpose of vexation, delay, or defeating the ends of justice. This is subject to a first Proviso stating attendance of witnesses already cross-examined shall not be compelled unless necessary for the ends of justice, and a second Proviso allowing examination via audio-video electronic means at a State Government notified designated place. Additionally, the Magistrate may require that reasonable expenses incurred by the witness be deposited in Court before summoning.
BNSS_S263:8075eb95e5dc7f30bbae63c73a4b9c: Upon consideration, examination, and hearing, if the Magistrate finds ground for presuming the accused committed an offence triable under this Chapter which the Magistrate is competent to try and could adequately punish, he shall frame in writing a charge against the accused within a period of sixty days from the date of first hearing on charge. The charge shall then be read and explained to the accused, and he shall be asked whether he pleads guilty of the offence charged or claims to be tried.
BNSS_S260:4b9144bd7acb388f84a5dd89247924: A Court of Session taking cognizance under sub-section (2) of section 222 shall follow the procedure for trial of warrant-cases instituted otherwise than on a police report before a Court of Magistrate, with a Proviso that the victim be examined as a prosecution witness unless the Court records reasons otherwise, and trials may be held *in camera*. Upon discharge or acquittal where no reasonable cause for accusation existed, the Court may direct the accuser (excluding the President, the Vice-President, the Governor of a State, or the Administrator of a Union territory) to show cause and may order compensation not exceeding five thousand rupees for reasons to be recorded. Such compensation is recovered as a fine imposed by a Magistrate, and while the accuser is not exempted from civil or criminal liability, a Proviso stipulates that any amount paid shall be taken into account in subsequent civil suits. The person ordered to pay may appeal to the High Court, and payment shall not be made until the appeal period elapses or the appeal is decided.
BNSS_S270:c5823c29904e9bf054d12b71743e7c: Section 270 stipulates that the accused shall be called upon to enter upon his defence and produce his evidence. Furthermore, the provisions of section 266 shall apply to the case within the context of the Conclusion of trial.
BNSS_S272:46d8624937e6dd0736d6dd72edb63e: Under Section 272, when proceedings have been instituted upon complaint and the complainant is absent on any day fixed for the hearing of the case, the Magistrate may, in his discretion, discharge the accused if the offence may be lawfully compounded or is not a cognizable offence. This discharge may occur at any time before the charge has been framed, notwithstanding anything hereinbefore contained. Such action is contingent upon the Magistrate first giving thirty days’ time to the complainant to be present.
BNSS_S267:f366f9390df5d6f5830c1f25078d8b: In any warrant-case instituted otherwise than on a police report, when the accused appears or is brought before a Magistrate, the Magistrate shall proceed to hear the prosecution and take all such evidence as may be produced in support of the prosecution. On the application of the prosecution, the Magistrate may issue a summons to any of its witnesses directing him to attend or to produce any document or other thing.
BNSS_S274:45ae334dbe7f126297f6957f22c2a4: In a summons-case, the Magistrate shall state the particulars of the offence to the accused and ask whether he pleads guilty or has any defence to make, without the necessity to frame a formal charge. A Proviso stipulates that if the Magistrate considers the accusation groundless, he shall, after recording reasons in writing, release the accused, and such release shall have the effect of discharge.
BNSS_S265:4927f2634093d6218641212928a1b2: If the accused refuses to plead, does not plead, claims to be tried, or the Magistrate does not convict under section 264, the Magistrate shall fix a date for the examination of witnesses, provided the Magistrate shall supply in advance to the accused the statement of witnesses recorded during investigation by the police. The Magistrate may, on the application of the prosecution, issue a summons to any witness to attend or produce any document or other thing. On the fixed date, the Magistrate shall take all evidence produced in support of the prosecution, subject to provisos that the Magistrate may permit the cross-examination of any witness to be deferred or recall any witness for further cross-examination, and that the examination of a witness may be conducted by audio-video electronic means at the designated place to be notified by the State Government.
BNSS_S275:58f3d6ae57a40d2a7723a0c3512e3d: If the accused pleads guilty, the Magistrate shall record the plea as nearly as possible in the words used by the accused. The Magistrate may, in his discretion, convict the accused thereon.
BNSS_S276:5f16a3e1ac2b50004ab1c2192dd2c5: Section 276 provides for conviction on plea of guilty in absence of accused in petty cases where a summons has been issued under section 229. The accused may transmit to the Magistrate, by post or messenger, a letter containing his plea and the amount of fine specified in the summons. The Magistrate may, in his discretion, convict the accused in his absence and sentence him to pay the fine specified in the summons, adjusting the transmitted amount, or similarly convict and sentence the accused if an authorized advocate pleads guilty, provided the Magistrate records the plea as nearly as possible in the words used by the advocate.
BNSS_S273:7214e97f5f16caf32b73ab9e41adc4: In cases instituted upon complaint or information where a Magistrate, upon discharging or acquitting an accused of an offence triable by a Magistrate, finds no reasonable ground for the accusation, the Magistrate may order the complainant or informant to pay compensation not exceeding the amount of fine he is empowered to impose, with default resulting in simple imprisonment for a period not exceeding thirty days subject to sub-section (6) of section 8 of the Bharatiya Nyaya Sanhita, 2023. Such orders do not exempt the person from civil or criminal liability, though a proviso specifies that any amount paid shall be taken into account in awarding compensation in any subsequent civil suit relating to the same matter. Applying to summons-cases and warrant-cases, a complainant or informant ordered by a Magistrate of the second class to pay compensation exceeding two thousand rupees may appeal as if convicted, with compensation payment regulated by the appeal period/decision or the expiration of one month if no appeal is applicable.
BNSS_S284:26eb4ed9c2bf5c8f6e2b1fdb358359: Section 284 empowers the High Court to confer power upon any Magistrate invested with the powers of a Magistrate of the second class to try summarily specific offences. This authority applies to offences punishable only with fine or with imprisonment for a term not exceeding six months with or without fine. It further extends to any abetment of or attempt to commit any such offence.
BNSS_S277:4abcb8c6490c5c1998f5cb367b51b6: If the Magistrate does not convict the accused under section 275 or section 276, the Magistrate shall proceed to hear the prosecution and take all evidence produced in support of the prosecution, and hear the accused and take all evidence produced in his defence. On application by the prosecution or the accused, the Magistrate may issue a summons to any witness directing him to attend or to produce any document or other thing. Before summoning any witness on such application, the Magistrate may require that the reasonable expenses of the witness incurred in attending for the purposes of the trial be deposited in Court.
BNSS_S285:dbe35fc6da259ca9c8eb61232f2d60: For procedure for summary trials under this Chapter, the procedure specified in this Sanhita for the trial of summons-case shall be followed, except as hereinafter mentioned. No sentence of imprisonment for a term exceeding three months shall be passed in the case of any conviction under this Chapter.
BNSS_S282:2736d72a7ed49679ea13f829158648: Section 282 empowers a Magistrate, during the trial of a summons-case relating to an offence punishable with imprisonment for a term exceeding six months, to convert the proceeding into a warrant-case if it appears in the interests of justice. The Magistrate may proceed to re-hear the case in the manner provided by this Sanhita for the trial of warrant-cases. This mechanism includes the authority to recall any witness who may have been examined.
BNSS_S279:f29fb86618b5c26cb30d8c1a95a050: If summons has been issued on complaint and the complainant fails to appear on the appointed day or any subsequent adjourned day, the Magistrate shall, after giving thirty days’ time to the complainant to be present, acquit the accused, unless the Magistrate thinks it proper to adjourn the hearing. A proviso allows the Magistrate to dispense with the complainant's personal attendance and proceed with the case if they are represented by an advocate or the officer conducting the prosecution, or if the Magistrate deems personal attendance unnecessary. These provisions also apply to cases where the non-appearance of the complainant is due to his death.
BNSS_S278:751fec3cf80529335ee5e50f892e66: If the Magistrate, upon taking evidence referred to in section 277 and any further evidence produced of his own motion, finds the accused not guilty, he shall record an order of acquittal. Where the Magistrate finds the accused guilty and does not proceed in accordance with section 364 or section 401, he shall pass sentence upon him according to law. Additionally, under section 275 or section 278, a Magistrate may convict the accused of any offence triable under this Chapter based on facts admitted or proved, regardless of the nature of the complaint or summons, provided the Magistrate is satisfied that the accused would not be prejudiced.
BNSS_S288:1c04e8079eb86872939915f67b907f: Section 288 mandates that every record and judgment shall be written in the language of the Court. The High Court may authorise any Magistrate empowered to try offences summarily to prepare the record, judgment, or both, through an officer appointed by the Chief Judicial Magistrate, provided the resulting record or judgment is signed by such Magistrate.
BNSS_S287:47eff482da82387ba3f787c3f2fece: In every case tried summarily in which the accused does not plead guilty, the Magistrate shall record the substance of the evidence. Furthermore, the Magistrate shall record a judgment containing a brief statement of the reasons for the finding.
BNSS_S269:f5a47239eed2faea51cc2a8cb527ce: If a Magistrate, competent to try an offence triable under this Chapter and of the opinion that such offence could be adequately punished by him, finds grounds for presuming the accused committed the offence, he shall frame in writing a charge to be read and explained to the accused, who may be convicted at the Magistrate's discretion upon a guilty plea. If the accused refuses to plead, does not plead, claims to be tried, or is not convicted under sub-section (3), they must state at the commencement of the next hearing, or forthwith if the Magistrate records reasons in writing, whether they wish to cross-examine prosecution witnesses for recall, cross-examination, and re-examination (if any) before being discharged. Following the discharge of remaining witnesses after cross-examination and re-examination (if any), if witnesses under sub-sections (5) and (6) cannot be secured despite reasonable measures under this Sanhita, the Magistrate may close the prosecution evidence for reasons recorded in writing and proceed on the basis of materials on record, deeming such witnesses not examined for not being available.
BNSS_S286:05583de1d1afa511e9a46d1edef206: Under Section 286, in every case tried summarily, the Magistrate shall enter specific particulars in a form directed by the State Government. These particulars include the serial number of the case, date of commission of the offence, date of report or complaint, name of the complainant (if any), and the name, parentage, and residence of the accused. The record must further detail the offence complained of and the offence (if any) proved—including the value of the property for cases under clause (i), clause (ii), or clause (iii) of sub-section (1) of section 283—the plea of the accused and his examination (if any), the finding, the sentence or other final order, and the date on which proceedings terminated.
BNSS_S271:ca00c50c1e45428f93552a3bcb4083: If the Magistrate finds the accused not guilty in any case under this Chapter where a charge has been framed, they shall record an order of acquittal; if found guilty, and not proceeding under section 364 or section 401, the Magistrate shall, after hearing the accused on the question of sentence, pass sentence according to law. Where a previous conviction is charged under sub-section (7) of section 234 and not admitted, the Magistrate may, after conviction, take evidence in respect of the alleged previous conviction and record a finding thereon. This is subject to a Proviso that no such charge shall be read out, the accused asked to plead, or the previous conviction referred to by the prosecution or evidence, until the accused has been convicted under sub-section (2).
BNSS_S281:41d1964516bd9d36ff7bfe9f1b0564: In any summons-case instituted otherwise than upon complaint, a Magistrate of the first class or, with the previous sanction of the Chief Judicial Magistrate, any other Judicial Magistrate, may, for reasons to be recorded by him, stop the proceedings at any stage without pronouncing any judgment. Where such stoppage of proceedings is made after the evidence of the principal witnesses has been recorded, the Magistrate may pronounce a judgment of acquittal, and in any other case, may release the accused, which release shall have the effect of discharge.
BNSS_S294:ce717dce8e6727df8e9fc862b6cf73: The Court shall deliver its judgment in open Court in terms of section 293. The judgment must be signed by the presiding officer of the Court. This section contains no Provisos, Exceptions, or Explanations.
BNSS_S290:116b13b26cead77fb0ce86338f8088: A person accused of an offence may file an application for plea bargaining within thirty days from the date of framing of charge, accompanied by an affidavit stating the application is voluntary, the accused understands the nature and extent of punishment, and they have not previously been convicted of the same offence. Following notice to the Public Prosecutor or the complainant and the accused, the Court shall examine the accused *in camera* to satisfy itself that the application was filed voluntarily. If satisfied, the Court provides time, not exceeding sixty days, for the Public Prosecutor or complainant and the accused to reach a mutually satisfactory disposition, which may include compensation and other expenses to the victim; otherwise, if the application is involuntary or there is a prior conviction for the same offence, the Court proceeds per the Sanhita from the stage the application was filed.
BNSS_S289:2317df526a5188cd5f5b626735b590: This Chapter applies to an accused where the report is forwarded by the officer in charge of the police station under section 193, or where a Magistrate takes cognizance of an offence on complaint—after examining the complainant and witnesses under section 223 and issuing process under section 227—for offences other than those for which the punishment of death or of imprisonment for life or of imprisonment for a term exceeding seven years has been provided. An exception is made for offences that affect the socio-economic condition of the country or have been committed against a woman or a child. Under sub-section (2), the Central Government shall, by notification, determine the offences which shall be the offences affecting the socio-economic condition of the country.
BNSS_S255:29205e7c56b3191a169b719d0f6154: The Judge shall take the evidence for the prosecution, examine the accused, and hear the prosecution and the defence on the point. If the Judge considers that there is no evidence that the accused committed the offence, the Judge shall record an order of acquittal.
BNSS_S296:6830dc6c03842fe2324e68522cf12b: Section 296 stipulates that for the purposes of discharging its functions under the chapter on plea bargaining, a Court shall possess all powers vested under this Sanhita. These powers specifically include those pertaining to bail, trial of offences, and other matters relating to the disposal of a case in such Court.
BNSS_S298:298767732c3ccc382bb2a167765bb0: Section 298 mandates that the provisions of this Chapter shall have effect notwithstanding anything inconsistent therewith contained in any other provisions of this Sanhita, and nothing in such other provisions shall be construed to constrain the meaning of any provision of this Chapter. It contains an Explanation specifying that for the purposes of this Chapter, the expression "Public Prosecutor" has the meaning assigned to it under clause (v) of section 2 and includes an Assistant Public Prosecutor appointed under section 19.
BNSS_S297:bbbdcc1bbb1a3dc3f08dc5648096da: Section 297 mandates that the provisions of section 468 shall apply for setting off the period of detention undergone by the accused against the sentence of imprisonment imposed under this Chapter. This application shall function in the same manner as the provisions of section 468 apply in respect of the imprisonment under other provisions of this Sanhita.
BNSS_S300:c0ed6524c2b2542ad82478f1d795c2: Section 300 stipulates the non-application of this Chapter to any "juvenile" or "child." These actors are defined verbatim in section 2 of the Juvenile Justice (Care and Protection of Children) Act, 2015 (2 of 2016).
BNSS_S291:22983b40e23aeefb5146fd501d9fe8: To work out a mutually satisfactory disposition under clause (a) of sub-section (4) of section 290, the Court follows specific procedures based on the mode of institution. For cases instituted on a police report, the Court shall issue notice to the Public Prosecutor, the police officer who has investigated the case, the accused, and the victim to participate in a meeting, subject to provisos that the process must be completed voluntarily and the accused may participate with his advocate, if any, engaged in the case. For cases instituted otherwise than on a police report, the Court shall issue notice to the accused and the victim to participate in a meeting, subject to provisos that the process be completed voluntarily and that the victim or the accused may participate with their engaged advocate.
BNSS_S293:774faf6df4e826aff8289ba1b62548: Upon a satisfactory disposition under section 292, the Court shall award compensation to the victim and hear parties on the quantum of punishment, release on probation of good conduct or admonition under section 401, or dealing under the Probation of Offenders Act, 1958 (20 of 1958) or any other law for the time being in force. The Court may release the accused on probation or provide benefits under section 401 or the Probation of Offenders Act, 1958 (20 of 1958); if minimum punishment is provided, it may sentence the accused to half of such minimum punishment, or one-fourth of such minimum punishment if the accused is a first-time offender and has not been convicted of any offence in the past. If the offence is not covered under the aforementioned clauses, the Court may sentence the accused to one-fourth of the punishment provided or extendable, or one-sixth of the punishment provided or extendable if the accused is a first-time offender and has not been convicted of any offence in the past.
BNSS_S295:60e8508a3f1cb0cf82da2d04259c22: The judgment delivered by the Court under this section shall be final. An exception is provided whereby no appeal shall lie in any Court against such judgment, except for the special leave petition under article 136 and writ petition under articles 226 and 227 of the Constitution.
BNSS_S301:1e1d76d20ad31b3f8edc0c940dd623: Within this Chapter, “detained” includes being detained under any law providing for preventive detention. The term “prison” includes any place declared by the State Government, by general or special order, to be a subsidiary jail, as well as any reformatory, Borstal institution, or other institution of a like nature.
BNSS_S299:68826f8c8a3a02a42720a624cf3ef9: Notwithstanding anything contained in any law for the time being in force, the statements or facts stated by an accused in an application for plea bargaining filed under section 290 shall not be used for any other purpose. Such statements or facts shall be used only for the purpose of this Chapter.
BNSS_S306:9c2a4f42e5b407c43067df436d1c84: Section 306 stipulates that the provisions of this Chapter are without prejudice to the Court's power under section 319 to issue a commission for the examination, as a witness, of any person confined or detained in a prison. It further mandates that the provisions of Part B of Chapter XXV shall apply to the examination on commission of any such person in the prison as they apply to the examination on commission of any other person.
BNSS_S305:504533401d54d08c0033ad808f553c: Subject to the provisions of section 304, upon delivery of an order made under sub-section (1) of section 302 and duly countersigned under sub-section (2) thereof, the officer in charge of the prison shall cause the person named in the order to be taken to the Court to be present at the time mentioned in the order. The officer shall further cause the person to be kept in custody in or near the Court until he has been examined or until the Court authorises him to be taken back to the prison in which he was confined or detained.
BNSS_S304:24603c83a1133e52dbd87f8c000087: Under Section 304, the officer in charge of prison shall abstain from carrying out a Court's order made under section 302 and send a statement of reasons if the person is unfit due to sickness or infirmity, is under committal for trial, under remand pending trial or pending a preliminary investigation, or if their custody expires before the time required for complying with the order and returning to prison. Abstention is also required if an order by the State Government or Central Government under section 303 applies. A Proviso specifies that the officer shall not abstain for reasons mentioned in clause (b) if the person's attendance for giving evidence is required at a place not more than twenty-five kilometres distance from the prison.
BNSS_S309:ba9d1095b965b9555a022412070701: In all summons-cases tried before a Magistrate, inquiries under sections 164 to 167 (both inclusive), and proceedings under section 491 otherwise than in the course of a trial, the Magistrate shall make a memorandum of the substance of the evidence in the language of the Court as the examination of each witness proceeds. Under a Proviso, if the Magistrate is unable to make such memorandum himself, he shall, after recording the reason of his inability, cause such memorandum to be made in writing or from his dictation in open Court. This memorandum shall be signed by the Magistrate and shall form part of the record.
BNSS_S303:52ae1dd537c67e38b09154f50d6e51: The State Government or the Central Government may, by general or special order, direct that any person or class of persons shall not be removed from the prison in which he or they may be confined or detained, such that no order made under section 302, whether before or after the order, shall have effect in respect of such person or class of persons. Under sub-section (2), before making such an order, the State Government or the Central Government (in cases instituted by its central agency) shall have regard to the nature of the offence or grounds on which the person or class of persons has been ordered to be confined or detained in prison, the likelihood of the disturbance of public order if the person or class of persons is allowed to be removed from the prison, and the public interest, generally.
BNSS_S310:a68882ef5d8dd3dbf84e29f9b063d1: In all warrant-cases tried before a Magistrate, witness evidence shall be taken down in writing by the Magistrate, by his dictation in open Court, or, in cases of physical or other incapacity, by a Court officer under his direction and superintendence. A Proviso allows such evidence to be recorded by audio-video electronic means in the presence of the advocate of the person accused of the offence. If the Magistrate causes the evidence to be taken down by another, he shall record a certificate stating the reasons for his incapacity; such evidence is ordinarily recorded in narrative form, though the Magistrate may exercise discretion to use a question and answer format. The evidence so taken down shall be signed by the Magistrate and form part of the record.
BNSS_S308:5593c51758765313f641d04656ad21: Evidence in trials or other proceedings shall be taken in the presence of the accused, or, if personal attendance is dispensed with, in the presence of his advocate, including through audio-video electronic means at the designated place notified by the State Government. A Proviso stipulates that for women below the age of eighteen years alleged to have been subjected to rape or any other sexual offence, the Court may take measures to ensure she is not confronted by the accused while ensuring the right of cross-examination. An Explanation clarifies that "accused" includes a person in relation to whom any proceeding under Chapter IX has been commenced under this Sanhita.
BNSS_S312:a0d193eb66d09ea2957511544d6cc1: Section 312 mandates that evidence taken under section 310 or section 311 shall be recorded in the language of the Court if provided in that language. If evidence is given in any other language, it may be recorded in that language if practicable; otherwise, a true translation in the language of the Court shall be prepared as the examination proceeds, signed by the Magistrate or presiding Judge. Where evidence is recorded in a language other than the language of the Court, a true translation in the language of the Court must be prepared as soon as practicable and signed by the Magistrate or presiding Judge. A Proviso stipulates that if evidence is recorded in English and no party requires a translation in the language of the Court, the Court may dispense with such translation.
BNSS_S314:d73f1c424871e473d44d982ca0c15e: Section 314 mandates that evidence given in a language not understood by an accused present in person shall be interpreted to him in open Court in a language understood by him. Where the accused appears by an advocate, evidence given in a language other than the language of the Court and not understood by the advocate shall be interpreted to such advocate. For documents put for the purpose of formal proof, it remains in the discretion of the Court to interpret as much thereof as appears necessary.
BNSS_S315:70040e66e06e5ded34788b7d326e59: Upon recording the evidence of a witness, a presiding Judge or Magistrate shall also record such remarks (if any) as they think material respecting the demeanour of such witness. These remarks must pertain to the conduct of the witness whilst under examination.
BNSS_S311:1452a531f4837c0b833466b04ebf3d: In all trials before a Court of Session, the evidence of each witness shall, as his examination proceeds, be taken down in writing by the presiding Judge himself, by his dictation in open Court, or by an officer of the Court appointed under his direction and superintendence. Such evidence shall ordinarily be taken down in the form of a narrative, though the presiding Judge may, in his discretion, take down or cause to be taken down any part of such evidence in the form of question and answer. The evidence so taken down shall be signed by the presiding Judge and shall form part of the record.
BNSS_S313:27fea1ffef7cea7e5f357399d14b3d: Upon completion of the evidence of each witness taken under section 310 or section 311, the evidence shall be read over to the witness in the presence of the accused, if in attendance, or of his advocate, and shall be corrected if necessary. If the witness denies the correctness of any part of the evidence, the Magistrate or presiding Judge may, instead of correcting the evidence, make a memorandum of the objection made by the witness and add such remarks as he thinks necessary. Furthermore, if the record is in a language different from that in which it was given and the witness does not understand that language, the record shall be interpreted to the witness in the language in which it was given or in a language which he understands.
BNSS_S316:96056753753a500141d522627bea1d: Section 316 mandates that when an accused is examined by a Magistrate or Court of Session, the whole examination, including every question and answer, be recorded in full by the presiding Judge or Magistrate, or under their direction by an appointed officer of the Court in cases of physical or other incapacity. The record, written in the language of examination or the language of the Court, must be read or interpreted to the accused for additions or explanations and signed by the accused and the Magistrate or presiding Judge, who certifies it as a full and true account. A Proviso specifies that where the accused is in custody and examined through electronic communication, the signature must be taken within seventy-two hours of such examination. This section does not apply to the examination of an accused person in the course of a summary trial.
BNSS_S318:031d872e59a057a3816f9d88b39841: Every High Court may, by general rule, prescribe the manner in which the evidence of witnesses and the examination of the accused shall be taken down in cases coming before it. Such evidence and examination shall be taken down in accordance with such rule.
BNSS_S307:2b2d2ac4862cf908abc6cc605bb2c4: Under Section 307, the State Government may determine the language of each Court within the State for purposes of this Sanhita. This provision contains an exception that excludes the High Court from the State Government's determination.
BNSS_S320:3a10b03bba1960612141692c1d6459: Section 320 specifies that if a witness is within territories to which this Sanhita extends, the commission shall be directed to the Chief Judicial Magistrate within whose local jurisdiction the witness is found. If the witness is in India but in a State or area to which this Sanhita does not extend, the commission is directed to such Court or officer as the Central Government may specify by notification. For witnesses outside India, where the Central Government has arrangements with the Government of such country or place for taking evidence in criminal matters, the commission is issued in the form, directed to the Court or officer, and sent to the authority for transmission as the Central Government may prescribe by notification.
BNSS_S324:88140844514a42904cf81de06c4ef4: Section 324 governs the Adjournment of proceeding in every case where a commission is issued under section 319. Under this provision, the inquiry, trial or other proceeding may be adjourned for a specified time reasonably sufficient for the execution and return of the commission.
BNSS_S321:b2fbb65723ca0c710a2a86f1dcde97: Upon receipt of the commission, the Chief Judicial Magistrate or such Magistrate as he may appoint in this behalf shall summon the witness before him or proceed to the place where the witness is. The Magistrate shall take down the witness's evidence in the same manner and may, for this purpose, exercise the same powers as in trials of warrant-cases under this Sanhita.
BNSS_S283:362365969557f5aac03aa1916ce01e: A Chief Judicial Magistrate or Magistrate of the first class shall try in a summary way theft (under sub-section (2) of section 303, section 305, or section 306 of the Bharatiya Nyaya Sanhita, 2023), receiving or retaining stolen property (under sub-section (2) of section 317 of the Bharatiya Nyaya Sanhita, 2023), or assisting in the concealment or disposal of stolen property (under sub-section (5) of section 317 of the Bharatiya Nyaya Sanhita, 2023) where the value does not exceed twenty thousand rupees, alongside offences under sub-sections (2) and (3) of section 331, insult with intent to provoke a breach of the peace (section 352), criminal intimidation (sub-sections (2) and (3) of section 351) of the Bharatiya Nyaya Sanhita, 2023, their abetment, their attempt, or offences under section 20 of the Cattle-trespass Act, 1871. A Magistrate may, after giving the accused a reasonable opportunity of being heard and recording reasons in writing, try in a summary way offences not punishable with death or imprisonment for life or imprisonment for a term exceeding three years, subject to a Proviso that no appeal shall lie against the decision of a Magistrate to try a case in a summary way under this sub-section. If, during a summary trial, the Magistrate finds it undesirable to try the case summarily, they shall recall any witnesses examined and proceed to re-hear the case in the manner provided by this Sanhita.
BNSS_S322:8cb4554cee921673be41664435a45e: Section 322 stipulates that parties to proceedings under this Sanhita involving a commission may forward written interrogatories, which the Court or Magistrate directing the commission may deem relevant to the issue. The Magistrate, Court, or officer to whom the commission is directed or delegated is empowered to examine the witness based on these interrogatories. Parties may further appear before said Magistrate, Court, or Officer—either via an advocate or in person if not in custody—to examine, cross-examine, and re-examine the witness.
BNSS_S319:e39061e993df903cf78639c223a404: During an inquiry, trial or other proceeding, if a Court or Magistrate finds witness examination necessary for the ends of justice and attendance would cause unreasonable delay, expense or inconvenience, they may dispense with such attendance and issue a commission. A Proviso mandates that a commission shall be issued if the witness is the President or the Vice-President of India or the Governor of a State or the Administrator of a Union territory. Furthermore, when issuing a commission for the examination of a witness for the prosecution, the Court may direct the prosecution to pay amounts considered reasonable to meet the expenses of the accused, including the advocate's fees.
BNSS_S326:c61e0b6dc18d223cebbd122165ddac: The deposition of a civil surgeon or other medical witness, taken and attested by a Magistrate in the presence of the accused or taken on commission under this Chapter, may be given in evidence in any inquiry, trial or other proceeding under this Sanhita, although the deponent is not called as a witness. The Court may, or shall on the application of the prosecution or the accused, summon and examine any such deponent as to the subject-matter of his deposition.
BNSS_S323:d98a853ad48743a70d5dc7e7e96487: Once a commission issued under section 319 is duly executed, it must be returned to the issuing Court or Magistrate along with the deposition of the examined witness, forming part of the record. The commission, the return, and the deposition shall be open at all reasonable times to inspection of the parties and, subject to all just exceptions, may be read in evidence in the case by either party. Additionally, any deposition satisfying the conditions specified by section 27 of the Bharatiya Sakshya Adhiniyam, 2023, may also be received in evidence at any subsequent stage of the case before another Court.
BNSS_S332:8992675836e35ddd12d3a0cf8d444f: Section 332 stipulates that evidence of formal character may be given by affidavit and read in any inquiry, trial or other proceeding under this Sanhita, subject to all just exceptions. The Court may, if it thinks fit, and shall, on the application of the prosecution or the accused, summon and examine such person as to the facts contained in his affidavit.
BNSS_S331:89f2abbe8f774e76be5e7f3c020aa9: When an application is made to any Court during any inquiry, trial, or other proceeding under this Sanhita involving allegations respecting any public servant, the applicant may give evidence of the facts alleged in the application by affidavit. The Court may, if it thinks fit, order that evidence relating to such facts be so given.
BNSS_S330:d017ac2b5c1dcccc3950a9de0688c2: Documents filed by the prosecution or the accused must be listed, and the prosecution, the accused, or their advocates must admit or deny the genuineness of each document no later than thirty days after supply, subject to a Proviso allowing the Court to relax this limit with reasons to be recorded in writing and a further Proviso that no expert shall be called unless their report is disputed. The State Government shall provide the form of the list of documents by rules. Undisputed documents may be read in evidence in any inquiry, trial or other proceeding under this Sanhita without proof of signature, subject to a Proviso that the Court may, in its discretion, require such signature to be proved.
BNSS_S328:96e70f092c2ed19b2e8f6fbca03d69: Documents purporting to be reports under the hand of a gazetted officer of any Mint, Note Printing Press, Security Printing Press (including the Controller of Stamps and Stationery), Forensic Department or Division of Forensic Science Laboratory, Government Examiner of Questioned Documents, or State Examiner of Questioned Documents, as specified by Central Government notification, may be used as evidence in any inquiry, trial, or proceeding under this Sanhita without calling the officer as a witness. The Court may summon and examine such officers regarding the subject-matter of the report, subject to a Proviso that no such officer shall be summoned to produce records on which the report is based. Without prejudice to sections 129 and 130 of the Bharatiya Sakshya Adhiniyam, 2023, such officers require permission from the General Manager or officer in charge of the respective Mint, Press, Forensic Department, Laboratory, or Questioned Documents Organisation to give evidence derived from unpublished official records or disclose the nature or particulars of any test applied.
BNSS_S325:60fa578f6c88644e953b4ef3eed2ef: Section 325 stipulates that the provisions of Section 321 and so much of Section 322 and Section 323 relating to the execution of a commission and its return shall apply to commissions issued by specified Courts, Judges, or Magistrates as they apply to commissions issued under Section 319. These authorities include any Court, Judge, or Magistrate exercising jurisdiction within an area in India to which this Sanhita does not extend, as the Central Government may, by notification, specify, or any Court, Judge, or Magistrate exercising jurisdiction in any country or place outside India, as the Central Government may, by notification, specify, which possesses authority under the law in force in that country or place to issue commissions for the examination of witnesses in relation to criminal matters.
BNSS_S334:0446e1f77f266eb191c702f51627e1: In any inquiry, trial or other proceeding under this Sanhita, a previous conviction or acquittal may be proved via any mode provided by law for the time being in force, or by an extract certified under the hand of the officer having the custody of the records of the Court in which such conviction or acquittal was held. For convictions, proof may also be established by a certificate signed by the officer in charge of the jail in which the punishment or any part thereof was undergone, or by production of the warrant of commitment under which the punishment was suffered. Such proof must be accompanied by evidence as to the identity of the accused person with the person so convicted or acquitted.
BNSS_S336:ca92abe3d463e49284712686eb7b5b: Section 336 mandates that if a document or report prepared by a public servant, scientific expert, or medical officer is used as evidence in any inquiry, trial, or other proceeding, the Court shall secure the presence of the successor officer holding that post to give deposition if the original officer is transferred, retired, died, cannot be found, is incapable of giving deposition, or if their presence would likely cause delay. The first Proviso stipulates that no public servant, scientific expert, or medical officer shall be called unless their report is disputed by any of the parties of the trial or other proceedings. The second Proviso allows the deposition of such successor public servant, expert, or officer to be conducted through audio-video electronic means.
BNSS_S340:a1e562fc3e49f0d9ab01ab3e008a55: Section 340 establishes the right of any person accused of an offence before a Criminal Court, or any person against whom proceedings are instituted under this Sanhita, to be defended. Such person may of right be defended by an advocate of his choice.
BNSS_S329:a652f1f9dc012b35e287f6d8f40db3: Under Section 329, any document purporting to be a report under the hand of a Government scientific expert regarding matters submitted for examination or analysis in the course of any proceeding under this Sanhita may be used as evidence in any inquiry, trial, or other proceeding. The Court may, if it thinks fit, summon and examine such experts, who, if unable to attend personally and unless the Court has expressly directed him to appear personally, may depute a responsible officer conversant with the facts to satisfactorily depose in Court on his behalf. This section applies to the Chemical Examiner or Assistant Chemical Examiner to Government, the Chief Controller of Explosives, the Director of the Finger Print Bureau, the Director, Haffkeine Institute, Bombay, the Director, Deputy Director or Assistant Director of a Central or State Forensic Science Laboratory, the Serologist to the Government, or any other scientific expert specified or certified by notification by the State or Central Government.
BNSS_S333:302f9a4575039c72d5085918da50db: Affidavits used before any Court under this Sanhita may be sworn or affirmed before any Judge or Judicial or Executive Magistrate, any Commissioner of Oaths appointed by a High Court or Court of Session, or any notary appointed under the Notaries Act, 1952 (53 of 1952). Such affidavits shall be confined to, and state separately, facts the deponent is able to prove from his own knowledge and facts he has reasonable ground to believe to be true, clearly stating the grounds of such belief. Furthermore, the Court may order any scandalous and irrelevant matter in the affidavit to be struck out or amended.
BNSS_S327:1321209100b0b93006665c4d4ff7f9: Any document purporting to be a report of identification under the hand of an Executive Magistrate in respect of a person or property may be used as evidence in any inquiry, trial, or other proceeding under this Sanhita, although such Magistrate is not called as a witness. This is subject to a Proviso stating that where such report contains a statement of any suspect or witness to which the provisions of section 19, section 26, section 27, section 158, or section 160 of the Bharatiya Sakshya Adhiniyam, 2023 apply, such statement shall not be used except in accordance with the provisions of those sections. Furthermore, the Court may, if it thinks fit, or shall, on the application of the prosecution or of the accused, summon and examine such Magistrate as to the subject-matter of the said report.
BNSS_S344:f79c80b68c272ea8fe9254207e09b2: Section 344 authorizes the Court to which the commitment is made, at any time after commitment of a case but before judgment is passed, to tender a pardon on the same condition to any person. This mechanism is employed with a view to obtaining at the trial the evidence of any person supposed to have been directly or indirectly concerned in, or privy to, any such offence.
BNSS_S339:57d1d63992106669f0bfed546119d8: Any Magistrate inquiring into or trying a case may permit the prosecution to be conducted by any person other than a police officer below the rank of inspector, though no person other than the Advocate-General, Government Advocate, Public Prosecutor, or Assistant Public Prosecutor is entitled to do so without such permission. A proviso stipulates that no police officer shall be permitted to conduct the prosecution if he has taken part in the investigation into the offence with respect to which the accused is being prosecuted. Any person conducting the prosecution may do so personally or by an advocate.
BNSS_S317:d1ebf96a6f57eb115835e40c118b91: When the services of an interpreter are required by any Criminal Court for the interpretation of any evidence or statement, the interpreter shall be bound to state the true interpretation of such evidence or statement. This section contains no Provisos, Exceptions, or Explanations.
BNSS_S342:335075408f2a3996345c8d0873815b: Section 342 defines “corporation” as an incorporated company, body corporate, or a society registered under the Societies Registration Act, 1860 (21 of 1860), and permits a corporation accused in an inquiry or trial to appoint a representative without requiring the seal of the corporation. If a representative appears, requirements for actions in the presence of, explanations to, or the examination of the accused are construed as applying to the representative; if no representative appears, these requirements do not apply. The Court shall presume a person is appointed as the representative if a written statement is filed signed by the managing director or a person having management of the corporation's affairs, unless the contrary is proved, and the Court shall determine any questions regarding the representative's status.
BNSS_S341:beec6f855af8c06e982d7c0d421b1d: In a trial or appeal before a Court, if the accused is not represented by an advocate and has not sufficient means to engage an advocate, the Court shall assign an advocate for his defence at the expense of the State. With the previous approval of the State Government, the High Court may make rules providing for the mode of selecting advocates under sub-section (1), the facilities to be allowed to such advocates by the Courts, and the fees payable to such advocates by the Government. Furthermore, the State Government may, by notification, direct that the provisions of sub-sections (1) and (2) apply in relation to any class of trials before other Courts in the State as they apply in relation to trials before Courts of Session.
BNSS_S348:b6c05fd68324a5ad9d0a6ed47e56f8: Any Court may, at any stage of any inquiry, trial or other proceeding under this Sanhita, summon any person as a witness, examine any person in attendance though not summoned as a witness, or re-call and re-examine any person already examined. The Court shall summon and examine or re-call and re-examine any such person if his evidence appears to it to be essential to the just decision of the case.
BNSS_S337:5b2be5a2893cb23e5bf148cd0483a2: Section 337 provides that a person tried by a Court of competent jurisdiction and convicted or acquitted shall not be liable to be tried again for the same offence or same facts under sub-section (1) of section 244 or sub-section (2) thereof, except for a distinct offence tried with the consent of the State Government under sub-section (1) of section 243. Subsequent trials are permitted if an act’s consequences constitute a different offence that were unhappened or unknown at conviction, if the first Court was not competent to try the subsequent offence, or for a person discharged under section 281 with consent from the discharging or subordinate Court. The section preserves section 26 of the General Clauses Act, 1897 and section 208 of this Sanhita, and includes an Explanation stating that the dismissal of a complaint or the discharge of the accused is not an acquittal for the purposes of this section.
BNSS_S347:206a3918d1449339664f54e6f2c74d: At any stage of any inquiry, trial or other proceeding, any Judge or Magistrate may, after due notice to the parties, visit and inspect any place where an offence is alleged to have been committed or any other place necessary for properly appreciating the evidence. The Judge or Magistrate shall, without unnecessary delay, record a memorandum of any relevant facts observed, which shall form part of the record of the case. If the prosecutor, complainant, accused or any other party to the case so desires, a copy of the memorandum shall be furnished to him free of cost.
BNSS_S335:49993fc1943309b2310138f2002ccc: If an accused has absconded and there is no immediate prospect of arresting him, the Court competent to try, or commit for trial, may examine prosecution witnesses and record their depositions in his absence, which may be given in evidence upon arrest if the deponent is dead, incapable of giving evidence, cannot be found, or his presence cannot be procured without an amount of delay, expense or inconvenience which, under the circumstances of the case, would be unreasonable. For offences punishable with death or imprisonment for life committed by persons unknown, the High Court or the Sessions Judge may direct a Magistrate of the first class to hold an inquiry and examine witnesses. Such depositions may be given in evidence against any person subsequently accused if the deponent is dead, incapable of giving evidence, or beyond the limits of India.
BNSS_S352:72b1fbb498fc908ee1ab9b457d600e: Under Section 352, any party to a proceeding may address concise oral arguments after the close of evidence and submit a memorandum under distinct headings that forms part of the record, provided a copy is simultaneously furnished to the opposite party. No adjournment of the proceedings shall be granted for filing written arguments unless the Court, for reasons to be recorded in writing, considers it necessary. Furthermore, the Court may regulate oral arguments if it is of the opinion that they are not concise or relevant.
BNSS_S346:cf6ab920dd50a918a83e78fd257d48: Section 346 requires inquiry or trial proceedings to continue on a day-to-day basis until all witnesses in attendance are examined unless the Court records reasons for adjournment; however, a Proviso mandates completion within two months from the date of filing of the chargesheet for offences under section 64, section 65, section 66, section 67, section 68, section 70 or section 71 of the Bharatiya Nyaya Sanhita, 2023. The Court may postpone or adjourn proceedings for recorded reasons on fit terms and may remand the accused via warrant, subject to Provisos that no remand exceeds fifteen days at a time, no adjournment occurs without examining witnesses in attendance except for special reasons recorded in writing, and no adjournment is granted solely to enable the accused to show cause against a proposed sentence. Additional Provisos prohibit adjournments at a party's request unless circumstances are beyond their control (limited to two adjournments after hearing objections and recording reasons), specify that an advocate being engaged in another Court is not a ground for adjournment, and allow the Court to record witness statements and dispense with examination-in-chief or cross-examination if the party or advocate is absent or not ready. Explanation 1 defines reasonable cause for remand as sufficient evidence raising suspicion of an offence and the likelihood of obtaining further evidence, while Explanation 2 specifies that adjournment terms may include payment of costs by the prosecution or the accused.
BNSS_S349:836007f611de7944b7929ecca60fb3: A Magistrate of the first class, if satisfied that it is expedient for any investigation or proceeding under this Sanhita, may order any person, including an accused person, to attend at the time and place specified to give specimen signatures, finger impressions, handwriting, or voice sample. The first proviso stipulates that no order shall be made under this section unless the person has at some time been arrested in connection with such investigation or proceeding. However, the second proviso allows the Magistrate, for reasons to be recorded in writing, to order any person to give such specimen or sample without him being arrested.
BNSS_S356:04cb9e9e0d6387c6c285fb2ee69f66: Section 356 permits the inquiry, trial, and judgment in absentia of a "proclaimed offender" who absconded to evade trial, deemed a "waiver of the right of such person to be present and tried in person," provided the Court records reasons in writing and, per the Proviso, "ninety days" have lapsed from the date of framing the charge. Before proceeding, the Court must ensure the issuance of two consecutive warrants of arrest (interval of at least "thirty days"), publication in a national or local daily newspaper (requiring appearance within "thirty days"), notification of a relative or friend, and affixing information at the house or homestead and the police station. Unrepresented offenders shall be provided an advocate at the expense of the State, and prior prosecution witness depositions are admissible, although a Proviso allows the Court to permit the offender to examine evidence taken in his absence if he is later arrested or appears. Witness examinations may be recorded by "audio-video electronic means preferably mobile phone," and no appeal lies unless the offender presents himself, with a Proviso barring appeals against conviction after "three years" from the judgment date; furthermore, the State may extend these provisions to absconders under section 84(1).
BNSS_S362:d0a8aee1b0368f987b6e24d71f9fbf: If, at any stage of an inquiry into an offence or a trial before a Magistrate and before signing the judgment, it appears the case ought to be tried by the Court of Session, the Magistrate shall commit it to that Court. Upon such commitment, the provisions of Chapter XIX shall apply.
BNSS_S360:f48e5c3219ab612162baee5c065b40: The Public Prosecutor or Assistant Public Prosecutor may, with the consent of the Court and before judgment is pronounced, withdraw from the prosecution of any person generally or regarding specific offences. Such withdrawal results in the accused being discharged if made before a charge has been framed, or acquitted if made after a charge has been framed or when no charge is required. The first Proviso mandates that if the offence relates to laws where the executive power of the Union extends, is investigated under any Central Act, involves misappropriation, destruction, or damage to Central Government property, or is committed by a person in Central Government service acting in official duty, a Prosecutor not appointed by the Central Government must obtain its permission, which the Court shall direct the Prosecutor to produce. The second Proviso stipulates that no Court shall allow such withdrawal without giving an opportunity of being heard to the victim.
BNSS_S343:9043a6bc9c918b5557e5a140af61a1: To obtain evidence, a Chief Judicial Magistrate (at any stage of investigation, inquiry, or trial) or a Magistrate of the first class inquiring into or trying the offence (at any stage of the inquiry or trial) may tender a pardon to a person directly or indirectly concerned in or privy to an offence, conditional upon a full and true disclosure of the whole of the circumstances within his knowledge relative to the offence and every other person concerned, whether as principal or abettor. This applies to any offence triable exclusively by the Court of Session or by the Court of a Special Judge, or any offence punishable with imprisonment which may extend to seven years or with a more severe sentence. The Magistrate must record his reasons and whether the tender was accepted, providing a free copy of the record to the accused upon application; the person accepting must be examined as a witness and, unless already on bail, be detained in custody until the termination of the trial. Following examination, the Magistrate taking cognizance shall, without making any further inquiry, commit the case to the Court of Session (if triable exclusively by it or if the Magistrate is the Chief Judicial Magistrate), to a Court of Special Judge (if triable exclusively by it), or make over the case to the Chief Judicial Magistrate for trial in any other case.
BNSS_S353:95491db24369e6b9f4c88deca8a281: Any person accused of an offence before a Criminal Court is a competent witness for the defence and may give evidence on oath in disproof of the charges made against him or any person charged together with him at the same trial, subject to Provisos that he shall not be called as a witness except on his own request in writing and his failure to give evidence shall not be the subject of any comment by any of the parties or the Court or give rise to any presumption against himself or any person charged together with him at the same trial. Any person against whom proceedings are instituted under section 101, or section 126, or section 127, or section 128, or section 129, or under Chapter X or under Part B, Part C or Part D of Chapter XI, may offer himself as a witness. A Proviso specifies that in proceedings under section 127, section 128, or section 129, the failure of such person to give evidence shall not be the subject of any comment by any of the parties or the Court or give rise to any presumption against him or any other person proceeded against together with him at the same inquiry.
BNSS_S357:765b85f3f4bc7935d84fe35a850bb7: If the accused, though not a person of unsound mind, cannot be made to understand the proceedings, the Court may proceed with the inquiry or trial. In the case of a Court other than a High Court, if such proceedings result in a conviction, the proceedings shall be forwarded to the High Court with a report of the circumstances of the case. The High Court shall pass thereon such order as it thinks fit.
BNSS_S361:81196d8255bbe8821533ccd2b34de0: During an inquiry into an offence or a trial before a Magistrate in any district, if evidence warrants a presumption that the Magistrate lacks jurisdiction to try or commit the case, that it should be tried or committed by another Magistrate in the district, or that it should be tried by the Chief Judicial Magistrate, he shall stay the proceedings. The Magistrate shall submit the case, with a brief report explaining its nature, to the Chief Judicial Magistrate or to such other Magistrate, having jurisdiction, as the Chief Judicial Magistrate directs. The Magistrate to whom the case is submitted may, if empowered, either try the case himself, refer it to any Magistrate subordinate to him having jurisdiction, or commit the accused for trial.
BNSS_S345:8a8f99b119b1fb90e4fd6a6cf2852e: If a Public Prosecutor certifies that a person accepting a tender of pardon under section 343 or section 344 has wilfully concealed anything essential or given false evidence, such person may be tried for the original offence, any other offence of which he appears to have been guilty in connection with the same matter, and the offence of giving false evidence. Provisos mandate that such person shall not be tried jointly with any other accused and requires the sanction of the High Court for the offence of giving false evidence, to which section 215 or section 379 shall not apply. Statements recorded under section 183 or section 343(4) may be given in evidence against them, and the accused may plead compliance with the conditions, which a Court of Session (before the charge is read out and explained) or Magistrate (before prosecution evidence is taken) must address. If the Court finds the accused has complied with the conditions, it shall pass judgment of acquittal notwithstanding anything contained in this Sanhita.
BNSS_S355:291fd83a63054351d92c7949667f85: At any stage of an inquiry or trial, if a Judge or Magistrate is satisfied, for reasons to be recorded, that the personal attendance of the accused is not necessary in the interests of justice or the accused persistently disturbs proceedings, they may, if the accused is represented by an advocate, dispense with attendance and proceed, while retaining the authority to direct personal attendance at a subsequent stage. If the accused is not represented by an advocate or if the Judge or Magistrate considers personal attendance necessary, they may, for reasons to be recorded, either adjourn such inquiry or trial, or order that the case of such accused be taken up or tried separately. The Explanation clarifies that personal attendance of the accused includes attendance through audio-video electronic means.
BNSS_S364:4b38099398e2b740a53f091ab368e1: If a Magistrate, after hearing evidence for the prosecution and the accused, opines the accused is guilty and deserves a punishment "different in kind from, or more severe than" that the Magistrate is empowered to inflict, or if a "Magistrate of the second class" opines a "bond or bail bond under section 125" is required, they may record this opinion and forward the accused and proceedings to the "Chief Judicial Magistrate" to whom they are subordinate. Where multiple accused are tried together and the Magistrate proceeds under sub-section (1) regarding any of them, all accused deemed guilty shall be forwarded to the "Chief Judicial Magistrate." The "Chief Judicial Magistrate" may then examine parties, recall and examine witnesses, take further evidence, and pass a "judgment, sentence or order" according to law.
BNSS_S366:c382d522b5824d89fb6d7da59fb111: Criminal Courts inquiring into or trying any offence are deemed "open Court" with general public access, subject to a Proviso allowing the presiding Judge or Magistrate to restrict access for the public generally or any particular person. Inquiries and trials for rape or offences under Bharatiya Nyaya Sanhita, 2023 sections 64, 65, 66, 67, 68, 70, 71 or Protection of Children from Sexual Offences Act, 2012 sections 4, 6, 8, 10 shall be conducted "in camera," subject to a Proviso allowing the presiding Judge to grant access to particular persons and a further Proviso that such trials be conducted by a woman Judge or Magistrate as far as practicable. Printing or publishing matter relating to sub-section (2) proceedings is unlawful without previous permission of the Court, although a Proviso allows the ban for rape offences to be lifted provided confidentiality of name and address of the parties is maintained.
BNSS_S370:1f454e598b6b484745af4e4e3ac545: Section 370(1) provides that whenever an inquiry or trial is postponed under section 367 or section 368, the Magistrate or Court may, after the person concerned has ceased to be of unsound mind, resume the inquiry or trial and require the accused to appear or be brought before such Magistrate or Court. Under Section 370(2), if the accused has been released under section 369 and the sureties produce him to the appointed officer, the certificate of such officer that the accused is capable of making his defence shall be receivable in evidence.
BNSS_S351:d29eede2d01e09e83659907c875bca: In every inquiry or trial, for the purpose of enabling the accused personally to explain any circumstances appearing in the evidence against him, the Court may, at any stage and without previously warning the accused, ask necessary questions, and shall, after prosecution witnesses are examined and before the accused is called for his defence, question him generally on the case, provided that in a summons case where personal attendance is dispensed with, the Court may also dispense with his examination under clause (b). No oath shall be administered to the accused, and he shall not render himself liable to punishment by refusing to answer such questions or by giving false answers. Such answers may be taken into consideration in the inquiry or trial and put in evidence for or against him in any other inquiry into, or trial for, any other offence. The Court may take help of Prosecutor and Defence Counsel in preparing relevant questions and may permit filing of a written statement by the accused as sufficient compliance.
BNSS_S372:7187ddb378b465919702a8933b588d: Section 372 mandates that if an accused appears to be of sound mind at the time of inquiry or trial and the Magistrate is satisfied from evidence that the accused committed an act which, if he had been of sound mind, would have been an offence, but was at the time of the act incapable of knowing its nature or that it was wrong or contrary to law by reason of unsoundness of mind, the Magistrate shall proceed with the case. If the accused ought to be tried by the Court of Session, the Magistrate shall commit him for trial before the Court of Session.
BNSS_S368:79630b07c9d72a64915bb248c98ef9: If a Magistrate or Court of Session finds an accused of unsound mind and incapable of making his defence, they shall record a finding after considering medical and other evidence and postpone proceedings. The Court shall refer the accused to a psychiatrist or clinical psychologist, subject to a Proviso allowing the aggrieved accused to appeal before a Medical Board consisting of the head of psychiatry unit in the nearest Government hospital and a faculty member in psychiatry in the nearest Government medical college. Upon determining incapacity to enter defence, the Court examines prosecution evidence and hears the advocate; if no prima facie case is made out, the accused is discharged and dealt with under section 369, while a Proviso mandates postponing trial for the treatment period opined by the psychiatrist or clinical psychologist if a prima facie case is made out. If a prima facie case exists but the accused is incapable of entering defence by reason of intellectual disability, the Court shall not hold the trial and shall order the accused to be dealt with in accordance with section 369.
BNSS_S359:a8fd1c4bc7dbfca08b85e1911a746d: Section 359 permits the compounding of specific offences under the Bharatiya Nyaya Sanhita, 2023, by designated persons as listed in the Tables of sub-sections (1) and (2), where sub-section (2) offences require Court permission. This mechanism extends to abetment or attempts under sub-section (3), including cases where the accused is liable under sub-section (5) of section 3 or section 190 of BNS, and may be facilitated by a High Court or Court of Session under section 442 revision powers per sub-section (6). Provisos include sub-section (4), allowing a person competent to contract or a legal representative per the Code of Civil Procedure, 1908, to compound with Court permission or consent; sub-section (5), requiring Court leave if trial or appeal is pending; sub-section (7), prohibiting compounding if a previous conviction leads to enhanced or different punishment; and sub-section (8), where composition effects an acquittal.
BNSS_S365:577dabf37f93235b22bb0b4d168f52: When a Judge or Magistrate ceases to exercise jurisdiction after recording evidence in an inquiry or trial, a succeeding Judge or Magistrate may act on evidence recorded by the predecessor or partly by both, though a Proviso allows the successor to re-summon witnesses for further examination, cross-examination, and re-examination if necessary in the interests of justice. Per sub-section (2), cases transferred under this Sanhita from one Judge to another Judge or one Magistrate to another Magistrate result in the former being deemed to cease to exercise jurisdiction and be succeeded by the latter under sub-section (1). Sub-section (3) explicitly excludes summary trials, cases in which proceedings have been stayed under section 361, and cases submitted to a superior Magistrate under section 364 from the application of this section.
BNSS_S371:4e69d9dc8126fc5a87c8cddfe7d343: If, when the accused appears or is again brought before the Magistrate or Court, the Magistrate or Court considers him capable of making his defence, the inquiry or trial shall proceed. If the Magistrate or Court considers the accused still incapable of making his defence, they shall act according to the provisions of section 367 or section 368, as the case may be. If the accused is found to be of unsound mind and consequently incapable of making his defence, the Magistrate or Court shall deal with such accused in accordance with the provisions of section 369.
BNSS_S373:8e8c04ba9f9636bdfe7bf7e9322133: Under Section 373, whenever any person is acquitted on the ground that, at the time of the alleged offence, they were, by reason of unsoundness of mind, incapable of knowing the nature of the act alleged as constituting the offence, or that it was wrong or contrary to law, the finding must be specific. This finding shall state specifically whether the person committed the act or not.
BNSS_S376:fb2d55897ab8d72f8a1450df8056bb: Where a person is detained under sub-section (2) of section 369 and is certified capable of making his defence by the Inspector-General of Prisons (if detained in a jail) or the Mental Health Review Board constituted under the Mental Healthcare Act, 2017 (10 of 2017) (if detained in a public mental health establishment), they shall be taken before the Magistrate or Court at an appointed time. The Magistrate or Court shall then deal with such person under the provisions of section 371. The certificate issued by the Inspector-General or visitors is receivable as evidence.
BNSS_S375:8f3296a0b9e6dec5bef3abbdfdd1d8: The State Government may empower the officer in charge of the jail in which a person is confined under the provisions of section 369 or section 374. This empowerment enables the officer in charge to discharge all or any of the functions of the Inspector-General of Prisons under section 376 or section 377.
BNSS_S377:41ee60a43b5c31c68a6dbeea3973bf: If a person detained under sub-section (2) of section 369 or section 374 is certified by an Inspector-General or visitors as fit for release without danger of doing injury to himself or any other person, the State Government may order his release, detention in custody, or transfer to a public mental health establishment. For such transfers, the State Government may appoint a Commission consisting of a Judicial and two medical officers to conduct a formal inquiry into the person's state of mind and take necessary evidence. This Commission shall report to the State Government, which may subsequently order the person's release or detention as it thinks fit.
BNSS_S382:c6832c14c64dccf0c515a29b8a80ea: A Magistrate receiving a complaint under section 379 or section 380 shall, notwithstanding anything contained in Chapter XVI, proceed to deal with the case as if it were instituted on a police report. If it is brought to the notice of such Magistrate, or any other Magistrate to whom the case is transferred, that an appeal is pending against the decision arrived at in the judicial proceeding out of which the matter arose, the Magistrate may adjourn the hearing until such appeal is decided.
BNSS_S363:0d119462a7b5593cf42e3621b434ae: If a person previously convicted of an offence punishable under Chapter X or Chapter XVII of the Bharatiya Nyaya Sanhita, 2023 (45 of 2023) with imprisonment for a term of three years or upwards is again accused of an offence punishable under either of those Chapters with imprisonment for a term of three years or upwards, the Magistrate, if satisfied there is ground for presuming the person committed the offence, shall send them for trial to the Chief Judicial Magistrate or commit them to the Court of Session. This is subject to the exception where the Magistrate is competent to try the case and is of opinion that he can himself pass an adequate sentence if the accused is convicted. Furthermore, any person accused jointly in the same inquiry or trial shall be similarly sent or committed, unless discharged under section 262 or section 268.
BNSS_S380:07eb13814e8a2c138ca3a548cd7c8a: Any person whose application for a complaint under sub-section (1) or sub-section (2) of section 379 was refused by a Court other than a High Court, or against whom such a complaint was made, may appeal to the Court to which such former Court is subordinate within the meaning of sub-section (4) of section 215. Upon notice to the parties concerned, the superior Court may direct the withdrawal of the complaint or the making of the complaint under section 379, in which case the provisions of section 379 shall apply accordingly. An order under section 380, and subject to such order, an order under section 379, shall be final and not subject to revision.
BNSS_S386:fcda56bb357e4281df724926a10bb4: Section 386 provides that when the State Government so directs, any Registrar or Sub-Registrar appointed under the Registration Act, 1908 (16 of 1908) shall be deemed to be a Civil Court. This designation applies within the meaning of sections 384 and 385.
BNSS_S369:3491f9663db0ac2badbe164228c6f2: If a person is found under section 367 or 368 to be incapable of entering defence due to unsoundness of mind or intellectual disability, the Magistrate or Court, as the case may be, shall order release on bail, provided that the condition does not mandate in-patient treatment and a friend or relative undertakes regular out-patient psychiatric treatment and prevention of injury to self or others. If bail cannot be granted or an appropriate undertaking is not given, the accused shall be kept where regular psychiatric treatment is provided and reported to the State Government, subject to the proviso that detention in a public mental health establishment must comply with rules under the Mental Healthcare Act, 2017 (10 of 2017). Finally, the Magistrate or Court shall determine if release is possible based on the nature of the act and extent of disability, with a proviso that discharge may be ordered under section 367 or 368 upon medical or specialist opinion if sufficient security is provided to prevent injury, or if discharge cannot be ordered, the accused may be transferred to a residential facility for persons with unsoundness of mind or intellectual disability for care, education, and training.
BNSS_S390:ce2d365f735343a218f52126a94e52: Any person sentenced by any Court other than a High Court under section 383, section 384, section 388, or section 389 may appeal to the Court to which decrees or orders made in such Court are ordinarily appealable. Such appeals are governed by the applicable provisions of Chapter XXXI, and the Appellate Court may alter or reverse the finding, or reduce or reverse the sentence appealed against. Appeals from convictions by a Court of Small Causes, or by any Registrar or Sub-Registrar deemed to be a Civil Court under section 386, shall lie to the Court of Session for the sessions division within which such Court or office is situate.
