BNSS_S379:3f873ca40cdd4d6d86c85d051f01b3: If a Court deems it expedient in the interests of justice to inquire into an offence referred to in clause (b) of sub-section (1) of section 215 committed in or in relation to a proceeding or document in that Court, it may, after a preliminary inquiry, record a finding, make a written complaint to a Magistrate of the first class having jurisdiction, take sufficient security for the accused’s appearance (or send the accused in custody if the offence is non-bailable), and bind over persons to give evidence. If the original Court has neither made a complaint nor rejected an application, the power may be exercised by the Court to which it is subordinate within the meaning of sub-section (4) of section 215. Such complaints must be signed by an appointed officer in a High Court, or by the presiding officer or an officer authorized in writing in any other case, where “Court” has the same meaning as in section 215.
BNSS_S385:4ff846c930da82632fe68a36e8686d: If the Court considers that a person accused of offences referred to in section 384, committed in its view or presence, should be imprisoned otherwise than in default of payment of fine, that a fine exceeding two hundred rupees should be imposed, or for any other reason of opinion, it shall record the facts constituting the offence and the statement of the accused before forwarding the case to a Magistrate having jurisdiction. The Court may require security for the appearance of such person, or if sufficient security is not given, shall forward such person in custody to such Magistrate. The Magistrate to whom any case is forwarded shall proceed to deal with it, as far as may be, as if it were instituted on a police report.
BNSS_S367:dba022ae47920a6dbca6f542d9ef81: If a Magistrate believes an accused is a person of unsound mind and incapable of making his defence, they must inquire into the fact, cause examination by a civil surgeon of the district or other State Government-directed medical officer, and examine said officer as a witness, potentially dealing with the person under section 369 pending inquiry. Upon the civil surgeon finding the accused is a person of unsound mind, the person is referred to a psychiatrist or clinical psychologist of a Government hospital or Government medical college for care, treatment, and prognosis to determine if the accused suffers from unsoundness of mind or intellectual disability, subject to a proviso where an aggrieved accused may appeal to a Medical Board consisting of the head of psychiatry unit in the nearest Government hospital and a faculty member in psychiatry in the nearest Government medical college. If the accused is found incapable of entering defence due to unsoundness of mind, the Magistrate—after examining prosecution evidence and hearing the advocate of the accused without questioning the accused—shall discharge the accused if no prima facie case is made out, or, per a proviso, if a prima facie case is made out, postpone proceedings for the period required for treatment as opined by the psychiatrist or clinical psychologist and deal with the accused under section 369. If the accused is found incapable of entering defence due to intellectual disability, the Magistrate shall order closure of the inquiry and deal with the accused under section 369.
BNSS_S383:5f8639522f67b140dd4abbc0a6f9ef: Upon delivery of any judgment or final order, a Court of Session or Magistrate of the first class may, if satisfied it is necessary and expedient in the interest of justice, take cognizance and summarily try a witness who knowingly or wilfully gave or fabricated false evidence with the intention that such evidence should be used in such proceeding, following the procedure prescribed for summary trials as nearly as may be practicable. After giving the offender a reasonable opportunity of showing cause, the offender may be sentenced to imprisonment for a term which may extend to three months, or to fine which may extend to one thousand rupees, or with both. This section does not affect the power of the Court to make a complaint under section 379 for the offence where the Court does not choose to proceed under this section. If an appeal or an application for revision is filed against the judgment or order, the Court shall stay further proceedings of the trial until the disposal of the appeal or application for revision, which the further proceedings shall abide by.
BNSS_S387:9e71173e7427cb6abdd9a1e6a9975e: When a Court has under section 384 adjudged an offender to punishment, or under section 385 forwarded him to a Magistrate for trial for refusing or omitting to do anything which he was lawfully required to do or for any intentional insult or interruption, the Court may, in its discretion, discharge the offender or remit the punishment. This relief is granted upon the offender's submission to the order or requisition of such Court, or on an apology being made to its satisfaction.
BNSS_S381:9eee8c1dda6d237e0a6861c90cbeb4: Any Court dealing with an application made to it for filing a complaint under section 379 or an appeal under section 380 is empowered under this section. Such Court shall have the power to make such order as to costs as may be just.
BNSS_S398:37bc683c725f699b93c2cd3db500bf: Section 398 mandates that every State Government shall prepare and notify a Witness Protection Scheme for the State. This mechanism is established with a view to ensure protection of the witnesses.
BNSS_S391:b0cab0f6032c598923931ce55fde74: Except as provided in sections 383, 384, 388 and 389, no Judge of a Criminal Court (other than a Judge of a High Court) or Magistrate shall try any person for any offence referred to in section 215. This prohibition applies when such offence is committed before themselves or in contempt of his authority, or is brought under his notice as such Judge or Magistrate in the course of a judicial proceeding.
BNSS_S392:9e71f08bcd84a0e3890a3f8b3c667c: In any Criminal Court of original jurisdiction, the presiding officer shall pronounce the judgment in open Court immediately after trial termination or within forty-five days by delivering the whole judgment, reading the whole, or reading the operative part and explaining the substance in a language understood by the accused or his advocate. Documentation requirements include shorthand transcription and signing for delivered judgments, dating and signing in open Court for read judgments, and providing free copies for operative-part pronouncements, with a Proviso that the Court shall, as far as practicable, upload the judgment on its portal within seven days. Accused in custody must attend in person or via audio-video electronic means, while those not in custody must attend unless attendance was dispensed with and the sentence is one of fine only or he is acquitted, subject to a Proviso allowing pronouncement despite the absence of some among multiple accused to avoid undue delay. Judgments are not invalid due to the absence of any party or advocate or notice defects, and this section does not limit the provisions of section 511.
BNSS_S394:2b08a1e800b208576a391048b1c845: Section 394 empowers a Court (other than a Magistrate of the second class), an Appellate Court, the High Court, or a Court of Session exercising revision powers to order that a person, previously convicted in India of an offence punishable with imprisonment for a term of three years, or upwards, and again convicted of an offence punishable with imprisonment for a term of three years or upwards, notify their residence and any change of, or absence from, such residence for a term not exceeding five years from the date of expiration of the sentence. This order applies to criminal conspiracies, abetment, and attempts to commit such offences, though it becomes void if the conviction is set aside on appeal or otherwise. Furthermore, the State Government may notify rules for the notification of residence, including punishments for breach, which are triable by a Magistrate of competent jurisdiction in the district where the place last notified as residence is situated.
BNSS_S400:a4299a64e70712e5bb055990285f28: Upon conviction in a complaint of a non-cognizable offence, the Court may order the accused to pay the complainant, in whole or in part, the cost incurred in the prosecution, including reasonable process-fees, witnesses, and advocate's fees. In default of payment, the accused shall suffer simple imprisonment for a period not exceeding thirty days. Such an order may also be made by an Appellate Court, the High Court, or the Court of Session when exercising its powers of revision.
BNSS_S389:54f64c88802bf9efd6f416f720a7d7: If a witness legally bound to appear before a Criminal Court in obedience to a summons neglects or refuses to attend at the specified place and time without just excuse, or departs the place before the time at which it is lawful for him to depart, the Court may, if satisfied it is expedient in the interests of justice, take cognizance of the offence. After giving the offender an opportunity of showing cause, the Court may sentence the witness to a fine not exceeding five hundred rupees. In every such case, the Court shall follow, as nearly as may be practicable, the procedure prescribed for summary trials.
BNSS_S402:75598ed98b8ec6f3e618892fd08f72: Section 402 mandates that where a Court could have dealt with an accused person under section 401 or the Probation of Offenders Act, 1958 (20 of 1958), or a youthful offender under the Juvenile Justice (Care and Protection of Children) Act, 2015 (2 of 2016) or any other law for treatment, training, or rehabilitation, but has not done so, it shall record the special reasons for not having done so. This obligation requires the Court to explicitly state these special reasons within its judgment.
BNSS_S405:df519a7ab8027d50f3b64111939d7e: The original judgment shall be filed with the record of the proceedings. Where the original is recorded in a language different from that of the Court, a translation thereof into the language of the Court shall be added to such record if either party so requires.
BNSS_S406:c3696577a279a051bb6f6c876ba40c: Section 406 mandates that in cases tried by the Court of Session or a Chief Judicial Magistrate, the Court or such Magistrate shall forward a copy of its or his finding and sentence (if any) to the District Magistrate. This copy must be sent to the District Magistrate within whose local jurisdiction the trial was held.
BNSS_S404:0d56638843e78310e15b4d63141017: Upon sentencing an accused to imprisonment, a copy of the judgment shall be provided immediately free of cost; further, upon application, a certified copy or translation shall be given without delay and free of cost if the judgment is appealable, subject to a Proviso that death sentences passed or confirmed by the High Court require an immediate free certified copy regardless of application. For death sentences where an appeal lies as of right, the Court must inform the accused of the period to prefer such appeal, and the rules regarding certified copies apply to orders under section 136 as they do to appealable judgments. Any person affected by a judgment or order may obtain copies of the judgment, order, deposition, or record upon application and payment of prescribed charges, subject to two Provisos allowing the Court to grant them free of cost for special reasons or to the Government via application by the Prosecuting Officer. Additionally, the High Court may prescribe rules for granting copies to persons not affected by a judgment or order upon payment of fees and subject to specified conditions.
BNSS_S408:b0b748db6d4f0255f7e7a47889b2cf: Section 408 empowers the High Court, upon submission of proceedings, to make a further inquiry or take additional evidence upon any point bearing upon the guilt or innocence of the convicted person, either by doing so itself or by directing the Court of Session to do so. Unless the High Court otherwise directs, the presence of the convicted person may be dispensed with during such inquiry or evidence collection. Where the High Court does not conduct the inquiry or take the evidence, the result must be certified to such Court.
BNSS_S407:486d810ca645670d100027e887f33e: When the Court of Session passes a sentence of death, the proceedings shall forthwith be submitted to the High Court, and the sentence shall not be executed unless it is confirmed by the High Court. The Court passing the sentence shall also commit the convicted person to jail custody under a warrant.
BNSS_S403:1419c9138fa2dba1e1cb84fc8bc84f: Save as otherwise provided by this Sanhita or by any other law for the time being in force, no Court, having signed its judgment or final order disposing of a case, shall alter or review the same. An exception is explicitly permitted to allow a Court to alter or review such judgment or final order solely to correct a clerical or arithmetical error.
BNSS_S393:60ea10ab0a7cc7766854581680643e: Every judgment under section 392 must be written in the language of the Court and contain the point or points for determination, the decision, and reasons, specifying the offence and the section of the Bharatiya Nyaya Sanhita, 2023 (45 of 2023) or other law for convictions, or directing liberty for acquittals; if conviction under the Bharatiya Nyaya Sanhita, 2023 (45 of 2023) is doubtful between two sections or parts of a section, the Court shall express the doubt and pass judgment in the alternative. For offences punishable with death, imprisonment for life, or imprisonment for a term of years, the judgment must state reasons for the sentence, including special reasons for death sentences which shall direct that the person be hanged by the neck till he is dead; furthermore, if an offence punishable with imprisonment for a term of one year or more results in a sentence of imprisonment for a term of less than three months, reasons must be recorded unless the sentence is imprisonment till the rising of the Court or the case was tried summarily. Every order under section 136, sub-section (2) of section 157, section 144, section 164, or section 166 must contain the point or points for determination, the decision, and the reasons.
BNSS_S410:db41ca4689fb9550d7782238d3115a: Under Section 410, in every case so submitted, the confirmation of the sentence, or any new sentence or order passed by the High Court, must adhere to specific signing requirements. When such Court consists of two or more Judges, the confirmation or new sentence or order shall be made, passed and signed by at least two of them.
BNSS_S397:281e15097b75e6b224286d957eb52a: All hospitals, public or private, whether run by the Central Government, the State Government, local bodies, or any other person, shall immediately provide first-aid or medical treatment, free of cost, to victims of offences covered under section 64, section 65, section 66, section 67, section 68, section 70, section 71, or sub-section (1) of section 124 of the Bharatiya Nyaya Sanhita, 2023 (45 of 2023), or under sections 4, 6, 8, or section 10 of the Protection of Children from Sexual Offences Act, 2012 (32 of 2012). Additionally, these hospitals shall immediately inform the police of such incident.
BNSS_S414:0bce53b6cc91e48ffd657755112791: Section 414 provides that any person ordered under section 136 to give security for keeping the peace or for good behaviour, or any person aggrieved by an order refusing to accept or rejecting a surety under section 140, may appeal such order to the Court of Session. A Proviso specifies that this section shall not apply to persons whose proceedings are laid before a Sessions Judge in accordance with the provisions of sub-section (2) or sub-section (4) of section 141.
BNSS_S416:55996283cd346511d21ff2afc496b1: Notwithstanding anything in section 415, Section 416 prohibits appeals where an accused person has pleaded guilty and has been convicted on such plea. No appeal shall lie if the conviction is by a High Court. If the conviction is by a Court of Session or Magistrate of the first or second class, no appeal is permitted except as to the extent or legality of the sentence.
BNSS_S413:ba6d3630f4aa587e5dc5e6b28d8f9d: No appeal shall lie from any judgment or order of a Criminal Court except as provided for by this Sanhita or by any other law for the time being in force. A Proviso stipulates that the victim shall have a right to prefer an appeal against any order passed by the Court acquitting the accused, convicting for a lesser offence, or imposing inadequate compensation. Such appeal shall lie to the Court to which an appeal ordinarily lies against the order of conviction of such Court.
BNSS_S399:bc595534e19ec5a976adbfc68930e1: If a person causes a police officer to arrest another person and the Magistrate determines there was no sufficient ground for such arrest, the Magistrate may award compensation, not exceeding one thousand rupees, to the person arrested for his loss of time and expenses in the matter, or to each person if more than one person is arrested, as the Magistrate thinks fit. All compensation awarded under this section may be recovered as if it were a fine, and if it cannot be so recovered, the person by whom it is payable shall be sentenced to simple imprisonment for such term not exceeding thirty days as the Magistrate directs, unless such sum is sooner paid.
BNSS_S411:55641dbc9d12e947ba62a12c1dbd42: Section 411 prescribes the procedure in case of difference of opinion when a case is heard before a Bench of Judges. If such Judges are equally divided in opinion, the case shall be decided in the manner provided by section 433.
BNSS_S420:935a9640bccb16f2030bc54bbef1b6: Section 420 stipulates that where the High Court, on appeal, has reversed an order of acquittal of an accused person and convicted him, he may appeal to the Supreme Court. This mechanism applies if the High Court has sentenced him to death, to imprisonment for life, or to imprisonment for a term of ten years or more.
BNSS_S418:069b99d1153c4c466b8cb1983a763f: Section 418 empowers the State Government, and the Central Government in cases investigated by an agency empowered under any Central Act other than this Sanhita, to direct the Public Prosecutor to appeal against the inadequacy of a sentence to the Court of Session (if passed by the Magistrate) or the High Court (if passed by any other Court). The Court of Session or High Court shall not enhance the sentence without providing the accused a reasonable opportunity of showing cause, during which the accused may plead for acquittal or reduction of the sentence. Appeals against sentences passed under section 64, section 65, section 66, section 67, section 68, section 70 or section 71 of the Bharatiya Nyaya Sanhita, 2023, shall be disposed of within a period of six months from the date of filing.
BNSS_S424:9ba355182afa45a52b4236081538d6: Under Section 424, if the appellant is in jail, they may present their petition of appeal and the copies accompanying the same to the officer in charge of the jail. The officer in charge of the jail shall thereupon forward such petition and copies to the proper Appellate Court.
BNSS_S428:11218e255b598bc0a1821f11fe1358: Section 428 mandates that the rules contained in Chapter XXIX regarding the judgment of a Criminal Court of original jurisdiction shall apply, so far as may be practicable, to the judgment in appeal of a Court of Session or Chief Judicial Magistrate. The section contains a Proviso stating that the accused shall not be brought up, or required to attend, to hear the judgment delivered, unless the Appellate Court otherwise directs.
BNSS_S401:293961f0e182c255d80a3e814c1e2f: Where no previous conviction is proved, a Court may release persons not under twenty-one years of age convicted of offences punishable with fine only or imprisonment for a term of seven years or less, or persons under twenty-one or any woman convicted of offences not punishable with death or imprisonment for life, on probation of good conduct via bond or bail bond for a period not exceeding three years, provided the offender or surety has a fixed place of abode or regular occupation. This is subject to a Proviso regarding the submission of proceedings from a Magistrate of the second class to a Magistrate of the first class, and the Court may also release an offender after due admonition for theft, theft in a building, dishonest misappropriation, cheating, or any offence under the Bharatiya Nyaya Sanhita, 2023, punishable with not more than two years’ imprisonment or fine only. Appellate Courts, the High Court, or Court of Session may make or set aside such orders via appeal or revision—subject to a Proviso that they shall not inflict a greater punishment than the original Court—and while failure to observe conditions allows for warrant issuance and apprehension, this section does not affect the Probation of Offenders Act, 1958, or the Juvenile Justice (Care and Protection of Children) Act, 2015.
BNSS_S415:d9470bd2b434dd80cb87f1a6610541: A person convicted in a trial held by a High Court in its extraordinary original criminal jurisdiction may appeal to the Supreme Court, whereas a person convicted by a Sessions Judge, an Additional Sessions Judge, or any other Court in which a sentence of imprisonment for more than seven years has been passed against them or another person at the same trial, may appeal to the High Court. Save as otherwise provided in sub-section (2), any person convicted by a Magistrate of the first class or of the second class, sentenced under section 364, or in respect of whom an order or sentence has been passed under section 401 by any Magistrate, may appeal to the Court of Session. Appeals filed against sentences passed under section 64, section 65, section 66, section 67, section 68, section 70, or section 71 of the Bharatiya Nyaya Sanhita, 2023, shall be disposed of within a period of six months from the date of filing.
BNSS_S421:08716ed6ac98883f6401a0a996b46b: Notwithstanding anything in this Chapter, when more persons than one are convicted in one trial and an appealable judgment or order has been passed in respect of any of such persons, a special right of appeal is established. Consequently, all or any of the persons convicted at such trial shall have a right of appeal.
BNSS_S426:5e0ac4b6e5a8ccbcf0a061533982c1: If the Appellate Court does not dismiss the appeal summarily, it shall give notice of the time and place of the hearing and furnish the grounds of appeal to the appellant or his advocate, the State Government's appointed officer, the complainant (in cases of conviction from a case instituted upon complaint), and the accused (if the appeal is under section 418 or section 419). The Court shall send for the record of the case and hear the parties, subject to a Proviso that the appeal may be disposed of without the record if it concerns only the extent or the legality of the sentence. Where the only ground for appeal from a conviction is the alleged severity of the sentence, the appellant shall not urge or be heard on any other ground except with the leave of the Court.
BNSS_S395:35abe80ddb99ab559a9fcc59ed2e8f: When a Court imposes a sentence of fine or a sentence (including a sentence of death) of which fine forms a part, it may order the fine to be applied to defray prosecution expenses, compensate for loss or injury recoverable in a Civil Court, pay compensation under the Fatal Accidents Act, 1855 (13 of 1855) for death or abetted death, or compensate a bona fide purchaser in cases of theft, criminal misappropriation, criminal breach of trust, cheating, or having dishonestly received or retained, or having voluntarily assisted in disposing of, stolen property knowing or having reason to believe the same to be stolen if such property is restored. If the case is subject to appeal, no payment shall be made before the period allowed for presenting the appeal has elapsed or the appeal is decided, though such orders may also be made by an Appellate Court, High Court, or Court of Session when exercising its powers of revision. When a Court imposes a sentence of which fine does not form a part, it may order the accused to pay specified compensation for loss or injury, and any sum paid or recovered under this section shall be taken into account in any subsequent civil suit relating to the same matter.
BNSS_S423:53821a2dea83fae97a7380eaee53ae: Every appeal shall be made in the form of a petition in writing presented by the appellant or his advocate. Unless the Court to which it is presented otherwise directs, every such petition shall be accompanied by a copy of the judgment or order appealed against.
BNSS_S432:827360e03b0c2c02ef0873bc09bf27: Under Section 432, an Appellate Court may, upon recording its reasons, take additional evidence itself or direct it to be taken by a Magistrate or, if the Appellate Court is a High Court, by a Court of Session or a Magistrate. Where evidence is taken by a Court of Session or Magistrate, it must be certified to the Appellate Court, which shall then proceed to dispose of the appeal. The accused or his advocate has the right to be present, and the taking of evidence is subject to the provisions of Chapter XXV, as if it were an inquiry.
BNSS_S434:5f92473b71bc1910902d945344731b: Judgments and orders passed by an Appellate Court upon an appeal shall be final, except in the cases provided for in section 418, section 419, sub-section (4) of section 425, or Chapter XXXII. A Proviso specifies that notwithstanding the final disposal of an appeal against conviction, the Appellate Court may hear and dispose of on the merits an appeal against acquittal under section 419 or an appeal for the enhancement of sentence under section 418, provided they arise out of the same case.
BNSS_S417:17112133d1efd69f5913831ea3e07a: Notwithstanding Section 415, no appeal is permitted for a convicted person where a High Court passes only a sentence of imprisonment for a term not exceeding three months or of fine not exceeding one thousand rupees, or both; a Court of Session passes only a sentence of imprisonment for a term not exceeding three months or of fine not exceeding two hundred rupees, or both; a Magistrate of the first class passes only a sentence of fine not exceeding one hundred rupees; or a Magistrate empowered under section 283 in a case tried summarily passes only a sentence of fine not exceeding two hundred rupees. A proviso allows an appeal if any other punishment is combined with such sentences, but excludes sentences that are appealable merely on the ground that the person convicted is ordered to furnish security to keep the peace, a direction for imprisonment in default of payment of fine is included, or more than one sentence of fine is passed if the total amount of fine imposed does not exceed the amount hereinbefore specified.
BNSS_S409:00c3fffbf10bcd6b88e1db1b635a33: In any case submitted under section 407, the High Court may confirm the sentence, pass any other sentence warranted by law, or acquit the accused person. The Court may also annul the conviction, and convict the accused of any offence of which the Court of Session might have convicted him, or order a new trial on the same or an amended charge. A proviso stipulates that no order of confirmation shall be made under this section until the period allowed for preferring an appeal has expired, or, if an appeal is presented within such period, until such appeal is disposed of.
BNSS_S419:c0efea2b4be7df5b6c6ead3b7b7c94: Subject to sub-sections (3) and (5), the District Magistrate may direct the Public Prosecutor to appeal to the Court of Session from a Magistrate's acquittal in a cognizable and non-bailable offence, while the State Government may direct an appeal to the High Court from an original or appellate order of acquittal by any Court other than a High Court (excluding orders under clause (a) or Court of Session in revision), and the Central Government may similarly direct such appeals if the investigation was conducted by an agency under a Central Act other than this Sanhita. Appeals to the High Court under sub-sections (1) or (2) require the leave of the High Court, though a complainant in a case instituted upon complaint may present an appeal to the High Court if granted special leave to appeal. Applications for special leave to appeal must be filed within six months if the complainant is a public servant, or sixty days in every other case from the date of the acquittal order, and if such application is refused, no appeal shall lie under sub-section (1) or sub-section (2).
BNSS_S433:cf0d904ff5e4759d56f77f3856ed9e: When an appeal under this Chapter is heard by a High Court before a Bench of Judges and they are divided in opinion, the appeal, with their opinions, shall be laid before another Judge of that Court. After such hearing as he thinks fit, that Judge shall deliver his opinion, and the judgment or order shall follow that opinion. Provided that if one of the Judges constituting the Bench, or the Judge to whom the appeal is laid under this section, so requires, the appeal shall be re-heard and decided by a larger Bench of Judges.
BNSS_S429:09466040aa15606ca666629403c94c: When a case is decided on appeal by the High Court under this Chapter, the High Court shall certify its judgment or order to the Court by which the finding, sentence or order appealed against was recorded or passed. If such Court is that of a Judicial Magistrate other than the Chief Judicial Magistrate, the judgment or order shall be sent through the Chief Judicial Magistrate, and if such Court is that of an Executive Magistrate, it shall be sent through the District Magistrate. The receiving Court shall thereupon make such orders as are conformable to the judgment or order of the High Court and, if necessary, amend the record in accordance therewith.
BNSS_S431:842a5d60325f94c5f0cb010bb5d9d8: When an appeal is presented under section 419, the High Court may issue a warrant directing that the accused be arrested and brought before it or any subordinate Court. The Court before which the accused is brought may commit him to prison pending the disposal of the appeal or admit him to bail.
BNSS_S425:430f94190d6ed9aa2161bb967e01e6: Upon examining a petition of appeal or judgment received under section 423 or section 424, the Appellate Court may summarily dismiss an appeal if there is no sufficient ground for interfering, potentially calling for the record of the case first. Under Provisos, section 423 appeals require a reasonable opportunity of being heard for the appellant or advocate, while section 424 appeals require a reasonable opportunity of being heard unless the appeal is frivolous or production of the accused in custody involves disproportionate inconvenience, and no summary dismissal under section 424 shall occur until the period allowed for preferring such appeal has expired. If the dismissing court is a Court of Session or a Chief Judicial Magistrate, it shall record its reasons, and if a section 424 appeal is dismissed summarily, the Court may, notwithstanding anything contained in section 434, hear and dispose of an unconsidered section 423 appeal if satisfied it is necessary in the interests of justice.
BNSS_S438:fffc34fca0f88df1b59e7a10f74156: The High Court or any Sessions Judge may call for and examine records of any proceeding from an inferior Criminal Court within their local jurisdiction to satisfy themselves regarding the correctness, legality or propriety of any finding, sentence or order, and the regularity of proceedings. In doing so, they may direct the suspension of any sentence or order execution and the release of the accused on their own bond or bail bond, with an Explanation clarifying that all Magistrates, whether Executive or Judicial and regardless of original or appellate jurisdiction, are deemed inferior to the Sessions Judge for this section and section 439. These revision powers shall not be exercised in relation to any interlocutory order passed in any appeal, inquiry, trial or other proceeding. Furthermore, if an application under this section is made by any person to either the High Court or the Sessions Judge, no further application by the same person shall be entertained by the other.
BNSS_S436:439e908e2cd7ef9bd4c6cce197149b: Under sub-section (1), if a Court determines a pending case involves a question as to the validity of any Act, Ordinance or Regulation (or provision therein) necessary for disposal, and believes it is invalid or inoperative—provided it has not been so declared by the subordinate High Court or the Supreme Court—the Court shall state its opinion and reasons and refer the case to the High Court. An Explanation defines “Regulation” as per the General Clauses Act, 1897 (10 of 1897) or the General Clauses Act of a State. Sub-section (2) empowers a Court of Session to refer any question of law to the High Court in cases where sub-section (1) does not apply. Per sub-section (3), a Court making such reference may, pending the High Court's decision, either commit the accused to jail or release him on bail.
BNSS_S437:57981cdac3d13d9f0ab54cec33ff1d: Upon a question being so referred, the High Court shall pass such order thereon as it thinks fit and cause a copy of such order to be sent to the Court by which the reference was made. The Court by which the reference was made shall dispose of the case conformably to the said order. Additionally, the High Court may direct by whom the costs of such reference shall be paid.
BNSS_S444:754c8de5c4fde3b2c4e20903bdfa68: Save as otherwise expressly provided by this Sanhita, no party has any right to be heard personally or by an advocate before any Court exercising its powers of revision. However, the Court may, if it thinks fit, hear any party either personally or by an advocate when exercising such powers.
BNSS_S439:dc0edad108a2bbaa77d96a3987524a: Upon examining any record under section 438 or otherwise, the High Court or the Sessions Judge may direct the Chief Judicial Magistrate, or the Chief Judicial Magistrate may himself or via a subordinate Magistrate, to conduct a further inquiry into any complaint dismissed under section 226 or sub-section (4) of section 227, or into the case of any person accused of an offence who has been discharged. This power is subject to a Proviso stating that no Court shall make any direction under this section for inquiry into the case of any person who has been discharged unless such person has had an opportunity of showing cause why such direction should not be made.
BNSS_S435:9ef486288d61d82f317655793aeb91: Every appeal under section 418 or section 419 shall finally abate on the death of the accused, and every other appeal under this Chapter, except an appeal from a sentence of fine, shall finally abate on the death of the appellant. A proviso stipulates that where an appeal is against a conviction and sentence of death or of imprisonment, any near relative may, within thirty days of the death of the appellant, apply to the Appellate Court for leave to continue the appeal, such that the appeal shall not abate if leave is granted. The Explanation defines “near relative” as a parent, spouse, lineal descendant, brother or sister.
BNSS_S442:ed9ea391721a80069db93ba3148a00: The High Court may, in its discretion, exercise powers conferred on a Court of Appeal by sections 427, 430, 431, and 432, or on a Court of Session by section 344, for proceedings it calls for or otherwise knows, with equally divided Judges disposing of cases per section 433. No order shall be made to the prejudice of the accused or other person without an opportunity of being heard personally or by advocate, and the High Court is not authorized to convert a finding of acquittal into one of conviction. Revision proceedings shall not be entertained at the instance of a party who could have appealed where an appeal lies and no appeal is brought. If a revision application is made under the erroneous belief that no appeal lies, the High Court may treat it as a petition of appeal if satisfied it is necessary in the interests of justice.
BNSS_S446:8aa1607533cff8aa12f78a7102a646: Section 446 empowers the Supreme Court to transfer any particular case or appeal from one High Court to another, or from a Criminal Court subordinate to one High Court to another Criminal Court of equal or superior jurisdiction subordinate to another High Court, whenever it is expedient for the ends of justice. The Supreme Court acts only on an application by motion from the Attorney-General of India or a party interested, which must be supported by affidavit or affirmation unless the applicant is the Attorney-General of India or the Advocate-General of the State. If such an application is dismissed as frivolous or vexatious, the Supreme Court may order the applicant to pay compensation to any person who opposed the application in such sum as it may consider appropriate.
BNSS_S441:97c0aa437ad41c80d345cf15734b8e: An Additional Sessions Judge shall have and may exercise all the powers of a Sessions Judge under this Chapter. This power applies in respect of any case which may be transferred to the Additional Sessions Judge by or under any general or special order of the Sessions Judge.
BNSS_S448:b5af01c1cdb8a46ce5243f6e3d7af1: Section 448 empowers a Sessions Judge to order the transfer of any particular case from one Criminal Court to another Criminal Court in his sessions division if expedient for the ends of justice. The Sessions Judge may act upon the report of the lower Court, an application of a party interested, or on his own initiative. Sub-sections (3), (4), (5), (6), (7), and (9) of section 447 apply to such applications, provided that the words “sum not exceeding ten thousand rupees” are substituted for the word “sum” in sub-section (7).
BNSS_S451:de3e2b9d1e1df5ff9bb7e85e721b40: Under Section 451, any District Magistrate or Sub-divisional Magistrate may make over, for disposal, any proceeding started before them to any Magistrate subordinate to them. They are further empowered to withdraw any case from, or recall any case made over to, any Magistrate subordinate to them. Following such withdrawal or recall, the District Magistrate or Sub-divisional Magistrate may dispose of the proceeding themselves or refer it for disposal to any other Magistrate.
BNSS_S454:6bc6137af04b991569e226769a55a9: When a sentence of death is passed by the High Court in appeal or in revision, the Court of Session shall cause the sentence to be carried into effect. This is achieved by the Court of Session issuing a warrant upon receiving the order of the High Court.
BNSS_S452:4fb0443be41c4ef5c09218ad67c5a6: A Sessions Judge or Magistrate making an order under section 448, section 449, section 450 or section 451 shall record his reasons for making it. This section contains no Provisos, Exceptions, or Explanations.
BNSS_S456:9b9c911fc0512bb94402b0c9014cf1: Section 456 provides for the commutation of sentence of death on pregnant woman. If a woman sentenced to death is found to be pregnant, the High Court shall commute the sentence to imprisonment for life.
BNSS_S449:4051d5f3c85b9e1e6bad2f3be1edc0: A Sessions Judge may withdraw any case or appeal from, or recall any case or appeal made over to, a Chief Judicial Magistrate subordinate to him under sub-section (1). Under sub-section (2), a Sessions Judge may recall any case or appeal made over to an Additional Sessions Judge at any time before the trial of the case or the hearing of the appeal has commenced. Upon such withdrawal or recall under sub-section (1) or sub-section (2), the Sessions Judge may either try the case in his own Court, hear the appeal himself, or make it over in accordance with the provisions of this Sanhita to another Court for trial or hearing.
BNSS_S458:759514c52e5ade95e9cfab59ec0003: Section 458 mandates that when an accused is sentenced to imprisonment for life or to imprisonment for a term (excluding cases under section 453), the Court shall forthwith forward a warrant and the accused to the jail or other place of confinement. A Proviso specifies that for imprisonment till the rising of the Court, preparing or forwarding a warrant to a jail is unnecessary, and the accused may be confined in such place as the Court may direct. Where the accused is not present in Court during sentencing, the Court shall issue a warrant for his arrest for forwarding him to the jail or other place of confinement, and the sentence shall commence on the date of his arrest.
BNSS_S453:e52c6e2bdf446afb0199b4f6874575: Upon receiving an order of confirmation or other order of the High Court in a case submitted to the High Court for the confirmation of a sentence of death, the Court of Session shall cause such order to be carried into effect. To execute the order passed under section 409, the Court of Session shall issue a warrant or take such other steps as may be necessary.
BNSS_S460:28f512670b81bb9525304fc6016934: Section 460 mandates the procedure for a "Warrant with whom to be lodged." It stipulates that when the prisoner is to be confined in a jail, the warrant shall be lodged with the jailor.
BNSS_S443:05b60c2c7083eaae590af57a497278: If persons convicted at the same trial apply to a High Court for revision and another person convicted at the same trial applies to the Sessions Judge for revision, the High Court shall decide, having regard to the general convenience of the parties and the importance of the questions involved, which of the two Courts should finally dispose of the applications. Applications transferred to the High Court shall be dealt with as if it were an application duly made before itself, and applications transferred to the Sessions Judge shall be dealt with as if it were an application duly made before himself. Where an application for revision is transferred by the High Court to the Sessions Judge, no further application for revision shall lie to the High Court or to any other Court at the instance of the person or persons whose applications for revision have been disposed of by the Sessions Judge.
BNSS_S470:11f04ec2bd6f12c357119a44cb31b8: Upon a sentence being fully executed, the officer executing it shall return the warrant to the Court from which it was issued. This return must include an endorsement under his hand certifying the manner in which the sentence has been executed.
BNSS_S466:b9051cc5bc3c66460db8a7e79a2445: Section 466 mandates that a sentence of death, imprisonment for life or fine passed on an escaped convict takes effect immediately. For a sentence of imprisonment for a term, it takes effect immediately if it is severer in kind than the sentence the convict was undergoing when he escaped, or takes effect after he has suffered imprisonment for a further period equal to that which, at the time of his escape, remained unexpired of his former sentence if it is not severer in kind. For the purposes of sub-section (2), a sentence of rigorous imprisonment shall be deemed to be severer in kind than a sentence of simple imprisonment.
BNSS_S464:56e0015af37a6ef4c613a9f9fc028f: Under Section 464(1), where an offender is sentenced to fine only and imprisonment in default of payment and the fine is not paid forthwith, the Court may order the fine payable in full within not more than thirty days, or in two or three installments (the first payable within not more than thirty days and others at intervals of not more than thirty days). The Court may suspend the execution of the sentence of imprisonment and release the offender upon a bond or bail bond conditioned for appearance; if the fine or any installment is not realised by the latest date, the Court may direct the sentence of imprisonment to be carried into execution at once. Section 464(2) extends these provisions to orders for payment of money where imprisonment may be awarded for non-recovery and money is not paid forthwith, specifying that if the person fails to enter into the required bond, the Court may at once pass sentence of imprisonment.
BNSS_S457:5f1e1950aa49c52ef17beb98433347: Unless otherwise provided by any law for the time being in force, the State Government may direct the place where any person liable to be imprisoned or committed to custody under this Sanhita shall be confined. A Court or Magistrate may direct that a person in confinement in a civil jail be removed to a criminal jail, and upon release, such person shall be sent back to the civil jail. Exceptions to this mandate exist if three years have elapsed since the person was removed to the criminal jail, deeming them released under section 58 of the Code of Civil Procedure, 1908, or if the Court certifies to the officer in charge of the criminal jail that the person is entitled to be released under section 58 of the Code of Civil Procedure, 1908.
BNSS_S463:668fae04ad918c0ff04721f313b96b: Notwithstanding anything in this Sanhita or in any other law for the time being in force, if an offender is sentenced to pay a fine by a Criminal Court in a territory to which this Sanhita does not extend, the Court may issue a warrant to the Collector of a district in territories to which this Sanhita extends to realise the amount as if it were an arrear of land revenue. Such warrant shall be deemed to be a warrant issued under clause (b) of sub-section (1) of section 461 by a Court in the territories to which this Sanhita extends. The provisions of sub-section (3) of section 461 regarding the execution of such warrant shall apply accordingly.
BNSS_S447:856673d802a5944f2cdea275615cea: Section 447 empowers the High Court to order an offence be tried by a Court not qualified under sections 197 to 205 (both inclusive) but otherwise competent, transfer cases/appeals to equal or superior jurisdiction, commit cases to a Court of Session, or transfer them to itself observing the original Court's procedure if a fair/impartial inquiry/trial is impossible, a question of law of unusual difficulty arises, or it is expedient for the ends of justice. The Court may act via lower Court report, application of an interested party, or its own initiative, subject to a Proviso that no application shall lie for transfer between Criminal Courts in the same sessions division unless rejected by the Sessions Judge. Applications must be made by motion supported by affidavit or affirmation (except for the Advocate-General), and accused applicants must provide written notice to the Public Prosecutor at least twenty-four hours before the hearing and may be directed to execute a bond or bail bond for compensation. The High Court may stay subordinate proceedings pending disposal (subject to a Proviso that such stay shall not affect remand power under section 346) and order compensation for frivolous or vexatious applications, without affecting Government orders under section 218.
BNSS_S455:3b80bbd820c66ad2ca447b1d1cc0d1: If a person is sentenced to death by the High Court and an appeal lies to the Supreme Court under sub-clause (a) or sub-clause (b) of clause (1) of article 134 of the Constitution, the High Court shall postpone execution until the period allowed for preferring such appeal expires or, if an appeal is preferred, until such appeal is disposed of. Where a death sentence is passed or confirmed by the High Court and an application is made for a certificate under article 132 or sub-clause (c) of clause (1) of article 134, the High Court shall postpone execution until the application is disposed of or, if a certificate is granted, until the period allowed for preferring an appeal to the Supreme Court on such certificate expires. Furthermore, if the High Court is satisfied that the person sentenced intends to present a petition to the Supreme Court for the grant of special leave to appeal under article 136 of the Constitution, the High Court shall postpone execution for a period it considers sufficient to enable the presentation of such petition.
BNSS_S459:65ee73005f56a1d0dba25d464b3da8: Every warrant for the execution of a sentence of imprisonment shall be directed to the officer in charge of the jail or other place. This direction pertains to the place in which the prisoner is, or is to be, confined.
BNSS_S465:5192d6e66c78ecdc80a4661103ab8d: Every warrant for the execution of a sentence may be issued by the Judge or Magistrate who passed the sentence. Alternatively, such warrant may be issued by his successor-in-office.
BNSS_S474:634947694408043d9256616aa5aa32: The appropriate Government may, without the consent of the person sentenced, commute a sentence of death to imprisonment for life, and a sentence of imprisonment for life to imprisonment for a term not less than seven years. The Government may further commute imprisonment for seven years or more to imprisonment for a term not less than three years, and imprisonment for less than seven years to fine. Additionally, a sentence of rigorous imprisonment may be commuted to simple imprisonment for any term to which that person might have been sentenced.
BNSS_S467:a96306198280b15cd91a4d2eb09a21: Under Section 467(1), if a person undergoing a sentence of imprisonment is sentenced on a subsequent conviction to imprisonment or imprisonment for life, such sentence shall commence at the expiration of the previous imprisonment unless the Court directs that the subsequent sentence shall run concurrently. A Proviso stipulates that where a person sentenced to imprisonment by an order under section 141 in default of furnishing security is sentenced for an offence committed prior to such order, the latter sentence shall commence immediately. Per subsection (2), when a person undergoing a sentence of imprisonment for life is sentenced on a subsequent conviction to imprisonment for a term or imprisonment for life, the subsequent sentence shall run concurrently with the previous sentence.
BNSS_S476:d0bd6474fb688aed76b8de17654099: Section 476 grants the Central Government concurrent power to exercise the powers conferred by sections 473 and 474 upon the State Government. This authority is specifically applicable in the case of sentences of death. This section contains no Provisos, Exceptions, or Explanations.
BNSS_S461:c4fb7de088dc3166cfb2b7f6ed4b3d: If an offender is sentenced to pay a fine but fails to make payment, the Court may issue a warrant for the levy of the amount by attachment and sale of any movable property, or issue a warrant to the Collector of the district to realise the amount as arrears of land revenue from the movable or immovable property, or both. A proviso to sub-section (1) stipulates that if the sentence directs imprisonment in default of payment and the offender has undergone the whole of such imprisonment, no warrant shall be issued unless the Court records special reasons in writing or has made an order for payment of expenses or compensation under section 395. The State Government may make rules regulating the execution of warrants under clause (a) of sub-section (1) and the summary determination of claims made by any person other than the offender in respect of attached property. Where a warrant is issued to the Collector under clause (b) of sub-section (1), the amount is realised in accordance with the law relating to recovery of arrears of land revenue, subject to a proviso that no such warrant shall be executed by the arrest or detention in prison of the offender.
BNSS_S472:bc6f17f97f1f1801b59de9c2ce17a2: A convict under the sentence of death, their legal heir, or any relative may file a mercy petition before the President of India under article 72 or the Governor of the State under article 161 of the Constitution within thirty days of the Superintendent of the jail informing them of the dismissal of appeal, review, or special leave to appeal by the Supreme Court, or the confirmation of death sentence by the High Court after the time for Supreme Court appeal or special leave has expired. Such petitions may be initially made to the Governor and, upon rejection or disposal, to the President within sixty days; the Superintendent of the jail must ensure all convicts file within sixty days or send all case records to the Central or State Government. The Central Government shall seek State Government comments and make recommendations to the President within sixty days of receipt, with the President deciding petitions of multiple convicts together and the Central Government communicating the order to the State Home Department and jail authorities within forty-eight hours. No appeal shall lie in any Court against the final order of the President or Governor under article 72 or article 161, nor shall the process of arriving at such decision be inquired into in any Court.
BNSS_S469:ceec2297ca646a851ff78a3011364d: Subsection (1) stipulates that nothing in section 466 or section 467 shall excuse any person from any part of the punishment to which he is liable upon his former or subsequent conviction. Under subsection (2), when an award of imprisonment in default of payment of a fine is annexed to a substantive sentence of imprisonment and the person undergoing the sentence is after its execution to undergo a further substantive sentence or further substantive sentences of imprisonment, effect shall not be given to the award of imprisonment in default of payment of the fine until the person has undergone the further sentence or sentences.
BNSS_S471:f7e99725adc623f70b93a248226a25: Any money (other than a fine) payable by virtue of any order made under this Sanhita, and the method of recovery of which is not otherwise expressly provided for, shall be recoverable as if it were a fine. The section includes a proviso that section 461, in its application to an order under section 400, shall be construed as if in the proviso to sub-section (1) of section 461, after the words and figures “under section 395”, the words and figures “or an order for payment of costs under section 400” had been inserted.
BNSS_S475:8d906ad8e00ee69d4e39af6c119c36: Notwithstanding anything contained in section 473, Section 475 restricts powers of remission or commutation where a sentence of imprisonment for life is imposed for an offence for which death is one of the punishments provided by law, or where a sentence of death has been commuted under section 474 into one of imprisonment for life. Under these circumstances, such person shall not be released from prison unless he had served at least fourteen years of imprisonment.
BNSS_S486:efdb60ce8aaa5eaf3da22abc3a5797: Every person standing surety to an accused person for his release on bail shall make a declaration before the Court. This declaration must specify the number of persons to whom he has stood surety, including the accused, and provide all relevant particulars.
BNSS_S478:5004a0ca1737deb2793d8383a5a2be: Any person other than a person accused of a non-bailable offence, arrested or detained without warrant by an officer in charge of a police station or brought before a Court, shall be released on bail if prepared to give bail. A Proviso stipulates that the officer or Court may, and shall if the person is indigent and unable to furnish surety, discharge them upon executing a bond for his appearance, with an Explanation that inability to give bail bond within a week of the date of his arrest is sufficient ground to presume the person is indigent; a further Proviso preserves the provisions of sub-section (3) of section 135 and section 492. Notwithstanding subsection (1), if a person fails to comply with bond or bail bond conditions regarding time and place of attendance, the Court may refuse subsequent bail in the same case, without prejudice to the Court's power to call upon any person bound by such bond or bail bond to pay the penalty under section 491.
BNSS_S484:b19b5771054377f315e536b187cffb: Under Section 484, the amount of every bond executed under this Chapter shall be fixed with due regard to the circumstances of the case and shall not be excessive. Furthermore, the High Court or the Court of Session may direct that the bail required by a police officer or Magistrate be reduced.
BNSS_S482:12957e7e1c9cf6931b0b52607852cb: A person apprehending arrest for a non-bailable offence may apply to the High Court or the Court of Session for a direction that, in the event of such arrest, they shall be released on bail. The Court may impose conditions, including making oneself available for interrogation by a police officer, not making any inducement, threat or promise to any person acquainted with the facts of the case, not leaving India without previous permission, and other conditions under sub-section (3) of section 480. If subsequently arrested without warrant by an officer in charge of a police station or if a Magistrate issues a bailable warrant in conformity with the direction, the person shall be released on bail if prepared to give bail. Per sub-section (4), this section does not apply to arrests for offences under section 65 and sub-section (2) of section 70 of the Bharatiya Nyaya Sanhita, 2023.
BNSS_S481:5ec6e595608a8071f38055acaa7130: Before conclusion of the trial and before disposal of the appeal, the Court trying the offence or the Appellate Court shall require the accused to execute a bond or bail bond to appear before the higher Court as and when such Court issues notice in respect of any appeal or petition filed against the judgment. Such bond shall be in force for six months, and if the accused fails to appear, the bond stands forfeited and the procedure under section 491 shall apply.
BNSS_S480:490adcf6e3edb4986a6e336fb2cfe3: Section 480 permits bail for persons accused of non-bailable offences arrested by an officer in charge of a police station or before a Court (excluding High Court or Court of Session), provided there are no reasonable grounds for guilt of an offence punishable with death or imprisonment for life, or prior convictions for cognizable offences punishable with death, imprisonment for life, imprisonment for seven years or more, or two or more cognizable offences punishable with imprisonment for three years or more but less than seven years. Four Provisos allow bail for children, women, the sick, or infirm; for other special reasons; stipulate that identification by witnesses or police custody beyond the first fifteen days are insufficient grounds for refusal; and mandate a hearing for the Public Prosecutor for offences punishable with death, imprisonment for life, or imprisonment for seven years or more. Bail or bonds are granted if reasonable grounds for a non-bailable offence are absent but further inquiry is required (subject to section 492), with mandatory conditions regarding attendance, commission of similar offences, and witness tampering for offences punishable with imprisonment which may extend to seven years or more, or offences under Chapters VI, VII, or XVII of the Bharatiya Nyaya Sanhita, 2023 (including abetment, conspiracy, or attempt), provided reasons for release are recorded in writing. Finally, the Court may re-arrest a person released on bail, grant bail in Magistrate trials not concluded within sixty days from the first date fixed for taking evidence, or release an accused on a bond after trial conclusion but before judgment if reasonable grounds exist that the accused is not guilty.
BNSS_S488:0c0bc68fc706783bcb7e2373a34781: Section 488 authorizes the Court, where insufficient sureties have been accepted through mistake, fraud or otherwise, or subsequently become insufficient, to issue a warrant of arrest to bring the person released on bail before it. The Court may then order the person to find sufficient sureties. Upon failing to do so, the Court may commit him to jail.
BNSS_S477:d9804124d62d0b1333fa77ed5a018d: Under Section 477(1), the State Government's powers under sections 473 and 474 to remit or commute a sentence require concurrence with the Central Government if the offence was investigated by an agency empowered to make investigation into an offence under any Central Act other than this Sanhita, involved the misappropriation or destruction of, or damage to, any property belonging to the Central Government, or was committed by a person in the service of the Central Government while acting or purporting to act in the discharge of his official duty. Additionally, under subsection (2), no order of suspension, remission or commutation of sentences passed by the State Government for a person convicted of offences relating to matters to which the executive power of the Union extends, and sentenced to separate terms of imprisonment which are to run concurrently, shall have effect unless an order for the suspension, remission or commutation of such sentences has also been made by the Central Government in relation to the offences committed with regard to matters to which the executive power of the Union extends.
BNSS_S473:617208bb317e1db9d468051b2fb2c3: The appropriate Government may, at any time, suspend the execution of a sentence or remit the whole or any part of the punishment, either without conditions or upon conditions accepted by the person sentenced, which may be fulfilled by the person or be independent of his will. Upon application, the appropriate Government may require the presiding Judge of the Court to provide an opinion with reasons and a certified copy of the trial record; if conditions are not fulfilled, the appropriate Government may cancel the suspension or remission, allowing any police officer to arrest the person without warrant and remand them to undergo the unexpired portion of the sentence. These provisions apply to Criminal Court orders restricting liberty or imposing liability upon a person or property, where “appropriate Government” means the Central Government for matters under the Union's executive power or the State Government in other cases. A Proviso stipulates that for any sentence (other than a sentence of fine) passed on a person above the age of eighteen years, petitions are only entertained if the person is in jail, presented through the officer in charge (if by the person) or containing a declaration that the person is in jail (if by another).
BNSS_S487:991de2b483f1f4626462b991ecc873: Upon the execution of a bond or bail bond, the person for whose appearance it has been executed shall be released. If the person is in jail, the court admitting him to bail shall issue an order of release to the officer in charge of the jail, who shall release him upon receipt of the orders. An exception is provided stating that nothing in section 487, section 478, or section 480 shall be deemed to require the release of any person liable to be detained for some matter other than that in respect of which the bond or bail bond was executed.
BNSS_S479:9484f19a3d434d4588d0aa8888fbdf: Under Section 479, an under trial prisoner (for offences not punishable by death or life imprisonment) who has undergone detention up to one-half of the maximum period of imprisonment specified for that offence under that law shall be released on bail, though a first-time offender may be released on bond after undergoing detention up to one-third of the maximum period of imprisonment. Per the second proviso, the Court may, after hearing the Public Prosecutor and recording reasons in writing, order continued detention longer than one-half or release on bail bond instead of bond, while the third proviso and Explanation mandate that detention during investigation, inquiry, or trial shall not exceed the maximum imprisonment and excludes delays caused by the accused. Notwithstanding sub-section (1) and subject to the third proviso, a person with multiple pending investigations, inquiries, or trials in more than one offence or multiple cases shall not be released on bail, and the Jail Superintendent must forthwith apply in writing to the Court for release upon completion of the one-half or one-third period.
BNSS_S485:32b866ad44c9e736c7cc4cf4900926: Before release on bond or bail bond, a person must execute a bond for a sum of money deemed sufficient by the police officer or Court, with one or more sufficient sureties conditioned that the person shall attend at the time and place mentioned until otherwise directed by the police officer or Court. The bond or bail bond shall contain any condition imposed for release and, if the case requires, bind the person to appear when called upon at the High Court, Court of Session, or other Court to answer the charge. To determine whether sureties are fit or sufficient, the Court may accept affidavits in proof of the facts contained therein or conduct an enquiry itself or cause an inquiry to be made by a Magistrate subordinate to the Court.
BNSS_S492:00bdcb0369ebf1dcfcdbeaf3315f8b: Without prejudice to the provisions of section 491, where a bond or bail bond for appearance of a person in a case is forfeited for breach of a condition, the bond executed by such person and the bond executed by one or more of his sureties shall stand cancelled. Subsequently, the person shall not be released only on his own bond if the police officer or the Court is satisfied there was no sufficient cause for the failure to comply with its condition. A Proviso specifies that, subject to other provisions of this Sanhita, the person may be released upon the execution of a fresh personal bond for such sum of money and bond by one or more of such sureties as the police officer or the Court thinks sufficient.
BNSS_S489:3ab80e50727f3a95185e788bb18398: All or any sureties for the attendance and appearance of a person released on bail may at any time apply to a Magistrate to discharge the bond, either wholly or so far as relates to the applicants. On such application, the Magistrate shall issue a warrant of arrest directing that the person so released be brought before him. Upon the appearance of such person pursuant to the warrant, or on his voluntary surrender, the Magistrate shall direct the bond to be discharged, call upon the person to find other sufficient sureties, and, if he fails to do so, may commit him to jail.
BNSS_S496:a13130c86d2d820464ae835c5c62a5: Section 496 empowers the High Court or Court of Session to direct any Magistrate to levy the amount due on a bond for appearance or attendance. This authority applies specifically to bonds for appearance or attendance at such High Court or Court of Session.
BNSS_S500:98fb07ac05f1b3f82093d7349783b6: Any person aggrieved by an order made by a Court or Magistrate under section 498 or section 499 may appeal to the Court to which appeals ordinarily lie from convictions by the former Court. The Appellate Court may direct the order to be stayed pending disposal of the appeal, or may modify, alter or annul the order and make any further orders that may be just. These powers may also be exercised by a Court of appeal, confirmation or revision while dealing with the case in which the order under sub-section (1) was made.
BNSS_S483:22dab75a60fff9612797df9bdf3c7d: A High Court or Court of Session may direct that an accused person in custody be released on bail, imposing conditions if the offence is specified in sub-section (3) of section 480, or may set aside or modify conditions imposed by a Magistrate. Provisos mandate giving notice to the Public Prosecutor before granting bail for offences triable exclusively by the Court of Session or punishable with imprisonment for life, unless the court is of opinion that it is not practicable to give such notice and records reasons in writing, and for offences triable under section 65 or sub-section (2) of section 70 of the Bharatiya Nyaya Sanhita, 2023, within fifteen days from the date of receipt of the notice of such application. For offences under section 65 or sub-section (2) of section 70 of the Bharatiya Nyaya Sanhita, 2023, the presence of the informant or any person authorised by him shall be obligatory at the hearing, and the court may further direct that any person released on bail under this Chapter be arrested and commit him to custody.
BNSS_S504:cf20812cce8af555a8817b75b9a8ed: If no person establishes a claim to property within six months and the person in whose possession such property was found cannot show it was legally acquired, the Magistrate may order that the property be at the disposal of the State Government. Such property may be sold by the State Government, and the proceeds of such sale shall be dealt with in such manner as the State Government may, by rules, provide. An appeal against any such order shall lie to the Court to which appeals ordinarily lie from convictions by the Magistrate.
BNSS_S493:4e5dbddc5838d8d7f0afe22774e4ed: When any surety to a bail bond becomes insolvent or dies, or when any bond is forfeited under the provisions of section 491, the Court by whose order such bond was taken or a Magistrate of the first class may order the person from whom such security was demanded to furnish fresh security in accordance with the directions of the original order. If such security is not furnished, such Court or Magistrate may proceed as if there had been a default in complying with such original order.
BNSS_S495:9f8bd570d5e93f2f06aed70910c262: All orders passed under section 491 shall be appealable. In the case of an order made by a Magistrate, the appeal shall be to the Sessions Judge, and in the case of an order made by a Court of Session, the appeal shall be to the Court to which an appeal lies from an order made by such Court.
BNSS_S512:c60d6da78992af45fbe723612d1cc5: Section 512 stipulates that no attachment made under this Sanhita shall be deemed unlawful. Furthermore, no person making such attachment shall be deemed a trespasser on account of any defect or want of form in the summons, conviction, writ of attachment, or other proceedings relating thereto.
BNSS_S499:7f9fa6101a25508ed5952831e612d0: Upon the conviction of a person for an offence which includes, or amounts to, theft or receiving stolen property, where an innocent purchaser bought the stolen property without knowing or having reason to believe it was stolen and money was taken from the convicted person's possession upon arrest, the Court may grant relief. Following the application of such purchaser and the restitution of the stolen property to the person entitled to the possession thereof, the Court may order that a sum not exceeding the price paid by such purchaser be delivered to him. Such delivery must be made within six months from the date of such order.
BNSS_S510:87dd98c59073dc71c499531e310ed1: No finding, sentence or order by a Court of competent jurisdiction shall be deemed invalid due to an omission to frame a charge, or any error, omission, irregularity, or misjoinder of charges, unless a Court of appeal, confirmation or revision finds a failure of justice has in fact been occasioned. If a failure of justice occurred, the Court of appeal, confirmation or revision may order the framing of a charge and recommencement of trial from that point for omissions, or direct a new trial upon a charge framed as it thinks fit for errors, omissions or irregularities. A Proviso stipulates that if the Court is of opinion that the facts of the case are such that no valid charge could be preferred against the accused in respect of the facts proved, it shall quash the conviction.
BNSS_S501:22616ff2c3b7a0392ab5b131e9d73c: Upon conviction under section 294, section 295, or sub-sections (3) and (4) of section 356 of the Bharatiya Nyaya Sanhita, 2023, the Court may order the destruction of all the copies of the thing in respect of which the conviction was had, provided they are in the custody of the Court or remain in the possession or power of the person convicted. Additionally, on a conviction under section 274, section 275, section 276 or section 277 of the Bharatiya Nyaya Sanhita, 2023, the Court may order the food, drink, drug or medical preparation in respect of which the conviction was had to be destroyed.
BNSS_S503:308078ecf6f32404e04ed396426f33: Upon a police officer's seizure of property being reported to a Magistrate and such property not being produced before a Criminal Court during an inquiry or trial, the Magistrate may order the disposal of the property, its delivery to the person entitled to the possession thereof, or, if such person cannot be ascertained, its custody and production. If the entitled person is known, the Magistrate may order delivery on such conditions as they think fit; if the person is unknown, the Magistrate may detain the property and shall issue a proclamation specifying the articles and requiring any person who may have a claim to appear and establish such claim within six months from the date of such proclamation.
BNSS_S505:ab4af05e5a891248e06cf8578fa8df: Section 505 empowers a Magistrate to whom seizure is reported to direct the sale of property if the person entitled to the possession is unknown or absent and the property is subject to speedy and natural decay, or if the Magistrate is of opinion that the sale would be for the benefit of the owner, or if the value of such property is less than ten thousand rupees. The provisions of sections 503 and 504 shall, as nearly as may be practicable, apply to the net proceeds of such sale.
BNSS_S509:974f12f45361b8de781827c710c871: If a Court, upon receiving or being tendered a confession or other statement of an accused person recorded or purporting to be recorded under section 183 or section 316, finds the Magistrate has not complied with the provisions of either section, it may, notwithstanding anything contained in section 94 of the Bharatiya Sakshya Adhiniyam, 2023, take evidence regarding such non-compliance. The Court may admit such statement if it is satisfied that the non-compliance has not injured the accused in his defence on the merits and that he duly made the statement recorded. These provisions apply to Courts of appeal, reference and revision.
BNSS_S513:5f1164fe170c15319bb8736b77baa0: For the purposes of this Chapter, Section 513 defines the term “period of limitation,” unless the context otherwise requires. This term specifically refers to the period specified in section 514 for taking cognizance of an offence.
BNSS_S491:95f1278de8aa75c165f56f8931f0e8: Where a bond for appearance, production of property, or any other bond is proved forfeited to the satisfaction of the Court, a transferee Court, or—for other bonds—the Court by which the bond was taken or a Magistrate of the first class, the Court shall record the grounds and call upon the bound person to pay the penalty or show cause, with the Explanation noting that conditions for appearance or production of property extend to transferee Courts. If sufficient cause is not shown and the penalty is not paid, the Court may recover the penalty as if it were a fine imposed by it under this Sanhita, though the Proviso states that if the penalty is not paid and cannot be recovered in that manner, the surety shall be liable to imprisonment in civil jail for a term which may extend to six months. The Court may remit any portion of the penalty after recording its reasons, and if a surety dies before the bond is forfeited, his estate shall be discharged from all liability. For security under section 125, section 136, or section 401, a certified copy of a judgment convicting a person of an offence breaching bond conditions (or a bond under section 494) may be used as evidence against the surety, and the Court shall presume the offence was committed unless the contrary is proved.
BNSS_S520:6ae0c78c500edd443aa921f052db51: Section 520 governs trials before High Courts. When an offence is tried by the High Court otherwise than under section 447, the High Court shall observe the same procedure as a Court of Sessions would observe if it were trying the case.
BNSS_S511:ff22c5ecadcece2d4f874d5c482283: Subject to the provisions hereinbefore contained, no finding, sentence, or order passed by a Court of competent jurisdiction shall be reversed or altered by a Court of appeal, confirmation of revision on account of any error, omission, or irregularity in the complaint, summons, warrant, proclamation, order, judgment, other proceedings before or during trial, inquiry, or any sanction for the prosecution, unless a failure of justice has in fact been occasioned. In determining whether such error, omission, or irregularity has occasioned a failure of justice, the Court shall have regard to the fact whether the objection could and should have been raised at an earlier stage in the proceedings.
BNSS_S507:4065b99987b70b38735cf6fc5444f3: Section 507 mandates that if any Magistrate, not being empowered by law in this behalf, performs specific actions, their proceedings shall be void. These actions include attaching and selling property under section 85, issuing search-warrants for items in postal authority custody, demanding security to keep the peace or for good behaviour, discharging persons lawfully bound to be of good behaviour, cancelling bonds to keep the peace, making orders for maintenance, making orders under section 152 as to a local nuisance, or prohibiting the repetition or continuance of a public nuisance under section 162. Furthermore, proceedings are void if the Magistrate makes an order under Part C or Part D of Chapter XI, takes cognizance of an offence under clause (c) of sub-section (1) of section 210, tries an offender or summarily, passes a sentence under section 364 on proceedings recorded by another Magistrate, decides an appeal, calls for proceedings under section 438, or revises an order passed under section 491.
BNSS_S515:222e81528da263dbca9f35f12e68af: The period of limitation, in relation to an offender, commences on the date of the offence, or, where the commission of the offence was not known to the person aggrieved or any police officer, on the first day such offence comes to the knowledge of such person or any police officer, whichever is earlier. Where the identity of the offender is unknown, the period commences on the first day the identity is known to the person aggrieved or the police officer making investigation into the offence, whichever is earlier. In computing the period, the day from which such period is to be computed shall be excluded.
BNSS_S517:42b747ea48d880bf3279d30a96f6c0: Where the period of limitation expires on a day when the Court is closed, the Court may take cognizance on the day on which the Court reopens. The section includes an Explanation stating that a Court shall be deemed to be closed on any day if it remains closed during its normal working hours.
BNSS_S519:798fc274302994f07d50410e939743: Notwithstanding anything contained in the foregoing provisions of this Chapter, any Court may take cognizance of an offence after the expiry of the period of limitation. Such cognizance is permissible if the Court is satisfied on the facts and in the circumstances of the case that the delay has been properly explained or that it is necessary so to do in the interests of justice.
BNSS_S523:34a739f12f2d728ea56e2f9cbb3e83: With the previous approval of the State Government, every High Court may make rules regarding persons permitted to act as petition-writers in subordinate Criminal Courts, regulating the issue of licences to such persons, their conduct of business, and the scale of fees to be charged. These rules may also provide a penalty for contravention of any rules so made, determine the authority for investigation and the imposition of penalties, and address any other matter required or permitted by State Government rules. All rules made under this section shall be published in the Official Gazette.
