BNSS_S525:7ae4251c21430d0f61929fd69107e4: No Judge or Magistrate shall try or commit for trial any case to or in which he is a party, or personally interested, except with the permission of the Court to which an appeal lies from his Court, and no Judge or Magistrate shall hear an appeal from any judgment or order passed or made by himself. The section includes an Explanation stating that a Judge or Magistrate shall not be deemed to be a party to, or personally interested in, any case by reason only that he is concerned therein in a public capacity, has viewed the place in which an offence is alleged to have been committed or any other place in which any other transaction material to the case is alleged to have occurred, or has made an inquiry in connection with the case.
BNSS_S527:d3d2eebc958477374b1f3a4b9f93bf: Section 527 pertains to a public servant having any duty to perform in connection with the sale of any property under this Sanhita. The section mandates that such a public servant shall not purchase or bid for the property.
BNSS_S521:fa6288fd971a6a90affeab96ef72ea: The Central Government may make rules consistent with this Sanhita, the Air Force Act, 1950 (45 of 1950), the Army Act, 1950 (46 of 1950), the Navy Act, 1957 (62 of 1957), and any other law relating to the Armed Forces of the Union, regarding whether persons subject to army, naval, or air-force law are tried by a Court or a Court-martial. A Magistrate, following such rules, shall in proper cases deliver a person charged with an offence liable to be tried by either, together with a statement of the offence, to the commanding officer of the unit or the nearest army, naval, or air-force station; the Explanation clarifies that “unit” includes a regiment, corps, ship, detachment, group, battalion or company, and “Court-martial” includes any Tribunal with similar powers. Magistrates shall use utmost endeavours to apprehend persons upon written application by the commanding officer of any unit or body of soldiers, sailors or airmen, and a High Court may direct a prisoner in a State jail be brought before a Court-martial for trial or examination.
BNSS_S529:2c94a5f6b9775425c2c766fecc2bd5: Every High Court shall exercise its superintendence over the subordinate Courts of Session and Courts of Judicial Magistrates. This duty is mandated to ensure an expeditious and proper disposal of cases by the Judges and Magistrates.
BSA_S8:7fb3bd3c5c73eaebead5e0348be64d564: Where there is reasonable ground to believe that two or more persons have conspired together to commit an offence or an actionable wrong, anything said, done or written by any one of such persons in reference to their common intention, after the time when such intention was first entertained by any one of them, is a relevant fact as against each of the persons believed to be so conspiring. Such facts are relevant both for the purpose of proving the existence of the conspiracy and for the purpose of showing that any such person was a party to it. As demonstrated in the Illustration, these facts remain relevant to prove existence and complicity even if a person was ignorant of them, if the actors were strangers, or if the acts occurred before they joined or after they left the conspiracy.
BSA_S6:a93cf10990b37f715e7eb2e0bba068bdc: Section 6 deems any fact constituting motive or preparation for any fact in issue or relevant fact relevant, alongside the previous or subsequent conduct of any party, agent to any party, or the person against whom an offence is the subject of a proceeding, provided such conduct influences or is influenced by a fact in issue or relevant fact. Explanation 1 stipulates that "conduct" excludes statements unless those statements accompany and explain acts other than statements, without affecting the relevancy of statements under any other section. Explanation 2 provides that when the conduct of any person is relevant, any statement made to him or in his presence and hearing which affects such conduct is also relevant.
BSA_S12:ec1745ee2822b3b6c102aeeb1130820a: Facts showing the existence of any state of mind—such as intention, knowledge, good faith, negligence, rashness, ill-will, or goodwill towards any particular person—or any state of body or bodily feeling are relevant when such states are in issue or relevant. Explanation 1 mandates that a fact showing a relevant state of mind must demonstrate that the state of mind exists in reference to the particular matter in question, not generally. Explanation 2 stipulates that where the previous commission by an accused of an offence is relevant within the meaning of this section, the previous conviction of such person shall also be a relevant fact.
BSA_S4:dfa7f8443f6cad66c6962372ee3b725d7: Section 4 states that facts not in issue are relevant if they are so connected with a fact in issue or a relevant fact as to form part of the same transaction, whether occurring at the same time and place or at different times and places. Through illustrations, this includes statements or actions by an accused, victim, or bystanders during a beating, and acts such as destroying property, attacking troops, or breaking open jails during an armed insurrection (waging war against the Government of India) even if the accused was not present at all occurrences. The section further encompasses letters in correspondence relating to a libel and the successive delivery of goods to intermediate persons.
BNS_S38:905a8c22f8e496455813661d80040252: Subject to the restrictions specified in section 37, the right of private defence of the body extends to the voluntary causing of death or of any other harm to the assailant if the offence is an assault causing apprehension of death or grievous hurt, or an assault with the intention of committing rape, gratifying unnatural lust, kidnapping or abducting, or wrongfully confining a person under circumstances causing apprehension of inability to have recourse to the public authorities. The right further extends to an act of throwing or administering acid or an attempt to throw or administer acid which may reasonably cause the apprehension that grievous hurt will otherwise be the consequence.
BNS_S41:65c6a723110a2d12f1e8e9e12ea06e08: Subject to the restrictions specified in section 37, the right of private defence of property extends to the voluntary causing of death or any other harm to the wrong-doer if the offence or attempt to commit it is robbery, house-breaking after sunset and before sunrise, or mischief by fire or any explosive substance committed on any building, tent or vessel used as a human dwelling or as a place for the custody of property. Additionally, this right extends to theft, mischief, or house-trespass under such circumstances as may reasonably cause apprehension that death or grievous hurt will be the consequence if such right of private defence is not exercised.
BNS_S108:b66e3891ac0500e8789dc90d6af070a: Under Section 108 of the Bharatiya Nyaya Sanhita, 2023, if any person commits suicide, whoever abets the commission of such suicide shall be punished. The penalty for this offence is imprisonment of either description for a term which may extend to ten years, and the individual shall also be liable to fine.
BNS_S93:2da3750b73293d224a6d5cafa8779499: Section 93 stipulates that whoever, being the father or mother of a child under the age of twelve years, or having the care of such child, exposes or leaves such child in any place with the intention of wholly abandoning such child, shall be punished with imprisonment of either description for a term which may extend to seven years, or with fine, or with both. The section includes an Explanation clarifying that it is not intended to prevent the trial of the offender for murder or culpable homicide, as the case may be, if the child die in consequence of the exposure.
BNS_S138:75e14e3ed24edeea64ec59bebd789e0: Section 138 defines the offence of "Abduction." It states that whoever by force compels, or by any deceitful means induces, any person to go from any place, is said to abduct that person.
BNS_S130:543c3fe7dd9cfc45e3ad99ddb283495: Section 130 defines assault as occurring when a person makes any gesture, or any preparation intending or knowing it to be likely that such gesture or preparation will cause any person present to apprehend that he who makes that gesture or preparation is about to use criminal force to that person. An Explanation specifies that mere words do not amount to an assault, but the words used may give gestures or preparations such a meaning as may make those gestures or preparations amount to an assault.
BNS_S147:78a24bc46e9940f8df8d0ce8cb4470e: Whoever wages war against the Government of India, attempts to wage such war, or abets the waging of such war, shall be punished with death, or imprisonment for life and shall also be liable to fine. An illustration specifies that joining an insurrection against the Government of India constitutes the offence defined in this section.
BNS_S172:4e9e232f2d4178ecf52d5803b64cb53: Section 172 defines the offence of personation at an election, committed by anyone who applies for a voting paper in the name of any other person (whether living or dead), in a fictitious name, or applies in their own name after having already voted once at the same election. The offence also encompasses any person who abets, procures, or attempts to procure voting by any person in any such way. A Proviso states that nothing in this section shall apply to a person authorized to vote as proxy for an elector under any law for the time being in force, insofar as they vote as a proxy for such elector.
BNS_S144:f5d3f0247f917d4d3074c093a4b26de: Section 144 penalizes whoever, knowingly or having reason to believe that a child has been trafficked, engages such child for sexual exploitation in any manner with rigorous imprisonment for a term which shall not be less than five years, but which may extend to ten years, and a fine. Furthermore, whoever, knowingly or having reason to believe that a person has been trafficked, engages such person for sexual exploitation in any manner shall be punished with rigorous imprisonment for a term which shall not be less than three years, but which may extend to seven years, and shall also be liable to fine.
BNS_S203:b32ddd1801307ede11e131d3222c69a: Section 203 stipulates that whoever, being a public servant and legally bound as such public servant not to purchase or bid for certain property, purchases or bids for that property in his own name, in the name of another, jointly, or in shares with others, commits an offence. Such person shall be punished with simple imprisonment for a term which may extend to two years, or with fine, or with both. Additionally, the property, if purchased, shall be confiscated.
BNS_S142:3b030979b6b646ecddd63c880c69740: Section 142 applies to whoever, knowing that any person has been kidnapped or has been abducted, wrongfully conceals or confines such person. Such person shall be punished in the same manner as if he had kidnapped or abducted such person with the same intention or knowledge, or for the same purpose as that with or for which he conceals or detains such person in confinement.
BNS_S260:78a3f3b2f7dc1a7b9551a6234b71995: Section 260 penalizes a public servant legally bound to apprehend or keep in confinement any person under sentence of a Court for any offence or lawfully committed to custody who intentionally omits to apprehend, intentionally suffers such person to escape, or intentionally aids such person in escaping or attempting to escape from such confinement. The punishment is (a) imprisonment for life or imprisonment of either description for a term which may extend to fourteen years, with or without fine, if the person is under sentence of death; (b) imprisonment of either description for a term which may extend to seven years, with or without fine, if the person is subject, by a sentence of a Court or commutation, to imprisonment for life or imprisonment for a term of ten years, or upwards; or (c) imprisonment of either description for a term which may extend to three years, or with fine, or with both, if the person is subject by a sentence of a Court to imprisonment for a term not extending to ten years or was lawfully committed to custody.
BNS_S220:e210963f322508d3925ec2ac7156340: Section 220 penalizes whoever, at any sale of property held by the lawful authority of a public servant, as such, purchases or bids for property on account of any person, whether himself or any other, whom he knows to be under a legal incapacity to purchase that property at that sale. The offence also includes bidding for such property not intending to perform the obligations under which one lays oneself by such bidding. Such person shall be punished with imprisonment of either description for a term which may extend to one month, or with fine which may extend to two hundred rupees, or with both.
BNS_S271:0540adc0640d285b21f43c25060350b: Section 271 applies to whoever unlawfully or negligently does any act which they know or have reason to believe is likely to spread the infection of any disease dangerous to life. The prescribed punishment is imprisonment of either description for a term which may extend to six months, or with fine, or with both.
BNSS_S81:47e02c46a57162958e3b4865789f6e6: When a warrant directed to a police officer is to be executed beyond the local jurisdiction of the issuing Court, it shall ordinarily be taken for endorsement to an Executive Magistrate or a police officer not below the rank of an officer in charge of a police station within the local limits of the execution jurisdiction. The endorsing Magistrate or police officer shall endorse his name, providing sufficient authority for execution and requiring local police to assist if so required. However, if there is reason to believe that the delay occasioned by obtaining such endorsement will prevent execution, the police officer may execute the warrant without endorsement in any place beyond the local jurisdiction of the issuing Court.
BNSS_S66:fc4e19664f2ae80507ff41734ca589f: Where the person summoned cannot, by the exercise of due diligence, be found, the summons may be served by leaving one of the duplicates with some adult member of his family residing with him. If so required by the serving officer, the person with whom the summons is left shall sign a receipt therefor on the back of the other duplicate. The section includes an Explanation stating that a servant is not a member of the family within the meaning of this section.
BNSS_S159:00d5e614d62b5907a1be8efd3638b4: Under Section 159, where a Magistrate directs a local investigation under section 158, the Magistrate may furnish the person with written instructions for guidance and declare who shall pay the necessary expenses. The report of such person may be read as evidence in the case. Additionally, when a Magistrate summons and examines an expert under section 158, the Magistrate may direct by whom the costs of such summoning and examination shall be paid.
BNS_S301:f603c53afeacc0d294c79d36fd54255: Section 301 penalizes any person who, with the intention of wounding the feelings of any person, or of insulting the religion of any person, or with the knowledge that the feelings of any person are likely to be wounded, or that the religion of any person is likely to be insulted thereby, commits any trespass in any place of worship, on any place of sepulchre, or any place set apart for the performance of funeral rites or as a depository for the remains of the dead. The offence further includes offering any indignity to any human corpse or causing disturbance to any persons assembled for the performance of funeral ceremonies. Such person shall be punished with imprisonment of either description for a term which may extend to one year, or with fine, or with both.
BNSS_S111:00a3705e926066530b12368e4f2965: Section 111 defines "contracting State" as any country or place outside India with arrangements made by the Central Government through a treaty or otherwise. It defines "identifying" as the establishment of proof that property was derived from or used in the commission of an offence, and "tracing" as determining the nature, source, disposition, movement, title or ownership of property. "Proceeds of crime" refers to any property derived or obtained directly or indirectly from criminal activity (including crime involving currency transfers) or the value of such property, while "property" encompasses all corporeal or incorporeal, movable or immovable, tangible or intangible assets, including deeds and instruments evidencing title to or interest in property derived or used in the commission of an offence and property obtained through proceeds of crime.
BNSS_S25:0aef5885ddf99c15339d24a5f374192: When a person is convicted at one trial of two or more offences, the Court, subject to section 9 of the Bharatiya Nyaya Sanhita, 2023, may sentence them to several prescribed punishments to run concurrently or consecutively based on the gravity of offences. For consecutive sentences, the Court is not required to send the offender for trial before a higher Court solely because the aggregate punishment exceeds the punishment it is competent to inflict for a single offence, subject to a Proviso that (a) in no case shall such person be sentenced to imprisonment for a longer period than twenty years and (b) the aggregate punishment shall not exceed twice the amount of punishment which the Court is competent to inflict for a single offence. For the purpose of appeal by a convicted person, the aggregate of the consecutive sentences passed under this section shall be deemed to be a single sentence.
BNSS_S169:90ce4f670725782f1e0f8b4a52287d: Every police officer receiving information of a design to commit any cognizable offence shall communicate such information to the police officer to whom he is subordinate. This communication must also be made to any other officer whose duty it is to prevent or take cognizance of the commission of any such offence.
BNSS_S203:e539cfe0d52951f13e2071e60d6272: Section 203 provides that when an offence is committed while the person by or against whom, or the thing in respect of which, the offence is committed is performing a journey or voyage, the offence may be inquired into or tried by a Court. This jurisdiction extends to any Court through or into whose local jurisdiction that person or thing passed in the course of that journey or voyage.
BNSS_S228:6198642115aa6572cb10cd70fc6925: Under Section 228(1), a Magistrate issuing a summons may, if reason exists, dispense with the personal attendance of the accused and permit him to appear by his advocate. Pursuant to Section 228(2), the Magistrate inquiring into or trying the case may, in his discretion at any stage of the proceedings, direct and, if necessary, enforce the personal attendance of the accused in the manner hereinbefore provided.
BNSS_S99:af0a2aec7faadb0fb5ea2b611138fe4: Any person having any interest in any newspaper, book or other document subject to a declaration of forfeiture under section 98 may, within two months from the date of publication in the Official Gazette, apply to the High Court to set aside such declaration on the ground that the issue did not contain matter referred to in sub-section (1) of section 98. Such applications shall be heard and determined by a Special Bench composed of three Judges if the High Court consists of three or more Judges, or by all Judges if the High Court consists of less than three Judges, with decisions in cases of difference of opinion being in accordance with the opinion of the majority. If the High Court is not satisfied that the issue contained matter referred to in sub-section (1) of section 98, it shall set aside the declaration of forfeiture, and for newspapers, copies may be given in evidence to prove the nature or tendency of words, signs or visible representations.
BNSS_S198:244ef23d4d24a998d549e91562708c: Section 198 provides that an inquiry or trial may be conducted by a Court having jurisdiction over any of such local areas if it is uncertain in which of several local areas an offence was committed, or where an offence is committed partly in one local area and partly in another. This jurisdiction also applies where an offence is a continuing one, and continues to be committed in more local areas than one, or where the offence consists of several acts done in different local areas.
BNS_S70:16344ab5ff7c10a3e1f7a28d9217165e: Section 70(1) stipulates that where a woman is raped by one or more persons constituting a group or acting in furtherance of a common intention, each person shall be deemed to have committed the offence of rape and shall be punished with rigorous imprisonment for a term which shall not be less than twenty years, but which may extend to imprisonment for life which shall mean imprisonment for the remainder of that person’s natural life, and with fine. Two provisos for this sub-section mandate that such fine shall be just and reasonable to meet the medical expenses and rehabilitation of the victim and shall be paid to the victim. Under Section 70(2), where a woman under eighteen years of age is raped by one or more persons constituting a group or acting in furtherance of a common intention, each person shall be deemed to have committed the offence of rape and shall be punished with imprisonment for life, which shall mean imprisonment for the remainder of that person’s natural life, and with fine, or with death, subject to the same two provisos regarding the fine being just and reasonable to meet the medical expenses and rehabilitation of the victim and being paid to the victim.
BNSS_S292:e907d8f2baf6710c93c00109694553: Where a satisfactory disposition of the case is worked out in a meeting under section 291, the Court shall prepare a report signed by the presiding officer and all other persons who participated in the meeting. If no such disposition has been worked out, the Court shall record such observation and proceed in accordance with the provisions of this Sanhita from the stage the application under sub-section (1) of section 290 has been filed.
BNSS_S210:cc140565c60538929e70068bfa59ef: Subject to the provisions of this Chapter, any Magistrate of the first class, and any Magistrate of the second class specially empowered under sub-section (2), may take cognizance of any offence upon receiving a complaint of facts (including complaints filed by a person authorised under any special law), upon a police report submitted in any mode including electronic mode, or upon information received from any person other than a police officer or upon the Magistrate's own knowledge. Additionally, the Chief Judicial Magistrate may empower any Magistrate of the second class to take cognizance under sub-section (1) of offences that are within their competence to inquire into or try.
BNSS_S302:1534ae9d039b6e378d60a116eb4081: Section 302 empowers a Criminal Court to order the officer in charge of the prison to produce a confined or detained person for answering to a charge of an offence, for the purpose of any proceedings, or for examination as a witness. An order issued by a Magistrate of the second class must be countersigned by the Chief Judicial Magistrate to whom such Magistrate is subordinate to be forwarded to or acted upon by the officer in charge of the prison. Such orders must be accompanied by a statement of the facts rendering the order necessary, which the Chief Judicial Magistrate may consider before declining to countersign.
BNSS_S230:5f083b20a86417abb7afb986083871: In proceedings instituted on a police report, the Magistrate shall, without delay and in no case beyond fourteen days from the date of production or appearance of the accused, furnish free of cost to the accused and the victim (if represented by an advocate) the police report, the first information report recorded under section 173, statements recorded under sub-section (3) of section 180 (excluding parts requested for exclusion under sub-section (7) of section 193), confessions and statements recorded under section 183, and any other document or relevant extract forwarded under sub-section (6) of section 193. The first proviso permits the Magistrate to direct that requested excluded parts of statements be furnished after perusing them and considering the police officer's reasons; the second proviso allows the Magistrate, if satisfied a document is voluminous, to furnish copies through electronic means or direct personal or advocate inspection in Court; and the third proviso stipulates that supply of documents in electronic form shall be considered duly furnished.
BNSS_S268:f05c0792cdd3ec062efe90884364d4: Upon taking all the evidence referred to in section 267, the Magistrate shall discharge the accused if, for reasons to be recorded, the Magistrate considers that no case against the accused has been made out which, if unrebutted, would warrant his conviction. Furthermore, a Magistrate may discharge the accused at any previous stage of the case if, for reasons to be recorded by such Magistrate, he considers the charge to be groundless.
BNSS_S354:f7026432866eef67cc57443c0731c6: Except as provided in sections 343 and 344, no influence shall be used to an accused person to induce him to disclose or withhold any matter within his knowledge. This prohibition applies to influence exerted by means of any promise or threat or otherwise.
BNSS_S378:b1babb3eae6c1bfb2e5f623955c653: Under Section 378(1), the State Government may order the delivery of a person detained under section 369 or section 374 to the care and custody of a relative or friend upon application and provision of security ensuring the person is properly cared for, prevented from doing injury to himself or others, produced for inspection by designated officers, and, if detained under sub-section (2) of section 369, produced before the Magistrate or Court. Per Section 378(2), if a person accused of an offence whose trial was postponed due to unsound mind and incapacity to make his defence is certified by the inspecting officer as capable of making his defence, the Magistrate or Court shall require the relative or friend to produce the accused. Upon such production, the Magistrate or Court shall proceed in accordance with section 371, and the certificate of the inspecting officer shall be receivable as evidence.
BNSS_S338:d91f2663ab43730a41ea137c9e0795: The Public Prosecutor or Assistant Public Prosecutor in charge of a case may appear and plead without any written authority before any Court in which that case is under inquiry, trial or appeal. If a private person instructs an advocate to prosecute, the Public Prosecutor or Assistant Public Prosecutor shall conduct the prosecution, and the instructed advocate shall act under their directions and may, with the permission of the Court, submit written arguments after the evidence is closed in the case.
BNSS_S129:1d92e9e6b764288843fba15f98aa83: Upon receiving information that a person within his local jurisdiction is by habit a robber, house-breaker, thief, forger, receiver of stolen property knowing the same to have been stolen, or habitually protects or harbours thieves or aids in the concealment or disposal of stolen property, an Executive Magistrate may intervene. The Magistrate may also act if the person habitually commits, attempts to commit, or abets kidnapping, abduction, extortion, cheating, mischief, any offence under Chapter X or sections 178, 179, 180, or 181 of the Bharatiya Nyaya Sanhita, 2023, offences involving a breach of the peace, or offences under the Drugs and Cosmetics Act, 1940, Foreigners Act, 1946, Employees' Provident Fund and Miscellaneous Provisions Act, 1952, Essential Commodities Act, 1955, Protection of Civil Rights Act, 1955, Customs Act, 1962, Food Safety and Standards Act, 2006, or laws regarding hoarding, profiteering, adulteration of food or drugs, or corruption, or is so desperate and dangerous as to render his being at large without security hazardous to the community. In such cases, the Magistrate may require the person to show cause why he should not be ordered to execute a bail bond for his good behaviour for a period not exceeding three years, as the Magistrate thinks fit.
BNSS_S388:3f4a6dbe176bb7b4f5e98227130785: If a witness or person called to produce a document or thing before a Criminal Court refuses to answer questions or produce any document or thing in his possession or power without offering a reasonable excuse after a reasonable opportunity, the Court may, for reasons to be recorded in writing, sentence him to simple imprisonment. Alternatively, by warrant under the hand of the Presiding Magistrate or Judge, the Court may commit him to the custody of an officer of the Court for any term not exceeding seven days, unless he consents to be examined, answer, or produce the document or thing. In the event of persisting in his refusal, such person may be dealt with according to the provisions of section 384 or section 385.
BNSS_S422:0de1db71c717a5215b100d980c5763: Subject to sub-section (2), an appeal to the Court of Session or Sessions Judge shall be heard by the Sessions Judge or an Additional Sessions Judge, though a Proviso allows an appeal against a conviction on a trial held by a Magistrate of the second class to be heard and disposed of by the Chief Judicial Magistrate. Pursuant to sub-section (2), an Additional Sessions Judge or a Chief Judicial Magistrate shall hear only such appeals as the Sessions Judge of the division makes over by general or special order, or as the High Court directs via special order.
BNSS_S445:2d0b321604b3623b3783474747a0fa: When a case is revised under this Chapter by the High Court or a Sessions Judge, it or he shall, in the manner provided by section 429, certify its decision or order to the Court by which the finding, sentence or order revised was recorded or passed. The Court to which the decision or order is so certified shall thereupon make such orders as are conformable to the decision so certified. If necessary, the record shall be amended in accordance therewith.
BNSS_S440:c5be71da5d155318c314d41a302fc7: The Sessions Judge may exercise all or any powers exercisable by the High Court under sub-section (1) of section 442 for any proceeding whose record they have called for. In such proceedings, the provisions of sub-sections (2), (3), (4) and (5) of section 442 apply, with references to the High Court construed as references to the Sessions Judge. Decisions of the Sessions Judge on revision applications made by or on behalf of any person are final, and no further proceeding by way of revision at the instance of such person shall be entertained by the High Court or any other Court.
BNSS_S427:fd03e22e6b1d00d3707f996eb5804c: After perusing the record and hearing the appellant, their advocate, the Public Prosecutor, and the accused (in appeals under section 418 or section 419), the Appellate Court may dismiss the appeal if there is no sufficient ground for interfering. In appeals from orders of acquittal, it may reverse the order to direct further inquiry, re-trial, commitment for trial, or find the accused guilty and pass sentence; in appeals from conviction, it may reverse the finding and sentence to acquit or discharge the accused, order re-trial or commitment, alter the finding maintaining the sentence, or alter the nature or extent of the sentence without enhancing it. In appeals for enhancement of sentence, the Court may reverse findings to acquit or discharge, order re-trial, alter the finding maintaining the sentence, or alter the nature or extent of the sentence to enhance or reduce the same; it may also alter or reverse any other order or make consequential or incidental orders. Two Provisos mandate that sentences shall not be enhanced unless the accused has had an opportunity of showing cause and the Appellate Court shall not inflict greater punishment than might have been inflicted by the Court passing the order or sentence under appeal.
BNSS_S374:351f02392c5f6a8372d5684f7f2a58: Where an accused is acquitted on the ground of unsoundness of mind for an act that would constitute an offence but for the incapacity, the Magistrate or Court shall order the person to be detained in safe custody or delivered to a relative or friend. Sub-section (2) mandates that detention in a public mental health establishment must comply with State Government rules under the Mental Healthcare Act, 2017 (10 of 2017), while sub-section (3) requires that delivery to a relative or friend be based on their application and security to ensure the person is properly taken care of, prevented from doing injury to himself or any other person, and produced for inspection as directed by the State Government. The Magistrate or Court shall report the action taken under sub-section (1) to the State Government.
BNSS_S450:d1443c3e1a25ff09ebefd27d1c99af: Under Section 450(1), a Chief Judicial Magistrate may withdraw any case from, or recall any case made over to, any Magistrate subordinate to him, to inquire into or try such case himself, or refer it for inquiry or trial to any other competent Magistrate. Additionally, Section 450(2) empowers any Judicial Magistrate to recall any case made over by him under sub-section (2) of section 212 to any other Magistrate and inquire into or try such cases himself.
BNSS_S468:147013cd9ea3cc1c93c2e9730e27ca: Section 468 mandates that where an accused person is convicted and sentenced to imprisonment, excluding imprisonment in default of payment of fine, the period of detention undergone during investigation, inquiry, or trial of the same case before the date of conviction shall be set off against the term of imprisonment. Consequently, the liability of such person to undergo imprisonment is restricted to the remainder, if any, of the term imposed. A Proviso specifies that in cases referred to in section 475, such period of detention shall be set off against the period of fourteen years referred to in that section.
BNSS_S396:3599c1bedae0b3dd4f14dd14ede0e0: Every State Government, in co-ordination with the Central Government, shall prepare a scheme for providing compensation to a victim or his dependents for loss or injury requiring rehabilitation, with the District Legal Service Authority or State Legal Service Authority deciding the quantum upon Court recommendation. The trial Court may recommend compensation if compensation under section 395 is inadequate, or in cases of acquittal or discharge; alternatively, if the offender is not traced or identified and no trial takes place, the victim or his dependents may apply to the State or District Legal Services Authority. Following a due enquiry to be completed within two months, the Authority may award compensation or order immediate first-aid, free medical benefits on the certificate of a police officer not below the rank of the officer in charge of the police station or a Magistrate, or other interim relief. This compensation shall be in addition to the payment of fine under section 65, section 70, and sub-section (1) of section 124 of the Bharatiya Nyaya Sanhita, 2023.
BNSS_S494:bb7296f8048a8a040d1d4ec0cd64e4: Section 494 governs the requirement of a bond from a child. It stipulates that when a child is required by any Court or officer to execute a bond, such Court or officer may accept a bond executed by a surety or sureties only in lieu thereof.
BNSS_S462:3b140b3f1c1bb25fe1e5d9ef8aa71f: A warrant issued under clause (a) of sub-section (1) of section 461 by any Court may be executed within the local jurisdiction of such Court. Such warrant shall authorise the attachment and sale of any such property outside such jurisdiction when it is endorsed by the District Magistrate within whose local jurisdiction such property is found.
BNSS_S490:46b8416802bf1d7832b432252c15ce: When any person is required by any Court or officer to execute a bond or bail bond, such Court or officer may permit the deposit of a sum of money or Government promissory notes in lieu of executing such bond. The amount of the deposit shall be as the Court or officer may fix. An exception is explicitly provided, stating that this mechanism does not apply in the case of a bond for good behaviour.
BNSS_S384:73b1dcfa23986931cde1bc4c229ce3: When offences described in section 210, section 213, section 214, section 215, or section 267 of the Bharatiya Nyaya Sanhita, 2023 (45 of 2023) are committed in the view or presence of any Civil, Criminal, or Revenue Court, the Court may cause the offender to be detained in custody and, before the rising of the Court on the same day, take cognizance and sentence the offender, after giving a reasonable opportunity of showing cause, to a fine not exceeding one thousand rupees, or in default of payment, simple imprisonment for a term which may extend to one month, unless such fine be sooner paid. The Court shall record the fact constituting the offence, the statement (if any) made by the offender, the finding, and the sentence. If the offence is under section 267 of the Bharatiya Nyaya Sanhita, 2023 (45 of 2023), the record shall show the nature and stage of the judicial proceeding in which the Court interrupted or insulted was sitting, and the nature of the interruption or insult.
BNSS_S508:29870da29a06efbb1a09e85bd9d2eb: No finding, sentence, or order of any Criminal Court shall be set aside merely on the ground that the inquiry, trial, or other proceedings took place in a wrong sessions division, district, sub-division, or other local area. An exception is provided if it appears that such error has in fact occasioned a failure of justice.
BNSS_S522:99c39566a64f7422b0832d855a9ffc: Subject to the power conferred by article 227 of the Constitution, the forms set forth in the Second Schedule may be used for the respective purposes therein mentioned. These forms may be utilized with such variations as the circumstances of each case require and, if used, shall be sufficient.
BNSS_S528:8a922b3b79134b0730f6f1eab5e4d8: Section 528 provides for the saving of inherent powers of High Court, stipulating that nothing in this Sanhita shall limit or affect such powers. These inherent powers enable the High Court to make orders necessary to give effect to any order under this Sanhita, prevent abuse of the process of any Court, or otherwise secure the ends of justice.
BNSS_S502:c4f666a05ccdb9184d76a9e7436e51: Upon conviction of an offence by use of criminal force, show of force, or criminal intimidation resulting in the dispossession of immovable property, the Court may order possession restored by evicting any person in possession by force, if necessary, subject to a Proviso that no such order is made more than one month after the date of the conviction. If the trial Court has not made such an order, the Court of appeal, confirmation or revision may make the order while disposing of the appeal, reference, or revision. Such orders are subject to the provisions of section 500 as they apply to section 499 and shall not prejudice any right or interest to or in the immovable property which any person may be able to establish in a civil suit.
BNSS_S524:1d9d0a3f48c8fa484aacd918bc118a: Under Section 524, the State Government may, by notification, direct that references to an Executive Magistrate in sections 127, 128, 129, 164 and 166 shall be construed as references to a Judicial Magistrate of the first class. This mechanism is applicable if the Legislative Assembly of a State permits by a resolution and occurs after consultation with the High Court.
BNSS_S497:bcc92df5fe04a6a1dd68dac5165990: During investigation, inquiry, or trial, a Criminal Court or the Magistrate empowered to take cognizance or commit the case for trial may order the proper custody of "property"—which an Explanation defines to include any kind or document produced or in custody, or property regarding an offence committed or used for an offence—or, if subject to speedy and natural decay or otherwise expedient, its sale or disposal. Within fourteen days of production, the Court or Magistrate shall prepare a descriptive statement per State Government rules and take a photograph and, if necessary, videograph on mobile phone or electronic media, to be used as evidence in any inquiry, trial, or other proceeding. Within thirty days after the statement and photograph or videography are taken, the Court or Magistrate shall order the disposal, destruction, confiscation or delivery of the property.
BNSS_S526:774f5db4f64034b73569a88c8e2719: Section 526 mandates that no advocate who practices in the Court of any Magistrate shall sit as a Magistrate in that Court. This prohibition further extends to sitting as a Magistrate in any Court within the local jurisdiction of that Court.
BNSS_S530:e4f1b34324f3312c3a38879e379329: Under Section 530, all trials, inquires and proceedings under this Sanhita may be held in electronic mode through the use of electronic communication or the use of audio-video electronic means. This includes the issuance, service and execution of summons and warrant, the examination of complainant and witnesses, and the recording of evidence in inquiries and trials. Furthermore, all appellate proceedings or any other proceeding may be conducted in electronic mode.
BSA_S3:c4c43de0cdb48982d6475047dbafd19c9: In any suit or proceeding, evidence may be given of the existence or non-existence of every fact in issue and of such other facts as are hereinafter declared to be relevant, and of no others. An Explanation clarifies that this section shall not enable any person to give evidence of a fact which he is disentitled to prove by any provision of the law for the time being in force relating to civil procedure.
BNSS_S516:42f77761ee6d868e6ae1988ca73dc5: In computing the period of limitation, time spent prosecuting with due diligence in a Court of first instance, appeal, or revision is excluded, subject to a Proviso that the prosecution relates to the same facts, is in good faith, and the Court is unable to entertain it due to defect of jurisdiction or similar cause; additionally, the period of the continuance of an injunction or order staying prosecution, including the day on which it was issued or made and the day on which it was withdrawn, shall be excluded. Time required for notice of prosecution or obtaining previous consent or sanction of the Government or any other authority is also excluded, with the Explanation stating that the date on which the application was made and the date of receipt of the order shall both be excluded. Finally, the period of limitation excludes time during which the offender has been absent from India or from any territory outside India which is under the administration of the Central Government, or has avoided arrest by absconding or concealing himself.
BNSS_S430:ac157c4c082ab2853d7257aa82628a: Pending appeal, the Appellate Court—including the High Court in cases of appeal to a subordinate Court—may, for reasons recorded in writing, order that the execution of the sentence or order appealed against be suspended and release the convicted person on bail, on his own bond, or on a bail bond. Under the first Proviso, for offences punishable with death, imprisonment for life, or imprisonment for a term of not less than ten years, the Court shall give the Public Prosecutor opportunity to show cause in writing against release on own bond or bail bond, and under the second Proviso, the Public Prosecutor may file an application for the cancellation of the bail. Where a convicted person on bail is sentenced to imprisonment for a term not exceeding three years or is convicted of a bailable offence while on bail, the convicting Court shall, unless there are special reasons for refusing bail, order release on bail to afford sufficient time to present the appeal and obtain orders of the Appellate Court (deeming the sentence suspended), with such time being excluded in computing the term if the appellant is ultimately sentenced to imprisonment for a term or to imprisonment for life.
BSA_S5:bd06a9083ff3ccc9e36732504bded80c3: Section 5 stipulates that facts are relevant if they are the occasion, cause, or effect, immediate or otherwise, of relevant facts or facts in issue. It further includes facts which constitute the state of things under which they happened, or which afforded an opportunity for their occurrence or transaction. The section provides illustrative applications of these principles in the context of robbery, murder, and poisoning.
BSA_S9:6dbc9fdb9eec5011e707b0f32425ff7bf: Section 9 provides that facts not otherwise relevant become relevant if they are inconsistent with any fact in issue or relevant fact. Furthermore, such facts are relevant if, by themselves or in connection with other facts, they make the existence or non-existence of any fact in issue or relevant fact highly probable or improbable. The section includes Illustrations demonstrating these principles through scenarios of physical impossibility based on location and the exclusion of other potential actors.
BSA_S14:ea1a15e189927216c1d5e3d846ad95ea: Section 14 stipulates that when there is a question whether a particular act was done, the existence of any course of business, according to which it naturally would have been done, is a relevant fact. Illustrations clarify this relevance through the ordinary course of business for dispatching letters put in a certain place and the fact that a letter was posted in due course and not returned through the Return Letter Office to establish if it reached a recipient.
BSA_S16:b71492f6356126998b818f66f2d60577: Section 16 defines admissions as statements made by a party to the proceeding or an agent whom the Court regards as expressly or impliedly authorised. Statements by parties suing or sued in a representative character are not admissions unless made while the party held that character. Additionally, statements made during the continuance of the interest by persons with proprietary or pecuniary interest in the subject matter (made in that character), or persons from whom parties derived their interest in the subject matter, are admissions.
BSA_S1:28824c8006a5e80c935fae67b897e2459: The Bharatiya Sakshya Adhiniyam, 2023, applies to all judicial proceedings in or before any Court, including Courts-martial. This application excludes, as exceptions, affidavits presented to any Court or officer and proceedings before an arbitrator. The Act shall come into force on such date as the Central Government may, by notification in the Official Gazette, appoint.
BSA_S10:4cfed5ea2dd21714d51eb41b39965dee: In suits in which damages are claimed, any fact which will enable the Court to determine the amount of damages which ought to be awarded is relevant. This includes facts tending to enable the Court to determine the amount of damages.
BSA_S15:3762708be808eb95bc5f0bff46870caa: An admission is a statement, whether oral, documentary, or contained in electronic form, which suggests any inference as to any fact in issue or relevant fact. This statement must be made by any of the persons and under the circumstances hereinafter mentioned.
BSA_S7:4d70f61a32861eae8620fd55d63e07465: Section 7 stipulates that facts are relevant if they are necessary to explain or introduce a fact in issue or relevant fact, support or rebut an inference suggested by them, establish the identity of any person or thing, fix the time or place of occurrence, or show the relation of parties by whom such facts were transacted. Such facts are relevant only in so far as they are necessary for those specific purposes. The section's illustrations clarify relevance in contexts including the state of property and family in wills, the position and relations of parties in libel, the explanation of subsequent conduct such as absconding, and statements or circumstances that clarify the nature of a transaction, such as in theft or a riot.
BSA_S17:2bc5807c12eec08122165d564a1ce273: Section 17 provides that statements made by persons whose position or liability must be proved against a party to the suit are admissions, provided such statements would be relevant as against such persons regarding that position or liability in a suit brought by or against them. A mandatory condition is that these statements must be made while the person making them occupies such position or is subject to such liability. The section includes an illustration where, if A undertakes to collect rents for B and B sues A for not collecting rent due from C, C's statement that he owed B rent is an admission and a relevant fact against A if A denies that C owed rent to B.
BSA_S21:dcf1d49076179351f29f19183bf2cf4b: In civil cases, no admission is relevant if it is made upon an express condition that evidence of it is not to be given, or under circumstances from which the Court can infer that the parties agreed together that evidence of it should not be given. An Explanation clarifies that nothing in this section shall be taken to exempt any advocate from giving evidence of any matter of which he may be compelled to give evidence under sub-sections (1) and (2) of section 132.
BSA_S13:5c638e3baac4cf342c4704a5db2785dd: When there is a question whether an act was accidental or intentional, or done with a particular knowledge or intention, the fact that such act formed part of a series of similar occurrences, in each of which the person doing the act was concerned, is relevant. The section provides Illustrations involving successive fires in insured houses to obtain money, the making of false entries in a book to favor oneself, and the delivery of counterfeit currency to multiple parties to show the act was not accidental.
BSA_S24:af5636b4a2628c1fcf82c2b9364d66ef: Where more persons than one are being tried jointly for the same offence, a proved confession by one affecting themselves and others may be taken into consideration by the Court against both the maker and the other affected persons. Explanation I specifies that “Offence” includes the abetment of, or attempt to commit, the offence. Explanation II clarifies that a trial held in the absence of an accused who has absconded or failed to comply with a proclamation issued under section 84 of the Bharatiya Nagarik Suraksha Sanhita, 2023, shall be deemed to be a joint trial for the purpose of this section.
BSA_S23:81ff0c532f1fcea0fa6328e1ad994a86: Section 23 mandates that no confession made to a police officer shall be proved as against a person accused of any offence, and no confession made by any person while in the custody of a police officer shall be proved against him unless it is made in the immediate presence of a Magistrate. A Proviso provides that when any fact is deposed to as discovered in consequence of information received from a person accused of any offence, in the custody of a police officer, so much of such information, whether it amounts to a confession or not, as relates distinctly to the fact discovered may be proved.
BSA_S28:747958170afdea64b5f399fe846e2aeb: Entries in books of account, including those maintained in an electronic form, regularly kept in the course of business are relevant whenever they refer to a matter into which the Court has to inquire. However, such statements shall not alone be sufficient evidence to charge any person with liability. An illustration specifies that entries showing a person to be indebted for one thousand rupees are relevant, but not sufficient to prove the debt without other evidence.
BSA_S30:b6bb19702cc30fc05f046ca4aad7e810: Section 30 establishes that statements of facts in issue or relevant facts are themselves relevant facts if they are made in published maps or charts generally offered for public sale. This relevancy also extends to statements in maps or plans made under the authority of the Central Government or any State Government, provided they concern matters usually represented or stated in such maps, charts or plans.
BSA_S25:77cf62400cbd7f2edfb6b438634f0907: Section 25 states that admissions are not conclusive proof of the matters admitted but may operate as estoppels under the provisions hereinafter contained. The section also pertains to statements by persons who cannot be called as witnesses.
BSA_S29:2b27120505cbdc73b4bc4f5f98eca322: An entry in any public or other official book, register or record, or an electronic record, stating a fact in issue or relevant fact, is itself a relevant fact. This applies if the entry is made by a public servant in the discharge of his official duty, or by any other person in performance of a duty specially enjoined by the law of the country in which such book, register or record or an electronic record is kept.
BSA_S32:849cb70711e8695345f3be17fea029b3: When the Court has to form an opinion as to a law of any country, any statement of such law contained in a book purporting to be printed or published, including in electronic or digital form, under the authority of the Government of such country is relevant. Additionally, any report of a ruling of the Courts of such country contained in a book, including in electronic or digital form, purporting to be a report of such rulings, is relevant.
BSA_S27:19bec0bef178f38dd09eb30f576f33e5: Evidence given by a witness in a judicial proceeding or before a person authorized by law is relevant for proving the truth of facts in a subsequent judicial proceeding or a later stage of the same proceeding if the witness is dead, cannot be found, is incapable of giving evidence, is kept out of the way by the adverse party, or if their presence cannot be obtained without unreasonable delay or expense as considered by the Court. The Proviso requires that the proceeding be between the same parties or their representatives in interest, that the adverse party in the first proceeding had the right and opportunity to cross-examine, and that the questions in issue are substantially the same. An Explanation deems a criminal trial or inquiry to be a proceeding between the prosecutor and the accused within the meaning of this section.
BSA_S31:263237376c547f29039d9e2190df7a0c: When the Court has to form an opinion as to the existence of any fact of a public nature, any statement of it made in a recital contained in any Central Act or State Act is a relevant fact. Such relevancy extends to statements made in a Central Government or State Government notification appearing in the respective Official Gazette, or in any printed paper or in electronic or digital form purporting to be such Gazette.
BSA_S33:ee94692df8f2bf8f752f13e04f9bc566: When any statement of which evidence is given forms part of a longer statement, conversation, isolated document, document within a book, part of an electronic record, or a connected series of letters or papers, evidence shall be given of so much and no more of that statement, conversation, document, electronic record, book, or series of letters or papers. The amount of evidence provided is limited to what the Court considers necessary in that particular case to ensure a full understanding of the nature and effect of the statement and the circumstances under which it was made.
BSA_S38:8b02158e63f5333499ec6205f56de71f: Section 38 provides that any party to a suit or other proceeding may prove that any judgment, order or decree relevant under section 34, 35 or 36, which has been proved by the adverse party, was delivered by a Court not competent to deliver it. Furthermore, the party may show that such judgment, order or decree was obtained by fraud or collusion.
BSA_S40:f1db219a37564b64606d80953ae77798: Section 40 provides that facts, not otherwise relevant, are relevant if they support or are inconsistent with the opinions of experts, when such opinions are relevant. This mechanism applies to facts that affirm or deny expert opinions, such as symptoms exhibited by other persons poisoned by a certain poison or the status of other similarly situated harbours without sea-walls.
BSA_S19:e100106a96285badff43e9e177772df8: Admissions are relevant and may be proved against the person making them or their representative in interest, but cannot be proved by or on behalf of the person making them or their representative in interest. Exceptions exist where an admission may be proved by or on behalf of the person making it if: it is of such a nature that, if the person making it were dead, it would be relevant as between third persons under section 26; it consists of a statement of the existence of any state of mind or body, relevant or in issue, made at or about the time when such state of mind or body existed and is accompanied by conduct rendering its falsehood improbable; or it is relevant otherwise than as an admission.
BSA_S44:60d379e6a515bc8fc4ef09a98f217f8f: Section 44 stipulates that when the Court must form an opinion regarding the relationship of one person to another, the opinion expressed by conduct of any person who, as a member of the family or otherwise, possesses special means of knowledge on the subject, is a relevant fact. A Proviso specifies that such opinion shall not be sufficient to prove a marriage in proceedings under the Divorce Act, 1869 (4 of 1869) or in prosecution under sections 82 and 84 of the Bharatiya Nyaya Sanhita, 2023.
BSA_S41:e20c426cd17b4f0f8012e8dad815c55f: When the Court forms an opinion as to the person by whom any document was written or signed, the opinion of any person acquainted with the handwriting of that person regarding whether it was or was not written or signed by them is a relevant fact. An Explanation defines acquaintance as having seen the person write, receiving documents purporting to be written by that person in answer to documents written by oneself or under one's authority, or having documents purporting to be written by that person habitually submitted in the ordinary course of business. Additionally, when the Court forms an opinion as to the electronic signature of any person, the opinion of the Certifying Authority which has issued the Electronic Signature Certificate is a relevant fact.
BSA_S46:7769fbab68b1bdc3f02e782f39cecd0c: In civil cases, the fact that the character of any person concerned is such as to render probable or improbable any conduct imputed to him is irrelevant. This is subject to an exception in so far as such character appears from facts otherwise relevant.
BSA_S39:fbde186209af89f767bcca8b8807c9da: Under Section 39(1), when the Court must form an opinion upon a point of foreign law, science or art, any other field, or identity of handwriting or finger impressions, the opinions of persons specially skilled in such areas are relevant facts, and such persons are called experts. Under sub-section (2), when the Court must form an opinion on any matter relating to information transmitted or stored in any computer resource or any other electronic or digital form, the opinion of the Examiner of Electronic Evidence referred to in section 79A of the Information Technology Act, 2000 (21 of 2000) is a relevant fact. An Explanation clarifies that for the purposes of sub-section (2), an Examiner of Electronic Evidence shall be an expert.
BSA_S37:e4f84a63a1d111dcb77d978d43c7e1ae: Section 37 provides that judgments, orders, or decrees, other than those mentioned in sections 34, 35, and 36, are irrelevant. An exception applies if the existence of such judgment, order, or decree is a fact in issue, or is relevant under some other provision of this Adhiniyam.
BSA_S45:ac1a3ff5715f6e62f3ac2460e9fec137: Section 45 stipulates that whenever the opinion of any living person is relevant, the grounds on which such opinion is based are also relevant. An illustration clarifies that an expert may give an account of experiments performed by him for the purpose of forming his opinion.
BSA_S43:db1fd4a45d3a84b1ed5203c28f8a8faf: The opinions of persons having special means of knowledge thereon are relevant facts when the Court must form an opinion regarding the usages and tenets of any body of men or family, or the constitution and governance of any religious or charitable foundation. Such opinions are also relevant when the Court must form an opinion regarding the meaning of words or terms used in particular districts or by particular classes of people.
BSA_S49:6175d9155424b7ba42dd359f4cba0260: In criminal proceedings, the fact that the accused has a bad character is irrelevant unless evidence has been given that he has a good character, in which case it becomes relevant. Explanation 1 provides that this section does not apply to cases in which the bad character of any person is itself a fact in issue. Explanation 2 states that a previous conviction is relevant as evidence of bad character.
BSA_S50:63302a6187ff9a307fc8d407a613ac2d: In civil cases, the fact that the character of any person is such as to affect the amount of damages which he ought to receive is relevant. The Explanation clarifies that in section 50 and sections 46, 47, and 49, the word “character” includes both reputation and disposition. Except as provided in section 49, evidence may be given only of general reputation and general disposition, and not of particular acts by which reputation or disposition has been shown.
BSA_S35:9ca859f104d3e1b7a5ae775fd57a6bc3: A final judgment, order or decree of a competent Court or Tribunal in the exercise of probate, matrimonial, admiralty or insolvency jurisdiction is relevant when it confers upon, takes away from, or declares any person entitled to any legal character or any specific thing absolutely (not as against any specified person), provided such legal character or title is relevant. Such judgment, order or decree is conclusive proof regarding the time when conferred legal character accrued, the time when declared legal character accrued, the time when legal character ceased, and the time from which any thing declared to which a person is entitled was the property of that person.
BSA_S54:5407fa78dea1699f1be845a5c3842c4d: Section 54 stipulates that all facts may be proved by oral evidence. This mechanism specifically excludes the contents of documents.
BSA_S56:f6e41165d675d6f39587d6e364519b51: Section 56 of the Bharatiya Sakshya Adhiniyam, 2023, governs the proof of contents of documents. It stipulates that the contents of documents may be proved by either primary or secondary evidence.
BSA_S47:2b2d11494bc382917d7d1f4258d8e0d0: In criminal proceedings, the fact that the person accused is of a good character is relevant. This section establishes the relevance of the character of the person accused within such proceedings. No Provisos, Exceptions, or Explanations are contained in this section.
BSA_S51:5c827f284e199cd606700b713bb1e6dc: Section 51 of the Bharatiya Sakshya Adhiniyam, 2023, stipulates that no fact of which the Court will take judicial notice need be proved. This provision addresses the legal principle of facts judicially noticeable.
BSA_S52:ab88841e120d3a1828daf31511cc24a5: Section 52(1) mandates that the Court shall take judicial notice of all laws in force in the territory of India (including laws having extra-territorial operation), international treaties, agreements, conventions, decisions at international associations, and proceedings of the Constituent Assembly, Parliament, and State Legislatures. This mandate extends to seals of Courts, Tribunals, Courts of Admiralty and Maritime Jurisdiction, and Notaries Public; public office details, festivals, fasts, and holidays notified in the Official Gazette; the territory of India; recognized countries' existence, titles, and flags; hostilities; court officers and authorized advocates; and rules of the road on land or at sea. Under sub-section (2), for these facts and matters of public history, literature, science or art, the Court may resort to reference books or documents and may refuse judicial notice unless the requesting person produces such necessary documentation.
BSA_S53:aeb8fa9d3206f4075b3b22143d23c58c: No fact needs to be proved in any proceeding if the parties thereto or their agents agree to admit it at the hearing, agree to admit it by any writing under their hands before the hearing, or are deemed to have admitted it by their pleadings under any rule of pleading in force at the time. A Proviso stipulates that the Court may, in its discretion, require the facts admitted to be proved otherwise than by such admissions.
BSA_S62:2a8b78c3afde450608948a101b0984a7: Section 62 establishes special provisions regarding evidence relating to electronic records. It mandates that the contents of electronic records may be proved in accordance with the provisions of section 63.
BSA_S59:b049684bd10685a6180d3a54f1b9256d: Section 59 mandates that documents shall be proved by primary evidence. This rule applies except in the cases hereinafter mentioned.
BSA_S58:4e988fa18bb3880275d36ef0725af68e: Section 58 defines "Secondary evidence" to include certified copies, copies made from the original by mechanical processes ensuring accuracy (and copies compared with such copies), and copies made from or compared with the original. It encompasses counterparts of documents against parties who did not execute them, oral accounts of a document's contents provided by a person who has seen it, and both oral and written admissions. Additionally, it includes evidence from a skilled person who has examined a document consisting of numerous accounts or other documents that cannot conveniently be examined in Court. The section provides Illustrations regarding the status of photographs, machine-copies, the requirement of comparison for transcribed copies, and the exclusion of oral accounts of copies or photographs as secondary evidence of the original.
BSA_S61:66f7b5bbcc0d2d80444021dbbe9fe669: Section 61 stipulates that nothing in the Adhiniyam shall apply to deny the admissibility of an electronic or digital record in the evidence on the ground that it is an electronic or digital record. Subject to section 63, such an electronic or digital record shall have the same legal effect, validity and enforceability as other document.
BSA_S64:e62a39be4ded53fd0a7c6f68592ed91c: Section 64 stipulates that secondary evidence of documents referred to in clause (a) of section 60 is inadmissible unless the proposing party has given "notice to produce" to the party in possession or power, or to his advocate or representative, as prescribed by law or as the Court considers reasonable. A Proviso states that such notice is not required if the Court thinks fit to dispense with it or in specific cases: (a) when the document is itself a notice; (b) when the adverse party must know he will be required to produce it; (c) when the original was obtained by fraud or force; (d) when the adverse party or his agent has the original in Court; (e) when the adverse party or his agent admitted the loss of the document; or (f) when the possessor is out of reach of, or not subject to, the process of the Court.
BSA_S66:58f1048088ffce69854e48c0bd98b246: Section 66 mandates that if the electronic signature of any subscriber is alleged to have been affixed to an electronic record, the fact that such electronic signature is the electronic signature of the subscriber must be proved. This requirement applies except in the case of a secure electronic signature.
BSA_S55:be0f842621a8f4255e914be96e1ee616: Section 55 mandates that oral evidence shall, in all cases whatever, be direct, requiring a witness who saw, heard, or perceived a fact by any other sense or manner, or the person who holds an opinion and its grounds, to provide such evidence. The first Proviso allows expert opinions in any treatise commonly offered for sale, and their grounds, to be proved by producing such treatises if the author is dead, cannot be found, is incapable of giving evidence, or cannot be called as a witness without an amount of delay or expense which the Court regards as unreasonable. The second Proviso provides that if oral evidence refers to the existence or condition of any material thing other than a document, the Court may, if it thinks fit, require the production of such material thing for its inspection.
BSA_S68:5128998a58ac781330ad67ced5418a5e: Section 68 specifies the requirements for proof when no attesting witness can be found. It mandates that it must be proved that the attestation of one attesting witness at least is in his handwriting. Furthermore, it must be proved that the signature of the person executing the document is in the handwriting of that person.
BSA_S60:3579dadf6364770863c59325b44768e9: Section 60 permits secondary evidence of the existence, condition, or contents of a document when the original is in the possession or power of the person against whom it is proved, a person out of reach of or not subject to the process of the Court, or a person legally bound to produce it after notice under section 64. Admissibility also applies if the original's status is admitted in writing by the opposing party or their representative in interest, is destroyed, lost, unavailable without the party's default or neglect, not easily movable, a public document under section 74, a certified copy permitted by this Adhiniyam or other law in force in India, or numerous accounts where the fact to be proved is the general result. An Explanation specifies admissible evidence: any secondary evidence for clauses (a), (c), and (d); the written admission for clause (b); only a certified copy for clauses (e) and (f); and evidence of the general result by a person skilled in the examination who has examined the documents for clause (g).
BSA_S67:0d4690f9744afdd0946ad599faf075de: Section 67 mandates that if a document is required by law to be attested, it shall not be used as evidence until at least one attesting witness, if alive, subject to the process of the Court, and capable of giving evidence, has been called to prove its execution. A Proviso specifies that it shall not be necessary to call an attesting witness to prove the execution of any document, not being a will, which has been registered in accordance with the provisions of the Indian Registration Act, 1908 (16 of 1908), unless its execution by the person by whom it purports to have been executed is specifically denied.
BSA_S70:e80249fd489c83343c405696e1769567: Section 70 provides that if the attesting witness denies or does not recollect the execution of the document, its execution may be proved by other evidence. This section contains no Provisos, Exceptions, or Explanations.
BSA_S69:eefcec2488184e6b954e32264256af97: Under Section 69, the admission of a party to an attested document of its execution by himself shall be sufficient proof of its execution as against him. This provision applies even though it be a document required by law to be attested.
BSA_S65:766f35da56d0a678c850ea72743d7959: If a document is alleged to be signed or written, whether wholly or in part, by any person, the signature or handwriting of that person must be proved. Specifically, the signature or the handwriting of so much of the document as is alleged to be in that person's handwriting must be proved to be in his handwriting.
BSA_S72:699c1e0692fc3531f97d3f04c328f724: To ascertain whether a signature, writing or seal is that of the person by whom it purports to have been written or made, any signature, writing, or seal admitted or proved to the satisfaction of the Court to have been written or made by that person may be compared with the one to be proved, regardless of whether it was produced or proved for any other purpose. The Court may direct any person present in Court to write any words or figures to enable the Court to compare them with words or figures alleged to have been written by such person. This section applies, with necessary modifications, to finger impressions.
BSA_S63:4a606658a7e3305824a6cfd80a6fae45: Section 63 deems "computer output"—information in an electronic record printed on paper, stored, recorded or copied in optical or magnetic media or semiconductor memory produced by a computer or communication device—as a "document" admissible as evidence without further proof of the original, provided the conditions in sub-section (2) regarding lawful control, ordinary course feeding, device operation/accuracy, and information reproduction or derivation are satisfied. Multiple devices used in standalone mode, on a computer system, on a computer network, on a computer resource, or through an intermediary are construed as a "single computer or communication device." Admission requires a certificate identifying the record, describing its production, providing device particulars, and addressing the sub-section (2) conditions, signed by a person in charge of the computer or communication device or the management of the relevant activities and an expert, stated to the best of the knowledge and belief of the person. Sub-section (5) provides explanations regarding how information is supplied to a computer or communication device and how computer output is produced.
BSA_S73:71db46c1a0eeeb9089ee63e7128077de: To ascertain whether a digital signature is that of the person by whom it purports to have been affixed, the Court may direct that person, the Controller, or the Certifying Authority to produce the Digital Signature Certificate. Additionally, the Court may direct any other person to apply the public key listed in the Digital Signature Certificate and verify the digital signature purported to have been affixed by that person.
BSA_S57:bb5d613e8e80147c892b930eb3720765: Primary evidence means the document itself produced for the inspection of the Court. Explanations 1, 2, and 3 specify that each part of a document executed in several parts, each counterpart being executed by one or some of the parties only as against the parties executing it, and documents made by a uniform process (printing, lithography, or photography) are primary evidence of the contents of the rest, but where they are all copies of a common original, they are not primary evidence of the contents of the original. Explanations 4, 5, 6, and 7 provide that primary evidence includes electronic or digital records stored simultaneously or sequentially in multiple files, records from proper custody unless disputed, video recordings simultaneously stored and transmitted, broadcast, or transferred, and automated storage in multiple computer resource spaces, including temporary files.
BSA_S75:4d82c40f174716ef335bb0f8ed528681: Every public officer having the custody of a public document, which any person has a right to inspect, shall on demand and payment of legal fees provide a copy with a certificate at its foot stating it is a "true copy of such document or part thereof." This certificate must be dated and subscribed by the officer with his name and official title, and sealed if the officer is authorized by law to use a seal, resulting in copies called "certified copies." An Explanation provides that any officer authorized by the ordinary course of official duty to deliver such copies shall be deemed to have the custody of such documents within the meaning of this section.
BSA_S82:de7f6e7fdd3fefbfa330a57e506dc3d0: The Court shall presume that maps or plans purporting to be made by the authority of the Central Government or any State Government were so made and are accurate. However, maps or plans made for the purposes of any cause must be proved to be accurate.
BSA_S80:a4b4b2d192d73c372c02564b84af5aca: The Court shall presume the genuineness of every document purporting to be the Official Gazette, a newspaper or journal, or a document directed by any law to be kept by any person, provided it is kept substantially in the form required by law and produced from proper custody. The section includes an Explanation stating that for the purposes of section 80 and section 92, a document is in "proper custody" if it is in the place in which, and looked after by the person with whom such document is required to be kept. Additionally, the Explanation specifies that no custody is improper if it is proved to have had a legitimate origin, or if the circumstances of the particular case render that origin probable.
BSA_S83:e2ebf4fe5ec7de18ee7470de0ccbbfa7: Under Section 83, the Court shall presume the genuineness of every book purporting to be printed or published under the authority of the Government of any country and containing any of the laws of that country. This presumption further applies to every book purporting to contain reports of decisions of the Courts of such country.
BSA_S71:5a7228b4d164169fbd98cdd88b7be9c9: Section 71 provides that an attested document not required by law to be attested may be proved as if it was unattested. This section contains no Provisos, Exceptions, or Explanations.
BSA_S84:9e5eab79f2224a170b35f0eecc7c1a34: Section 84 establishes a presumption as to powers-of-attorney. The Court shall presume that every document purporting to be a power-of-attorney, and to have been executed before and authenticated by a Notary Public, Court, Judge, Magistrate, Indian Consul, Vice-Consul, or representative of the Central Government, was so executed and authenticated.
BSA_S85:865ebd48afc7db4ee524553d21f0a49c: Under Section 85 of the Bharatiya Sakshya Adhiniyam, 2023, the Court shall presume specific facts regarding electronic agreements. For every electronic record purporting to be an agreement containing the electronic or digital signature of the parties, the Court shall presume it was so concluded by affixing the electronic or digital signature of the parties.
BSA_S81:5d611025928b7dd09ab700afae9e89ef: The Court shall presume the genuineness of every electronic or digital record purporting to be the Official Gazette, or purporting to be electronic or digital record directed by any law to be kept by any person, if such record is kept substantially in the form required by law and is produced from proper custody. According to the Explanation, for the purposes of this section and section 93, electronic records are in proper custody if they are in the place in which, and looked after by, the person with whom such document is required to be kept. The Explanation further stipulates that no custody is improper if it is proved to have had a legitimate origin, or the circumstances of the particular case are such as to render that origin probable.
BSA_S90:83de3c769d474277c5f5fadcc9f48f78: Section 90 provides that the Court may presume that an electronic message forwarded by the originator through an electronic mail server to the addressee to whom the message purports to be addressed corresponds with the message as fed into his computer for transmission. However, the Court shall not make any presumption as to the person by whom such message was sent.
BSA_S87:cf7a617b2a90d956650978b4f31657b7: Under Section 87, the Court shall presume, unless contrary is proved, that the information listed in an Electronic Signature Certificate is correct, provided the certificate was accepted by the subscriber. An exception is explicitly provided for information specified as subscriber information which has not been verified.
BSA_S77:ca193c81f33064997915896d71d207a3: Section 77 provides that Central or State Government acts, orders, or notifications are proved by department records certified by the head of those Departments or documents purporting to be printed by order of the Government, while Parliament or State Legislature proceedings are proved by journals, published Acts or abstracts, or copies purporting to be printed by order of the concerned Government.
